exam_tip: Aerosols control cloud formation, influence the radiation balance, and affect visibility. Without them, weather as we know it wouldn't exist. Volcanic aerosols can affect global climate.
key_facts: Essential for cloud formation -- clouds wouldn't form without condensation nuclei
Influence the Earth's radiation balance (more cloud = more reflected sunlight)
Drive the water cycle through their role in cloud and precipitation
Major volcanic eruptions can cool global temperatures via stratospheric aerosols
Determine baseline visibility and haze conditions
More aerosols can enable fog at slightly lower relative humidity

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of jet streams, including their significance to aviation.
concept: Jet streams are narrow bands of very strong wind (typically 60 kt or more, sometimes over 200 kt) found near the tropopause. They form where there are strong horizontal temperature gradients -- basically where warm and cold air masses meet at upper levels. The two main jet streams relevant to NZ operations are the subtropical jet (STJ) around 25-30 degrees S, and the polar front jet (PFJ) around 40-60 degrees S. NZ can be affected by both, depending on the season and the weather pattern. Jet streams are significant for aviation because of their wind speeds (tailwinds or headwinds that dramatically affect flight times and fuel burn), the clear air turbulence (CAT) found near jet stream boundaries, and the vertical wind shear on the flanks of the jet core. CAT is most intense on the cold (poleward) side of the jet and on the cyclonic (left in the Southern Hemisphere) side. If you're flying at jet stream altitudes, knowing where the core is helps you plan for turbulence and optimise your route for favourable winds.
exam_tip: Jet streams: typically 60+ kt near the tropopause. CAT is most intense on the cold (poleward) side of the jet and the cyclonic (left) side in the Southern Hemisphere. STJ ~25-30 degrees S, PFJ ~40-60 degrees S.
key_facts: Jet streams: narrow bands of strong wind (60+ kt) near the tropopause
Form where strong horizontal temperature gradients exist at upper levels
Subtropical jet (STJ): ~25-30 degrees S
Polar front jet (PFJ): ~40-60 degrees S -- varies with weather pattern
CAT most intense on cold (poleward) side and cyclonic (left in SH) side of jet
Jet streams affect flight planning: wind component, turbulence, fuel burn

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects on temperature within the atmosphere due to water vapour.
concept: Water vapour is the most powerful greenhouse gas in the lower atmosphere. It absorbs outgoing longwave (terrestrial) radiation and re-emits it in all directions, effectively trapping heat near the surface. This is why humid nights tend to stay warm (water vapour prevents radiative cooling) and dry desert nights get cold (no water vapour to trap heat). Water vapour also releases latent heat when it condenses into cloud or freezes into ice -- this warms the surrounding air. In fact, latent heat release is a major energy source driving weather systems, particularly thunderstorms and tropical cyclones. For NZ, water vapour's role in moderating temperature extremes (maritime climate) and driving precipitation is fundamental to the weather you'll encounter flying here.
exam_tip: Water vapour is the most potent greenhouse gas. It traps heat near the surface (humid nights stay warm, dry nights cool rapidly) and releases latent heat during condensation.
key_facts: Water vapour = most important greenhouse gas in the lower atmosphere
Absorbs outgoing longwave radiation, traps heat near the surface
Humid air: less radiative cooling at night (stays warmer)
Dry air: more radiative cooling at night (cools rapidly)
Releases latent heat during condensation -- powers weather systems
Concentration decreases rapidly with altitude and toward the poles

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects on temperature within the atmosphere due to carbon dioxide.
concept: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is the second most significant greenhouse gas after water vapour. Like water vapour, CO2 absorbs outgoing longwave radiation and re-emits it, warming the lower atmosphere. The key difference is that CO2 is well-mixed throughout the atmosphere (its concentration doesn't drop off with altitude like water vapour does), so its warming effect extends through a deeper layer. CO2 is also much longer-lived in the atmosphere than water vapour. From a pure meteorology perspective, the important point is that CO2 contributes to the baseline greenhouse effect that keeps Earth's surface temperature habitable. The increasing concentration of CO2 is the primary driver of long-term climate warming.
exam_tip: CO2 is a long-lived, well-mixed greenhouse gas. It absorbs outgoing longwave radiation like water vapour, but doesn't decrease with altitude the way water vapour does.
key_facts: CO2 = second most significant greenhouse gas
Absorbs outgoing longwave radiation, warms lower atmosphere
Well-mixed throughout atmosphere (doesn't decrease with altitude like water vapour)
Much longer-lived in the atmosphere than water vapour
Contributes to baseline greenhouse effect maintaining habitable temperatures
Increasing CO2 is the primary driver of long-term climate warming

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects on temperature within the atmosphere due to ozone.
concept: Ozone (O3) has a completely different heating mechanism from the greenhouse gases. While water vapour and CO2 absorb outgoing longwave radiation, ozone absorbs incoming shortwave UV radiation from the sun in the stratosphere. This absorption heats the stratosphere, creating the temperature increase with height that makes the stratosphere stable. The ozone layer sits mostly between about 15 and 35 km altitude. Without it, the stratosphere would be much colder, the tropopause would behave differently, and -- from a non-aviation perspective -- dangerous levels of UV radiation would reach the surface. For meteorology, ozone's heating of the stratosphere is what creates the stable cap that limits convective growth at the tropopause.
exam_tip: Ozone absorbs incoming UV radiation in the stratosphere -- a completely different mechanism from greenhouse gases. This is what makes the stratosphere warm and stable.
key_facts: Ozone absorbs incoming shortwave UV radiation (not outgoing longwave)
Heating occurs in the stratosphere (~15-35 km altitude)
Creates the temperature increase with height in the stratosphere
This makes the stratosphere inherently stable
Stability of the stratosphere caps convective growth at the tropopause
Different mechanism from water vapour/CO2 greenhouse effect

syllabus_desc: Describe temperature, its measurement and scales used in aviation
concept: Temperature is a measure of the thermal energy or molecular kinetic energy of a substance — the faster molecules move, the higher the temperature. In aviation, three temperature scales are used. Celsius (degrees C): defined with water freezing at 0 degrees and boiling at 100 degrees at standard pressure. Used in aviation weather products, METARs, performance charts, and most instruments. Kelvin (K): the absolute scale where 0 K is absolute zero (-273.15 degrees C). Used in thermodynamic calculations (the ideal gas law requires Kelvin). The conversion is K = degrees C + 273.15. Fahrenheit (degrees F): used in some US publications and some older aircraft manuals. Conversion: C = (F - 32) x 5/9. Air temperature is measured with a thermometer. In aviation, the Outside Air Temperature (OAT) is measured by a temperature probe — on aircraft, a Rosemount or similar type probe accounts for the adiabatic heating of ram air. The probe may slightly overread at high speeds (Total Air Temperature = Static Air Temperature + adiabatic heating correction). METAR temperature is measured at 1.5 m height in shade and rounded to the nearest whole degree C.
exam_tip: Three scales: Celsius (aviation standard), Kelvin (thermodynamics — K = C + 273.15), Fahrenheit (some US docs). METAR temperature measured at 1.5 m in shade. Aircraft OAT probes account for ram air adiabatic heating.
key_facts: Temperature: measure of molecular kinetic energy
Celsius: 0 = freezing, 100 = boiling (standard aviation use)
Kelvin: absolute scale — K = degrees C + 273.15 (thermodynamic calculations)
Fahrenheit: C = (F - 32) x 5/9
METAR temperature: measured at 1.5 m in shade, rounded to nearest 1 degree C
Aircraft OAT probe: accounts for adiabatic ram air heating (Total Air Temperature)

syllabus_desc: Describe the lapse rates — dry adiabatic, saturated adiabatic, and environmental
concept: Lapse rate describes how temperature changes with altitude. Three lapse rates are critical in aviation meteorology. Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR): the rate at which unsaturated air cools when lifted adiabatically (without exchanging heat with the environment). The DALR is approximately 3 degrees C per 1000 ft (9.8 degrees C per 1000 m) and is constant regardless of temperature or pressure. Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR): the rate at which saturated (cloud-forming) air cools when lifted. Because condensation releases latent heat, cooling is slower than for dry air. The SALR varies with temperature and pressure but averages approximately 1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft (4–6 degrees C per 1000 m). At high temperatures (and thus high moisture content), the SALR is slower. Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR): the actual temperature decrease with altitude in the ambient atmosphere at a given time and place — measured by radiosonde. The ELR varies continuously. Atmospheric stability is determined by comparing the ELR to the DALR/SALR: if ELR > DALR, the atmosphere is absolutely unstable; if ELR < SALR, it is absolutely stable; if ELR lies between them, it is conditionally unstable.
exam_tip: DALR: 3 degrees C/1000 ft (dry unsaturated air). SALR: ~1.5 degrees C/1000 ft (saturated, cloudy air — slower due to latent heat). ELR: actual atmosphere lapse rate. Stability = compare ELR to DALR and SALR.
key_facts: DALR: 3 degrees C per 1000 ft — constant for unsaturated air
SALR: ~1.5 degrees C per 1000 ft — slower due to latent heat release on condensation
SALR varies with temperature — faster in cold air, slower in warm air
ELR: actual ambient temperature profile — measured by radiosonde
ELR > DALR: absolutely unstable; ELR < SALR: absolutely stable
SALR < ELR < DALR: conditionally unstable (stable if dry, unstable if saturated)

syllabus_desc: Describe temperature inversions — types, formation, and effects on aviation
concept: A temperature inversion is a layer in which temperature increases with altitude rather than decreasing — the normal lapse rate is reversed. Inversions are stable layers that inhibit vertical motion — they suppress convection, trap moisture and pollutants below, and are associated with fog, haze, and low cloud below the inversion with clear skies above. Four main inversion types: radiation inversion — forms overnight at the surface when the ground cools by radiation. Subsidence inversion — forms when descending air in an anticyclone warms adiabatically faster than the underlying surface air, creating an elevated warm layer. Frontal inversion — at a frontal boundary where warm air overlies cold air. Marine inversion — cool maritime air overlies a warm land surface or vice versa. For aviation, inversions trap pollution and create poor visibility at low levels, inhibit cloud development and turbulence above them (giving smooth, clear air aloft), and mark a sudden change in wind and visibility on descent through them. An inversion layer on approach typically transitions from clear VMC above to haze/low cloud/fog below — the transition can be rapid and disorienting.
exam_tip: Inversion: temperature increases with altitude — stable layer. Traps moisture and pollutants below. Clear and smooth above; hazy/foggy below. Descending through an inversion on approach: rapid VMC to IFR transition.
key_facts: Temperature increases with height — stable layer, inhibits convection
Radiation inversion: overnight surface cooling, typically in lowest 300–500 ft
Subsidence inversion: anticyclonic descent — elevated warm layer caps convection
Frontal inversion: warm air overlies cold air at frontal boundary
Below inversion: hazy, foggy, poor visibility; above: clear, smooth, excellent VMC
Rapid VMC to IFR transition on descent through inversion — be prepared

syllabus_desc: Describe radiation as a heat transfer mechanism in the atmosphere
concept: Radiation is the transfer of energy as electromagnetic waves — it does not require a medium and can travel through vacuum. The Sun emits shortwave (solar) radiation predominantly in the visible and near-ultraviolet spectrum. The Earth and atmosphere absorb this and re-emit it as longwave (terrestrial) infrared radiation. The atmosphere is relatively transparent to incoming solar shortwave radiation but absorbs and re-emits longwave radiation — this is the greenhouse effect. During the day, the Earth's surface is heated by absorbing solar radiation. At night, the surface radiates heat to space, cooling rapidly — particularly on clear nights when there is no cloud to absorb and re-radiate longwave energy back down. Cloud acts like a thermal blanket — reducing both daytime heating (reflects solar radiation back to space) and nighttime cooling (absorbs outgoing longwave and re-radiates some back down). This is why clear nights cool more than cloudy nights — radiation fog can form. The radiation balance at the surface drives the diurnal (daily) temperature cycle that creates thermals and convection.
exam_tip: Radiation: electromagnetic energy transfer — solar shortwave in, terrestrial longwave out. Clear nights lose more heat by radiation than cloudy nights — that's why radiation fog needs clear skies to form.
key_facts: Solar radiation: shortwave (visible/UV) — heats Earth's surface during daytime
Terrestrial radiation: longwave (infrared) — Earth radiates heat to space at night
Greenhouse effect: atmosphere absorbs longwave, re-emits some downward
Clear nights: rapid surface cooling (radiation fog risk)
Cloudy nights: cloud absorbs longwave and re-radiates — surface stays warmer
Radiation drives diurnal temperature cycle — source of daytime thermals and convection

syllabus_desc: Describe conduction and convection as heat transfer mechanisms in the atmosphere
concept: Conduction is the transfer of heat through direct molecular contact — molecules vibrate and transfer energy to adjacent molecules. In the atmosphere, conduction is important only in the very thin surface layer directly in contact with the ground. The atmosphere is a poor conductor of heat — conduction does not transfer heat efficiently through the depth of the troposphere. Convection is the transfer of heat by the bulk movement of air (or any fluid). Warm air, being less dense, rises; cooler air sinks and replaces it. This bulk vertical movement of air is called convection and is the dominant heat transfer mechanism in the troposphere. Forced convection occurs when air is mechanically lifted (e.g. by terrain or a front). Free convection (or natural convection) occurs when surface heating creates positively buoyant air that rises as a thermal. Convective activity produces cumulus clouds and, if sufficient instability and moisture are present, cumulonimbus and thunderstorms. The daily cycle of surface heating and convection is the primary driver of cumulus development and afternoon thunderstorms over NZ in summer.
exam_tip: Conduction: molecular contact — only important at the immediate surface. Convection: bulk air movement — dominant tropospheric heat transfer. Free convection = thermals; forced convection = orographic/frontal lifting.
key_facts: Conduction: molecular vibration transfer — efficient in solids, very poor in air
Conduction important only in the very thin surface layer (<10 mm)
Convection: bulk movement of air transferring heat through the troposphere
Free convection: positive buoyancy drives thermals from heated surface
Forced convection: mechanical lifting by terrain, fronts, or wind convergence
Convection dominates tropospheric heat transfer — drives cumulus and Cb development

syllabus_desc: Describe latent heat and its role in atmospheric processes
concept: Latent heat is the energy absorbed or released when a substance changes phase without changing temperature — it is 'hidden' heat. When water evaporates, it absorbs latent heat from the surroundings (cooling them — evaporative cooling). When water vapour condenses, it releases latent heat back to the surroundings (warming them). When water freezes, it releases latent heat; when ice melts, it absorbs it. In the atmosphere, latent heat is a critical source of energy for convective storms. When moist air rises and cools to its dew point, condensation begins — the released latent heat adds to the buoyancy of the rising air parcel, making it warmer than its surroundings and accelerating the ascent. This latent heat release is why the Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR) is slower than the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate — the condensing air is being internally heated by its own latent heat. In tropical cyclones, latent heat from ocean evaporation is the primary energy source — the warm tropical ocean supplies water vapour which condenses in the eyewall Cb, releasing enormous amounts of latent heat to drive the storm.
exam_tip: Latent heat: energy absorbed (evaporation) or released (condensation) during phase change — no temperature change. Condensation releases latent heat — this is why SALR < DALR and why thunderstorms are self-sustaining. Cyclone energy = ocean latent heat.
key_facts: Latent heat: energy absorbed/released during phase change without temperature change
Evaporation: absorbs latent heat (cools surroundings)
Condensation: releases latent heat (warms surroundings)
Latent heat release in clouds adds buoyancy — drives convective acceleration
Why SALR < DALR: condensation releases latent heat, slowing cooling rate
Tropical cyclone energy source: latent heat from ocean evaporation/condensation

syllabus_desc: Describe the diurnal (daily) variation in surface temperature
concept: The diurnal temperature cycle is the daily pattern of surface temperature driven by the balance between incoming solar radiation and outgoing terrestrial radiation. At night, the surface loses heat by radiation and cools — minimum temperature is typically reached just before or at sunrise. After sunrise, solar radiation warms the surface faster than it loses heat — temperature rises. Maximum temperature is typically reached in early-to-mid afternoon (1400–1600 hours local solar time) when the sun's angle is still high but the accumulated heating effect is at its peak. After the solar maximum, terrestrial radiation exceeds incoming solar and the surface cools. The amplitude of the diurnal cycle depends on cloud cover (clouds reduce both the daytime maximum and nocturnal cooling), moisture content (dry ground heats and cools faster than moist), and terrain (valleys and inland areas have larger amplitude than coastal zones). In NZ, coastal aerodromes experience a moderated diurnal range (sea moderates temperature), while inland aerodromes in Canterbury, Otago, and Hawke's Bay have large diurnal ranges — very cold nights and hot afternoons.
exam_tip: Daily temperature minimum: just before sunrise. Maximum: early-to-mid afternoon. Clouds reduce the range (less daytime heating, less night cooling). Inland areas have bigger daily range than coastal areas.
key_facts: Minimum temperature: just before sunrise — maximum night cooling
Maximum temperature: early-to-mid afternoon (~1400–1600 local time)
Cloud reduces amplitude — less daytime heating and less nocturnal cooling
Dry ground: larger daily range (heats and cools faster than moist ground)
Coastal: moderated by sea — smaller diurnal range
NZ inland aerodromes (Canterbury, Otago): large diurnal range — cold nights, hot afternoons

syllabus_desc: Describe seasonal temperature variation and its effects on aviation
concept: Seasonal temperature variation results from the changing angle of the sun and the length of daylight hours as Earth orbits the sun. In the Southern Hemisphere, summer (December–February) brings higher sun angles, longer days, and greater solar radiation — temperatures are highest. Winter (June–August) has low sun angles, shorter days, and less solar input — temperatures are lowest. In NZ, the seasonal temperature range is moderated by the surrounding ocean — NZ does not experience the extreme seasonal variation of continental interiors. However, the seasons still produce significantly different aviation conditions: summer brings convective weather, higher density altitude, heat hazards, and afternoon thunderstorms; winter brings cold temperatures, icing conditions, fog, radiation inversions, and challenging surface conditions. The freezing level varies dramatically — from near sea level in Southland in winter to above 14,000 ft in the North Island in summer. Performance calculations, fuel planning, and icing awareness must all reflect the seasonal conditions. Seasonal variations also drive monsoon patterns, ITCZ migration, and the tropical cyclone season.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere summer = Dec-Feb (higher sun, longer days). NZ's ocean moderates the seasonal range. Summer: convection, high density altitude. Winter: icing, fog, cold performance (better engine power but icing risk).
key_facts: SH summer: December–February — higher sun angle, longer days, maximum heating
SH winter: June–August — lower sun angle, shorter days, minimum temperatures
NZ ocean moderation: less seasonal variation than continental climates
Summer: convection, afternoon Cb, high density altitude, heat haze
Winter: icing conditions, radiation fog, cold temperatures (better performance but icing risk)
Freezing level: near sea level (SI winter) to 14,000+ ft (NI summer)

syllabus_desc: Describe the variation of temperature with altitude in the atmosphere
concept: In the troposphere, temperature generally decreases with altitude at the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) — averaging about 6.5 degrees C per 1000 m but varying considerably. At the tropopause, temperature stops decreasing and levels off at approximately -56.5 degrees C (ISA) — this temperature minimum is called the cold point. In the stratosphere above, temperature first remains constant then increases with altitude (due to ozone absorbing solar UV radiation). At the stratopause (~50 km), temperature reaches a local maximum before decreasing again in the mesosphere. For aviation, the tropospheric temperature decrease with altitude is what drives the lapse rate calculations, density altitude, cloud base height, and freezing level. Significant deviations from the average ELR — inversions, unstable layers, isothermal layers — are detected by radiosonde and used to forecast cloud formation, turbulence, and convective potential. In NZ, the tropopause height varies seasonally and by latitude — higher (55,000 ft) over the tropics, lower (30,000–35,000 ft) over the Southern Ocean.
exam_tip: Temperature decreases in troposphere (ELR), is constant at tropopause (-56.5 degrees C ISA), then increases in stratosphere (ozone absorbs UV). The tropopause is lower at high latitudes (30,000–35,000 ft) and higher in the tropics (55,000 ft).
key_facts: Troposphere: temperature generally decreases with altitude (ELR avg 6.5 degrees C/1000 m)
Tropopause: temperature ceases to decrease — cold point at -56.5 degrees C in ISA
Stratosphere: temperature increases (ozone absorbs UV solar radiation)
NZ tropopause: 30,000–35,000 ft at mid-latitudes; 50,000–55,000 ft in tropics
Deviations from ELR (inversions, unstable layers) detected by radiosonde
ELR is the key parameter for forecasting convective cloud base, freezing level, stability

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors affecting surface temperature and heat transfer
concept: Surface temperature is influenced by multiple factors beyond solar radiation. Land type: dark surfaces (asphalt, bare soil) absorb more radiation and heat quickly; light surfaces (snow, sand, concrete) reflect more and heat less. This is the albedo effect. Water: water's high specific heat capacity means it heats and cools slowly compared to land — this is why coastal areas have moderate temperatures and ocean areas have a small diurnal and seasonal range. Vegetation: well-vegetated surfaces lose heat through evapotranspiration, moderating temperature. Urban areas (urban heat island effect) are warmer than surrounding rural areas due to heat-absorbing concrete and asphalt and heat generation from human activity. Altitude: temperature decreases with altitude at the ELR — elevated terrain is cooler than surrounding lowlands. Aspect: north-facing slopes in the SH receive less sun and are cooler; south-facing slopes receive more. Wind: strong winds mix the boundary layer and moderate surface temperature extremes. For NZ pilots, surface temperature at the aerodrome affects density altitude calculations and convective initiation time — on hot summer days, surface heating rapidly creates thermals by early afternoon.
exam_tip: Dark surfaces (asphalt) heat quickly (low albedo). Light surfaces (snow) reflect radiation (high albedo). Water heats slowly (high specific heat). Vegetation cools by evapotranspiration. Urban heat island = warmer than rural.
key_facts: Albedo effect: dark surfaces absorb more (low albedo); light surfaces reflect more (high albedo)
Water: high specific heat — heats and cools slowly, moderates coastal temperature
Vegetation: evapotranspiration cools the surface
Urban heat island: cities warmer than surrounding rural areas
Altitude: elevated terrain cooler due to ELR
Strong winds mix boundary layer — moderate temperature extremes

syllabus_desc: Describe upper air temperature measurement and its importance
concept: Upper air temperature is measured primarily by radiosondes — instrument packages carried aloft on weather balloons that transmit data back to the ground by radio as they ascend. A radiosonde typically measures temperature, humidity, and pressure at 1-second intervals as it rises through the troposphere and lower stratosphere. The ascent rate is typically 5–6 m/s, reaching bursting altitude of 20–35 km. Temperature, dewpoint, and wind data at all levels are used to construct a thermodynamic diagram (tephigram or skew-T log-P diagram) that is the primary tool for stability analysis, cloud base prediction, and icing level determination. In NZ, radiosondes are launched twice daily (0000 UTC and 1200 UTC) from Invercargill, Paraparaumu, Whenuapai, and Haast. Aircraft are another source of upper air temperature data — AMDAR (Aircraft Meteorological Data Relay) provides temperature and wind data from commercial aircraft en route. Satellite temperature soundings provide global coverage but lower vertical resolution than radiosondes. Upper air temperature data is the backbone of all NWP model initialisation and all significant weather forecasting.
exam_tip: Radiosondes: balloons carrying instruments to measure temperature, humidity, and pressure as they ascend. NZ launches at 4 stations twice daily (0000 and 1200 UTC). Data feeds NWP models and produces tephigrams for stability analysis.
key_facts: Radiosondes: balloon-carried instruments measuring temperature, humidity, pressure by radio
Ascent rate ~5–6 m/s; bursts at 20–35 km altitude
NZ launch stations: Invercargill, Paraparaumu, Whenuapai, Haast — twice daily
Data used to construct tephigrams — stability analysis, cloud base, icing levels
AMDAR: commercial aircraft transmit upper air temperature/wind data en route
Radiosonde data initialises NWP models for all NZ weather forecast products

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between temperature and air density
concept: Air density is the mass of air per unit volume (kg/m3). It varies with both temperature and pressure according to the ideal gas law: density = pressure / (gas constant x temperature in Kelvin). At constant pressure, an increase in temperature causes a decrease in density — the air expands. At constant temperature, an increase in pressure causes an increase in density. Warm air is less dense than cold air — this is why hot air balloons rise (the heated air inside is less dense than the surrounding cooler air), and why cumulus towers grow upward (surface-heated air parcels are less dense than surrounding air and are positively buoyant). Lower density means fewer air molecules per unit volume — which reduces engine power output (less mass airflow through the engine), reduces aerodynamic lift per unit area (wings require higher true airspeed to generate the same lift), and reduces propeller efficiency. Understanding the temperature-density relationship is fundamental to grasping density altitude and its impact on performance calculations for every flight.
exam_tip: Warm air = less dense = less engine power, less lift per unit area. Cold air = more dense = better performance. Density = pressure / (R x T in Kelvin). At constant pressure, density and temperature are inversely related.
key_facts: Density = pressure / (gas constant x temperature in Kelvin)
Warm air: less dense — molecules are further apart
Cold air: more dense — better engine power and aerodynamic efficiency
Lower density = less engine mass airflow = reduced thrust
Lower density = wing requires higher TAS to generate same lift
Hot air rises (positive buoyancy) because it is less dense than surrounding cooler air

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of temperature on aircraft and engine performance
concept: Temperature affects aircraft performance in three main ways — through density effects, direct thermal effects, and material effects. Density effects (the most important): high temperature reduces air density, which reduces engine power (piston engines are less efficient; turbines have less mass airflow for thrust), reduces wing efficiency (higher true airspeed required for the same lift), and extends take-off and landing distances. The net effect is captured in the density altitude concept — a piston aircraft at density altitude 6000 ft will perform as if it were at a physical altitude of 6000 ft even if it is at sea level. Direct thermal effects: high temperatures can cause metal expansion, affect lubricant viscosity, and create heat soak in components. Engine cylinder head temperatures must remain within limits. Cold temperatures increase air density (performance improves) but create icing hazards and may require cold weather starting procedures. For turbine engines, thrust generally increases in cold dense air. However, very cold temperatures (-40 degrees C and below) can affect fuel viscosity, hydraulic fluid, and battery performance significantly.
exam_tip: High temperature: reduced engine power, higher TAS for same lift, longer take-off roll — all captured by density altitude. Cold temperature: better engine performance but icing risk. Turbines give more thrust in cold dense air.
key_facts: High temperature: reduces density → less engine power, reduced aerodynamic efficiency
Longer take-off and landing roll in high temperature/density altitude conditions
Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature — the performance reference
Cold temperatures: increased density = better engine performance (especially turbines)
Cold weather hazards: icing, cold-soaked fuel/hydraulics, battery limitations
Very cold (-40 degrees C): fuel viscosity, hydraulic seals, and batteries affected

syllabus_desc: Describe temperature anomalies and their significance for aviation
concept: A temperature anomaly is a deviation from the expected (ISA) temperature at a given altitude. ISA deviation (ISA+/- value) describes how much warmer or colder the actual temperature is compared to ISA at a given level. ISA+15 means the actual temperature is 15 degrees C warmer than ISA at that level. Anomalies are significant for: performance planning (ISA+15 or more at an elevated aerodrome can substantially increase density altitude and degrade performance — important for take-off weight limits); altimetry (warm airmass: altimeter underreads, you are higher than indicated; cold airmass: altimeter overreads, you are lower — particularly hazardous on approach); icing (temperature anomalies determine whether icing conditions exist — a warm anomaly aloft with freezing below = freezing rain scenario); and forecasting (large temperature anomalies help forecasters identify blocking patterns, anomalous jet stream positions, and potential severe weather triggers). In NZ, warm temperature anomalies associated with northerly airflows from the tropics are common in summer; cold anomalies in deep southerly flows from the Southern Ocean occur year-round.
exam_tip: ISA+ = warmer than standard = reduced density = worse performance. ISA- = colder = better performance. Cold anomalies cause altimeter to overread (you are lower than indicated). Warm anomalies above with cold below = freezing rain risk.
key_facts: Temperature anomaly = deviation from ISA at a given level (ISA+/- value)
ISA+: warmer than standard — higher density altitude, reduced performance
ISA-: colder than standard — lower density altitude, better performance
Cold airmass: altimeter overreads (you are lower than indicated) — CFIT risk
Warm anomaly aloft with cold at surface: freezing rain scenario — severe icing
NZ: warm anomalies in northerly flows; cold anomalies in deep southerly flows year-round

syllabus_desc: Describe atmospheric pressure and its measurement
concept: Atmospheric pressure is the weight of the column of air above a unit area of the Earth's surface. At sea level, standard atmospheric pressure is 1013.25 hPa (hectopascals), also expressed as 29.92 inches of mercury (inHg) or 760 mm of mercury (mmHg). Pressure decreases with altitude because there is less air above. Pressure is measured using a barometer — the aneroid barometer (a sealed, corrugated metal capsule that expands and contracts with pressure changes) is used in aircraft altimeters and cockpit barometers. Mercury barometers use the height of a mercury column. For aviation purposes, pressure is reported in hPa to the nearest whole hPa in most of the world (including NZ). The change in pressure with altitude averages approximately 1 hPa per 30 ft (approximately 1 hPa per 9 m) at low altitudes, decreasing in rate at higher altitudes. Atmospheric pressure varies both with altitude and with changing weather systems — high-pressure areas have higher surface pressure, low-pressure areas have lower surface pressure than the average.
exam_tip: Standard sea level pressure: 1013.25 hPa. Pressure decreases with altitude — approximately 1 hPa per 30 ft at low levels. Pressure is measured with an aneroid barometer in aviation instruments.
key_facts: Atmospheric pressure = weight of air column above unit area of surface
Standard sea level pressure: 1013.25 hPa = 29.92 inHg = 760 mmHg
Pressure decreases with altitude — ~1 hPa per 30 ft at low levels
Aneroid barometer: sealed metal capsule that expands/contracts with pressure
Reported in hPa in NZ and most countries
High-pressure systems have higher-than-average surface pressure; lows have lower

topic: Altimeter Pressure Settings — QNH, QFE, QNE
syllabus_desc: Describe QNH, QFE, and QNE altimeter pressure settings
concept: Aircraft altimeters are aneroid instruments that convert measured pressure into altitude using a reference pressure setting. Three settings are in common use. QNH (Regional or Aerodrome QNH): the altimeter is set to the actual surface pressure corrected to mean sea level (MSL). An altimeter set to QNH reads elevation above sea level (AMSL). At the aerodrome, it reads the field elevation. QNH is the most commonly used setting for en-route and approach flight, and is the setting used in NZ's transition layer. QFE: the altimeter is set to the actual surface pressure at the aerodrome reference point. An altimeter set to QFE reads height above the aerodrome — it reads zero on the ground at that aerodrome. QFE is less commonly used in NZ but may be used for circuit operations at some aerodromes. QNE (Flight Level/Standard Setting): the altimeter is set to 1013.25 hPa (Standard Pressure). Above the transition altitude (6000 ft AMSL in NZ), all aircraft use QNE and report altitude as a Flight Level. In NZ, the transition altitude is 6000 ft and the transition layer is 6000–7000 ft — a buffer zone where aircraft may be using either QNH or QNE.
exam_tip: QNH: reads AMSL (set to MSL pressure) — most common. QFE: reads height above aerodrome (reads zero on ground). QNE: standard 1013 hPa — used above NZ transition altitude of 6000 ft — gives Flight Level.
key_facts: QNH: altimeter set to MSL pressure — reads altitude AMSL
QFE: set to aerodrome reference pressure — reads height above aerodrome (zero on ground)
QNE: set to 1013.25 hPa (standard) — reads Flight Level above transition altitude
NZ transition altitude: 6000 ft AMSL; transition layer: 6000–7000 ft
Above 7000 ft: all aircraft use Flight Levels (QNE)
Below 6000 ft: QNH used; ATC provides QNH setting for the area

syllabus_desc: Describe altimeter setting procedures and the transition altitude
concept: Correct altimeter setting procedures are critical for safe terrain clearance and separation from other aircraft. Below the transition altitude (6000 ft in NZ), aircraft use QNH — pilots must update the QNH setting regularly as they move between different QNH zones, because pressure varies across a region. ATC provides updated QNH in clearances, and it is also broadcast on ATIS. When climbing through the transition layer (6000–7000 ft), pilots set 1013.25 hPa and thereafter report altitude as a Flight Level. When descending from flight levels, at or before passing through 7000 ft, pilots revert to the area QNH. The transition altitude (TA) and transition level (TL) are key reference points published in AIP NZ. In NZ, the transition altitude is 6000 ft and the transition level is FL 075 (7500 ft on 1013.25 hPa) or as published by ATC in traffic conditions. During IFR approaches, altimeter setting is critical — the approach chart specifies which setting to use and at which altitude to transition from QNE to QNH. Failure to correctly set the altimeter has been a factor in controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) accidents.
exam_tip: Below 6000 ft: QNH. Climbing through 6000–7000 ft transition layer: set 1013 for flight levels. Descending: revert to QNH at or before 7000 ft. Update QNH as you fly between regions — pressure varies across NZ.
key_facts: Below 6000 ft: use QNH — update as you move between regional QNH zones
Climbing through 6000–7000 ft transition layer: set 1013.25 hPa
Descending: revert to area QNH at or before passing 7000 ft
NZ transition altitude: 6000 ft; transition level: FL075 or as directed by ATC
ATC provides QNH in clearances; also on ATIS at controlled aerodromes
Incorrect altimeter setting is a CFIT risk factor — double check on every descent

syllabus_desc: Describe the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)
concept: The International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) is a defined model of how atmospheric properties vary with altitude, used as a reference for aircraft performance, instrument calibration, and pressure altitude calculations. The ISA defines: sea level temperature of 15 degrees C (288.15 K), sea level pressure of 1013.25 hPa, sea level density of 1.225 kg/m3, and a standard lapse rate of -6.5 degrees C per 1000 m (approximately -2 degrees C per 1000 ft) up to the tropopause at 11 km (36,090 ft). Above the tropopause, temperature is constant at -56.5 degrees C in the stratosphere. The ISA is not a description of real atmospheric conditions — the actual atmosphere deviates from ISA in temperature, pressure, and density. These deviations — ISA deviation or ISA+/- — are important for aircraft performance (engine thrust, density altitude) and altimetry errors. Density is a function of both pressure and temperature — the real density at any altitude can be calculated from the actual temperature and pressure deviations from ISA. In NZ, hot summer days at elevated aerodromes produce significant ISA+ conditions.
exam_tip: ISA sea level: 15 degrees C, 1013.25 hPa, lapse rate 6.5 degrees C/1000 m. Real atmosphere deviates — ISA+ means warmer than standard. ISA+ reduces density, increases density altitude, degrades performance.
key_facts: ISA sea level: temp 15 degrees C, pressure 1013.25 hPa, density 1.225 kg/m3
ISA lapse rate: 6.5 degrees C per 1000 m (-2 degrees C per 1000 ft) to tropopause
Tropopause at 11 km (36,090 ft) in ISA — temperature constant at -56.5 degrees C above
Real atmosphere deviates from ISA — ISA+ = warmer than standard
ISA+ conditions reduce density and degrade aircraft performance
NZ hot summer days at elevated aerodromes = significant ISA+ scenarios

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between pressure altitude and density altitude
concept: Pressure altitude is the altitude in the standard atmosphere (ISA) that corresponds to a given pressure — it is what the altimeter reads when set to 1013.25 hPa (QNE). Density altitude is the altitude in the ISA that corresponds to the actual air density at your location. It accounts for both pressure (altitude) and temperature deviation from ISA. The relationship: Density Altitude (ft) = Pressure Altitude (ft) + [120 x (OAT - ISA temperature at that altitude)] for a rough calculation. At higher than ISA temperatures, density altitude is higher than pressure altitude — the aircraft is performing as if it were at a higher altitude than it physically is. This is critical for take-off and climb performance on hot days. At standard temperature, density altitude = pressure altitude. At ISA-20 degrees C (very cold), density altitude is less than pressure altitude — better than standard performance. NZ pilots must calculate density altitude for performance-critical operations at any aerodrome, particularly in summer at high-elevation aerodromes such as Queenstown, Wanaka, or Pukaki.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature. ISA+ = density altitude > pressure altitude = reduced performance. ISA- = density altitude < pressure altitude = better performance. Hot day at high elevation = worst case.
key_facts: Pressure altitude: altimeter reading with 1013.25 hPa set (QNE)
Density altitude: ISA altitude equivalent to actual air density (pressure + temperature effect)
ISA+: density altitude > pressure altitude — reduced performance
ISA-: density altitude < pressure altitude — better than standard performance
Rough formula: DA = PA + 120 x (OAT - ISA temp at that level)
High NZ aerodromes (Queenstown, Wanaka, Pukaki) + summer heat = critical density altitude

topic: Temperature Effects on Pressure and Density
syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature on atmospheric pressure and density
concept: The relationship between temperature, pressure, and density follows the ideal gas law: Pressure = density x gas constant x temperature (in Kelvin). At constant pressure, an increase in temperature causes a decrease in density — the air expands and becomes less dense. This is why hot air rises (it is less dense than surrounding cooler air) and why aircraft performance degrades in hot conditions. At the surface, high temperatures reduce surface air density, which reduces engine power (less mass airflow) and aerodynamic efficiency (wings generate less lift per unit area at lower density). In the upper atmosphere, temperature anomalies also affect pressure altitude — in a cold column of air, pressure surfaces are lower than in the ISA (altimeter reads high), and in a warm column they are higher. This has direct safety implications: flying toward a cold airmass at a constant indicated altitude, the true altitude decreases — 'from high to low, look out below'. The correction factor for altimetry errors in cold temperatures is applied in ICAO Annex 2 procedures and in NZ cold temperature corrections published in AIP NZ for precision approaches.
exam_tip: Hot air = less dense. 'From high to low, look out below' — flying toward cold air, the altimeter overreads and you are lower than indicated. NZ cold temperature corrections for precision approaches are mandatory.
key_facts: Hot air: expands, less dense — engine power and lift reduced
Cold air: more dense — better aircraft performance
Cold airmass: pressure surfaces lower than ISA — altimeter overreads (you are lower than indicated)
Warm airmass: pressure surfaces higher — altimeter underreads (you are higher than indicated)
Rule: 'from high to low, look out below' — flying toward lower temperature area
Cold temperature corrections for IFR approaches required per AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the altimeter errors caused by temperature deviations from ISA
concept: The pressure altimeter assumes the atmosphere conforms to the ISA temperature profile. When the actual temperature deviates significantly from ISA, the altimeter reads incorrectly. In cold air (ISA-), pressure levels are compressed lower in the atmosphere than the ISA model predicts — the altimeter overreads, meaning you are actually lower than the altitude indicated. In warm air (ISA+), pressure levels are expanded higher than ISA — the altimeter underreads, meaning you are actually higher than indicated. The cold temperature error is the more dangerous scenario and the one for which corrections are published. For example, at -30 degrees C (ISA-45 degrees C), the error at 5000 ft can be approximately 800 ft — you may believe you are at 5000 ft when you are actually at 4200 ft. ICAO Annex 2 requires that when temperature is significantly below ISA, crews apply a correction to published approach minima. MetService NZ provides cold temperature correction tables in AIP NZ ENR 1.7. This is a required competency for CPL pilots conducting IFR approaches in NZ winter conditions.
exam_tip: Cold air = altimeter overreads (you are lower than indicated). Rule: 'from high to low, look out below'. Cold temperature corrections are mandatory for IFR approaches. At ISA-45, the error at 5000 ft can be ~800 ft.
key_facts: Cold air: altimeter overreads — actual altitude lower than indicated (dangerous)
Warm air: altimeter underreads — actual altitude higher than indicated
ISA-45 at 5000 ft indicated: actual altitude can be ~800 ft lower
Cold temperature corrections required for IFR approaches in cold conditions
ICAO Annex 2 requirement; corrections published in AIP NZ ENR 1.7
Most critical at low altitudes during approach — where terrain clearance is most important

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of non-ISA pressure on altimeter accuracy
concept: The altimeter reads correctly only when the QNH setting entered matches the actual sea-level pressure. If the altimeter is set to an incorrect QNH, or if the QNH changes while in flight and is not updated, the altimeter will read incorrectly by approximately 30 ft per 1 hPa of difference. For example, if QNH is 1013 hPa but the aircraft has 1003 hPa set, the altimeter overreads by 300 ft — the aircraft is 300 ft lower than the indicated altitude. Conversely, if the QNH is 1023 hPa and 1013 hPa is set, the aircraft is 300 ft higher than indicated. The pressure gradient across a weather system or frontal boundary can be large — flying from a high-pressure area into a low-pressure area without updating the QNH setting results in the altimeter overreading. This is particularly hazardous in mountainous terrain. NZ ATC provides updated QNH as aircraft cross sector boundaries, and pilots must actively request and apply updated QNH whenever transiting between regions or when ATC provides updated settings.
exam_tip: 1 hPa altimeter error = 30 ft. Flying from high to low pressure without updating QNH: altimeter overreads, you are lower than indicated. Always update QNH when ATC provides it or when crossing into a different pressure region.
key_facts: 1 hPa QNH error = approximately 30 ft altitude error
Altimeter set too low (actual QNH higher): altimeter overreads (you are higher than indicated)
Altimeter set too high (actual QNH lower): altimeter underreads (you are lower than indicated)
Flying from high to low pressure without updating QNH = you are lower than indicated
ATC provides updated QNH when crossing sector boundaries
Request and apply updated QNH actively — essential for terrain clearance

syllabus_desc: Describe Buys Ballot's Law
concept: Buys Ballot's Law describes the relationship between wind direction and the position of low-pressure systems. In the Southern Hemisphere: if you stand with the wind at your back and turn 90 degrees to the left, the centre of the low-pressure system is approximately in that direction (slightly ahead of you, actually about 100–120 degrees to the left due to the angle of flow across isobars). This is a quick observational tool that allows a pilot or observer to estimate the location of a depression without a chart. Buys Ballot's Law works because, in the Southern Hemisphere, winds flow anticlockwise around a low — so the low is always to the left of the wind direction when facing downwind. In the Northern Hemisphere, the law is reversed — the low is to the right. The law is less precise in practice because surface winds cross isobars at an angle of 10–30 degrees (due to surface friction), but it remains a reliable rule of thumb. For NZ pilots, knowing that the low is to the left of the surface wind direction is useful for situational awareness of approaching frontal systems.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere: wind at your back, turn left 90 degrees — the low is roughly in that direction. Winds flow anticlockwise around lows in the SH. Reversed in the Northern Hemisphere.
key_facts: Southern Hemisphere: wind at back, turn left 90 degrees = low is in that direction
Based on anticlockwise flow around lows in the SH (clockwise in NH)
In practice, surface friction causes 10–30 degree angle across isobars
Useful quick tool for estimating depression location without a pressure chart
Northern Hemisphere: turn RIGHT — reversed due to NH clockwise flow around lows
More precise with upper winds (less surface friction effect)

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between pressure gradient and wind speed
concept: Wind is caused by the pressure gradient force — the force that pushes air from high to low pressure due to the pressure difference. The greater the pressure difference over a given distance (the steeper the pressure gradient), the stronger the resulting wind. On a surface analysis chart, the pressure gradient is indicated by the spacing of isobars — closely spaced isobars indicate a steep gradient and strong winds; widely spaced isobars indicate a gentle gradient and light winds. The pressure gradient force acts perpendicular to the isobars, from high to low pressure. However, due to the Coriolis effect, the actual wind does not blow directly from high to low — it is deflected and eventually flows approximately parallel to the isobars (geostrophic wind). At the surface, friction reduces the wind speed and causes wind to cross isobars slightly toward lower pressure (at an angle of 10–30 degrees). The relationship between isobar spacing and wind speed is used by pilots to quickly assess the expected wind strength from a surface analysis chart — the tighter the isobars, the stronger the wind.
exam_tip: Close isobars = steep pressure gradient = strong winds. Wide isobars = gentle gradient = light winds. Pressure gradient force pushes from high to low; Coriolis deflects it to produce near-parallel isobar flow (geostrophic wind).
key_facts: Pressure gradient force: pushes air from high to low pressure
Steep gradient (close isobars) = strong winds; gentle gradient (wide isobars) = light winds
Wind in free air flows approximately parallel to isobars (geostrophic)
Surface friction causes wind to cross isobars at 10–30 degrees toward low pressure
Coriolis effect deflects moving air left (SH) — produces rotation around systems
Read isobar spacing on surface analysis chart to quickly assess wind strength

syllabus_desc: Describe the backing and veering of wind
concept: Backing and veering describe the directional change of wind with time or altitude. Veering is a clockwise change in wind direction (e.g. north to northeast to east — clockwise when viewed from above), and backing is an anticlockwise change (e.g. north to northwest to west). In the Southern Hemisphere, the following apply: warm front approach is typically signalled by the wind backing (shifting anticlockwise). Cold front passage produces a sudden veering (clockwise shift). Wind veering with altitude (the SH wind typically backs — i.e. turns anticlockwise going up) is the opposite to the Northern Hemisphere (where wind veers with altitude). At the surface, wind backs from the upper wind direction due to friction — above the friction layer (approximately 2000 ft), wind direction approaches the geostrophic wind direction. For pilots, wind changes during flight can indicate the passage of fronts or changes in the pressure pattern — monitoring the trend is useful situational awareness. A sudden large veer is often the cold front passage indicator in NZ.
exam_tip: Veering = clockwise wind shift. Backing = anticlockwise. Southern Hemisphere: approaching warm front = backing; cold front passage = sudden veering (clockwise shift from NW to SW, for example).
key_facts: Veering: clockwise wind direction change; backing: anticlockwise change
Southern Hemisphere: wind BACKS with altitude (opposite to NH where it veers)
Warm front approach: surface wind typically backs in SH
Cold front passage: sudden veering — key signal of frontal passage
Friction layer: surface wind backs from geostrophic direction by 10–30 degrees
Above ~2000 ft: wind approaches geostrophic direction, less influence of friction

syllabus_desc: Describe the geostrophic wind
concept: The geostrophic wind is the theoretical wind that results from the exact balance between the pressure gradient force and the Coriolis force. It flows parallel to straight isobars at a speed proportional to the pressure gradient — where isobars are closely spaced, geostrophic wind is faster. In reality, the geostrophic wind is a good approximation to the actual wind in the free atmosphere above the friction layer (above approximately 2000 ft), particularly where isobars are roughly straight and not strongly curved. It is not a real wind but a theoretical concept that simplifies understanding of upper wind patterns. It does not apply near the equator (where Coriolis is zero) and it does not apply to curved flow where an additional centripetal force is involved (curved isobars require the gradient wind concept). In practice, pilots use geostrophic principles to estimate upper winds from chart isobar spacing, and forecasters use geostrophic wind calculations as a starting point for wind prediction. Upper wind charts (GRID) effectively represent analysed or forecast winds that approach geostrophic flow at higher levels.
exam_tip: Geostrophic wind: balance between pressure gradient force and Coriolis. Flows parallel to straight isobars. Good approximation above 2000 ft in free atmosphere. Not applicable at equator or in strongly curved flow.
key_facts: Balance between pressure gradient force and Coriolis force
Flows parallel to straight isobars — clockwise around highs, anticlockwise around lows (SH)
Speed proportional to isobar spacing — closer isobars = faster geostrophic wind
Good approximation above the friction layer (~2000 ft) where isobars are straight
Not valid near equator (Coriolis = 0) or in strongly curved isobar flow
Actual wind in free atmosphere closely approaches geostrophic — used in upper wind charts

syllabus_desc: Describe the gradient wind and its relationship to curved isobar flow
concept: The gradient wind accounts for the centripetal acceleration required when air flows along curved isobars — a correction to the geostrophic wind for curved flow. When isobars are curved (as around highs and lows), the flow requires a centripetal force toward the centre of curvature. Around a low-pressure system (in SH, anticlockwise flow), the centripetal force is directed inward toward the low — this assists the pressure gradient force. The result is that the gradient wind around a low is faster than the geostrophic wind for the same isobar spacing. Around a high-pressure system (SH, clockwise flow), the centripetal force is outward, opposing the pressure gradient — the gradient wind around a high is slower than the geostrophic wind. This explains why winds around lows (depressions) can be quite strong even when isobar spacing appears moderate, while highs often have surprisingly light winds near their centre despite reasonable isobar spacing. For CPL pilots, the gradient wind concept explains the asymmetry between wind strength in depressions versus anticyclones for comparable pressure gradient.
exam_tip: Gradient wind adds centripetal correction to geostrophic wind for curved isobars. Around lows: wind faster than geostrophic. Around highs: wind slower than geostrophic. This explains why depressions feel windier than the isobar spacing suggests.
key_facts: Accounts for centripetal acceleration in curved isobar flow
Around lows (SH anticlockwise): centripetal assists pressure gradient — wind faster than geostrophic
Around highs (SH clockwise): centripetal opposes pressure gradient — wind slower than geostrophic
Explains why depressions produce stronger winds than comparable-spaced isobars in a high
More accurate than geostrophic for flow around pressure systems
Not used in routine pilot calculations — but explains real-world wind asymmetry

syllabus_desc: Describe surface wind measurement and observation
concept: Surface wind is measured at a standard height of 10 m above the ground using anemometers and wind vanes. An anemometer measures wind speed — the cup anemometer (rotating cups that spin faster as wind speed increases) is the most common type. A wind vane aligns with the wind direction. At aerodromes, Automatic Weather Stations (AWS) measure and report wind continuously. At large aerodromes, multiple anemometers may be positioned along the runway to give wind readings at touchdown, midfield, and the other end — particularly important for operations in variable conditions. Wind speed is measured in knots (kt) for aviation. Wind direction is always reported as the direction FROM which the wind blows, referenced to True North in observations (METAR) but Magnetic North on visual displays and for pilot communications. Wind speed reporting: mean speed is the 10-minute average; gusts are the peak values within that period. If gusts exceed the mean speed by 10 kt or more, they are reported separately (e.g. 240/15G28 means wind from 240 degrees, mean speed 15 kt, gusts to 28 kt).
exam_tip: Wind direction is the direction FROM which wind comes. Reported as True North in METARs. ATIS and ATC use Magnetic. If gusts exceed mean by 10+ kt, they are separately reported. Standard anemometer height: 10 m.
key_facts: Measured at 10 m standard height using cup anemometer and wind vane
Wind direction: FROM which wind blows; True North in METARs and observations
ATC/ATIS: Magnetic North for wind direction to pilots
Mean speed: 10-minute average; gusts: peak values in that period
Gusts reported separately when they exceed mean by 10+ kt
AWS at aerodromes: continuous measurement; multiple sensors along long runways

syllabus_desc: Describe how wind is encoded and reported in aviation weather products
concept: Wind is encoded in aviation weather products using a standard format. In METARs and TAFs, wind is reported as a 5–7 character group: direction in degrees True (3 digits), speed in knots (2 digits), and gust if applicable (G prefix, 2 digits), followed by KT. For example: 24015G28KT = wind from 240 degrees True at 15 kt, gusting to 28 kt. Variable wind direction below 3 kt: VRB02KT. Variable direction at higher speeds: 240V310 (variable between 240 and 310 degrees). Calm wind: 00000KT. Wind in TAFs covers the period in each time group. On METAR, wind may be supplemented by the prevailing visibility and present weather. On ATIS, wind is given in magnetic degrees at the current intensity. In area forecasts (ARFOR) and SIGWX charts, wind at multiple levels is shown. Upper wind forecasts use the GRID chart format as previously described. Wind in PIREPs follows the standard format (e.g. UA /OV /TM /FLTLVL /SK /WX /TA /WV where WV is wind). Accurate wind interpretation is essential for fuel planning, groundspeed calculation, and crosswind assessment.
exam_tip: METAR wind format: DDDSSKT or DDDSSGGKT (D = direction True, SS = speed, GG = gust). VRB = variable direction. ATIS gives direction in Magnetic. Remember the True vs Magnetic distinction between METARs and ATC.
key_facts: METAR/TAF format: DDDSSKT or DDDSSGGKT — direction True, speed, gust in knots
e.g. 24015G28KT = 240 degrees True, 15 kt, gusting 28 kt
VRBxxKT = variable direction; 00000KT = calm
Variable direction with speed: 240V310 (direction varying between 240 and 310)
ATIS and ATC: Magnetic direction — always distinguish from METAR True direction
Upper wind forecasts: GRID charts with barbs at each level

syllabus_desc: Describe wind forecasting for aviation purposes
concept: Wind forecasting for aviation uses a combination of numerical weather prediction (NWP) models, upper air analysis, and human interpretation. NWP models solve the equations of atmospheric dynamics on a three-dimensional grid to produce forecasts of wind (speed and direction) at all levels and all times. MetService NZ uses the global models (ECMWF, UKMET, GFS) and its own NZ regional model. Upper wind forecasts are produced as GRID charts covering key pressure levels. Surface wind forecasts appear in TAFs, ARFORs, and wind forecasts for aerodromes. TAF wind forecasts include the expected surface wind for each time group. Forecast confidence decreases with time — TAF validity is typically 24–30 hours, ARFOR is 12–24 hours, and GRID charts may cover 6–72 hours ahead. Wind forecast verification is an important part of the flight planning process — checking actual observations and PIREPs against the forecast winds at your altitude. Any significant discrepancy should be noted and ATC advised, and fuel reserves should accommodate forecast uncertainty for longer flights.
exam_tip: NWP models generate wind forecasts at all levels — these feed GRID charts, TAFs, and ARFORs. Forecast confidence reduces with time. Check PIREPs against forecast winds on longer flights and advise ATC of major discrepancies.
key_facts: NWP models (ECMWF, UKMET, GFS + NZ regional) generate all-level wind forecasts
Upper wind forecasts: GRID charts at key pressure levels
Surface wind forecasts: TAFs (24–30 hr), ARFORs (12–24 hr)
Forecast accuracy degrades with time — uncertainty builds beyond 24 hours
Check actual winds (PIREPs, ATIS reports) against forecast during flight
Significant forecast error: advise ATC and review fuel reserves

syllabus_desc: Define the transition altitude.
concept: The transition altitude is the altitude below which you use QNH as your altimeter reference. In NZ, this is typically 10,000 feet MSL. When you're climbing through the transition altitude and reaching the transition level, you switch your altimeter setting from QNH to flight level mode. The specific transition altitude for your region is given in the AIP and ATIS. Some aerodromes have non-standard transition altitudes (lower in mountainous areas, for example), so always check before flight.
exam_tip: Transition altitude = altitude below which you use QNH. In NZ, typically 10,000 feet. Below this, set QNH. Switch to flight levels when transitioning upward.
key_facts: Transition altitude = lower boundary of the transition layer
Below this, use QNH for altimetry
NZ standard: 10,000 feet MSL
Non-standard values at some aerodromes (especially mountainous)
Check AIP and ATIS for local value
When climbing through it, you're nearing the transition level

syllabus_desc: Define the transition level.
concept: The transition level is the flight level above which you use flight levels as your altimeter reference. In NZ, this is typically FL 100. When you're climbing through the transition level, you stop using QNH and switch to flying by flight level. Descending back through it, you switch from flight levels back to QNH. The specific transition level for your region is given in the AIP. The height of the transition level (in actual altitude feet) varies depending on atmospheric pressure, but it's always referred to by its flight level designation.
exam_tip: Transition level = flight level above which you use flight levels. In NZ, typically FL 100. Above this, fly by flight level.
key_facts: Transition level = upper boundary of the transition layer
Above this, use flight levels for altimetry
NZ standard: FL 100
Actual altitude of FL 100 varies with atmospheric pressure
Check AIP for local value (may be non-standard)
When descending through it, you transition back to QNH

syllabus_desc: Define the exceptions that apply to transition altitude and transition level above.
concept: Standard transition altitude (10,000 feet) and transition level (FL 100) apply in most of NZ, but exceptions exist. Some aerodromes, particularly in mountainous terrain, have non-standard transition altitudes. For example, an aerodrome at high elevation might have a higher transition altitude to avoid the complexity of dense traffic in the transition layer. Similarly, some regions have non-standard transition levels. Before any flight involving transitions, always check the local AIP information and ATIS to confirm the transition altitude and level for your specific area. Flying to an unfamiliar region? Check the AIP before you go.
exam_tip: Exceptions: non-standard transition altitudes and levels apply at some aerodromes. Always check AIP and ATIS for local values.
key_facts: Standard NZ: 10,000 ft transition altitude, FL 100 transition level
Exceptions at some aerodromes (especially mountainous)
Non-standard values in AIP, Special Category aerodromes, and some controlled airspace
Always check AIP and ATIS before flight
Never assume standard values apply everywhere
Flying unfamiliar routes? Check transition procedures in advance

syllabus_desc: Explain why transition layers in other countries are found at lower or higher levels in the atmosphere.
concept: Different countries set their transition altitudes at different levels based on airspace organization, air traffic density, and geography. Some countries use FL 050 or lower; others use FL 100 or higher. The reason is mainly practical: the transition altitude divides controlled airspace (where aircraft fly on a common pressure reference for separation) from uncontrolled or less structured airspace (where individual pressure settings are used). In countries with very high air traffic density near lower levels, the transition altitude might be set higher to accommodate more aircraft below it. In countries where high-altitude traffic is sparse, it might be lower. NZ's choice of 10,000 feet / FL 100 is fairly typical for our geography and traffic patterns. Understanding why other countries differ helps you when flying internationally -- you need to learn the new transition rules for each country.
exam_tip: Countries set transition levels based on traffic density and airspace organization. Higher traffic density often means higher transition level. NZ: 10,000 ft/FL 100 is standard.
key_facts: Transition altitude varies by country (e.g., FL 050 in some, FL 100-150 in others)
Set based on airspace organization and traffic density
Higher traffic density often = higher transition level
Geographic and climatic factors may also influence choice
NZ: 10,000 ft transition altitude, FL 100 transition level
When flying internationally, always check local transition rules

syllabus_desc: Define elevation.
concept: Elevation is the height of the terrain above mean sea level. When you see a chart with aerodrome elevations marked, or when AIP describes terrain heights, those are elevations. Your aircraft's altitude above terrain is the difference between your altitude and the terrain elevation (altitude minus elevation equals height above ground level, AGL). Understanding elevation is crucial for maintaining terrain clearance. If terrain is at 5,000 feet elevation and your altimeter reads 7,000 feet (on QNH), you have 2,000 feet AGL. This is why checking terrain elevations along your route is essential for IFR flight.
exam_tip: Elevation = terrain height above MSL. Altitude minus elevation = height AGL. Critical for terrain clearance calculations.
key_facts: Elevation = height of terrain above mean sea level
Found on charts and in AIP
Altitude minus elevation = height above ground level (AGL)
Critical for calculating terrain clearance
Must check terrain elevations along route
Low-level flying in mountains requires vigilant elevation awareness

syllabus_desc: Explain what happens to an aircraft's flight profile when the altimeter sub-scale is not reset during flights between areas with differing MSL pressures.
concept: If you set your altimeter to QNH at one aerodrome (say, QNH 1013 hPa) and then fly to another region where the QNH is different (say, 1018 hPa), without resetting the altimeter, your indicated altitude will be wrong. The altimeter reads pressure, and if the pressure has changed by 5 hPa, your altimeter will read approximately 500 feet lower than it should (because higher pressure at the new location pushes the altimeter needle down). This is dangerous -- you might think you're at a safe altitude when you're actually 500 feet lower and risking terrain collision. This is why you must update QNH every time you fly into a region with a different QNH. The rule of thumb is to check and update QNH whenever you cross into a new QNH region (marked on the charts) or obtain a new ATIS/METAR showing a different QNH.
exam_tip: Not resetting QNH between regions = altitude error. 1 hPa difference ≈ 30 feet error. DANGEROUS. Always update QNH when crossing QNH regions.
key_facts: Altimeter reads pressure, not true altitude
Different QNH at different locations means different altimeter reading at same location
Not resetting = serious altitude error (dangerous for terrain clearance)
1 hPa error ≈ 30 feet altitude error
Must reset QNH when entering new QNH region
Check QNH from ATIS, METAR, or AIP before crossing regions

syllabus_desc: Describe localised pressure changes associated with lee troughs.
concept: A lee trough is an area of localised low pressure that forms in the lee (downwind side) of a mountain range. As wind flows over a mountain, it's forced to rise on the windward side, then descends rapidly on the lee side. This rapid descent warms the air and increases pressure initially, but the convergence of flow on the lee side can create a pressure trough -- a localised area of lower pressure than surrounding areas. Lee troughs are common in NZ (leeward side of the Southern Alps during westerly flow, for example). They can trigger thunderstorm development and are often shown on SIGWX charts. Understanding lee troughs helps you predict weather on the lee side of mountain ranges.
exam_tip: Lee trough = localised low pressure area on leeward side of mountains. Forms due to convergence in lee-side flow. Common in NZ, can trigger storms.
key_facts: Lee trough = localised low pressure on leeward side of mountains
Forms when descending air converges on lee side
Common in NZ: lee side of Southern Alps during westerlies
Can trigger thunderstorm development
Shown on SIGWX charts
Predict lee trough activity when strong flow crosses mountain ranges

syllabus_desc: Describe localised pressure changes associated with thermal (or 'heat') lows.
concept: A thermal low is a localised area of low pressure created by intense surface heating in a small area. The heated air rises, creating a local area of low pressure beneath it. Thermal lows are common in inland areas on hot, sunny days and are particularly pronounced over dark surfaces (asphalt, dark soil) that absorb heat efficiently. In NZ, thermal lows often develop over the Canterbury Plains on hot afternoons. They can trigger afternoon thunderstorm development and can create local wind convergence patterns. Thermal lows are typically weak and transient -- they dissipate after sunset when the heat source disappears. They're not usually shown on synoptic charts because they're too localised, but they're important for understanding local afternoon weather development.
exam_tip: Thermal low = localised low pressure from intense surface heating. Develops inland on hot days. Can trigger afternoon thunderstorms. Dissipates after sunset.
key_facts: Thermal low = localised low pressure from surface heating
Develops on hot, sunny days over dark surfaces
Common in inland areas (e.g., Canterbury Plains in NZ)
Can trigger afternoon thunderstorm development
Creates local wind convergence patterns
Typically dissipates after sunset (transient feature)

syllabus_desc: Describe localised pressure changes associated with thunderstorms.
concept: Thunderstorms create dramatic localised pressure changes. The vigorous updrafts in a thunderstorm draw air upward, creating a localised area of low pressure at the surface (the pressure trough). The spreading anvil aloft and cooled air descending on the storm's periphery create high pressure aloft and lower pressure at the surface in the cold pool. As the storm weakens, the cold pool can persist and trigger new convection or lift adjacent air. Storm outflows (gust fronts) can show up as pressure jumps on surface observations. From a pilot's perspective, the key is that pressure changes associated with storms can be rapid and significant, and they're often associated with severe turbulence and wind shifts.
exam_tip: Thunderstorms create localised pressure troughs at the surface and pressure jumps at outflow boundaries. Pressure changes can be rapid and associated with severe turbulence.
key_facts: Thunderstorms create localised low pressure beneath updrafts
Pressure trough visible at surface
Outflow boundaries show pressure jumps
Cold pool aloft creates pressure pattern aloft
Pressure changes often rapid and significant
Associated with severe wind shear and turbulence

syllabus_desc: Describe diurnal pressure variations.
concept: Atmospheric pressure has a predictable daily (diurnal) variation in many locations. Pressure tends to be lowest in the early morning (around sunrise) and highest in late afternoon. This is driven by the diurnal heating cycle -- the atmosphere warms during the day (increasing volume and pressure aloft), then cools at night. The amplitude of this variation is typically 2-4 hPa in many locations. Over oceans, the variation is less pronounced. In continental regions, it can be stronger. Understanding diurnal pressure variation is important because it affects density altitude (higher afternoon pressure temperature means worse performance) and because unusual absence of the expected diurnal pattern can indicate a weather change approaching.
exam_tip: Diurnal pressure variation: pressure lowest at sunrise, highest in late afternoon. Typically 2-4 hPa amplitude. Related to heating cycle. Larger over land than ocean.
key_facts: Diurnal = daily, recurring every 24 hours
Pressure lowest around sunrise
Pressure highest in late afternoon
Amplitude typically 2-4 hPa
Driven by daytime heating and nighttime cooling
More pronounced over land, less over ocean

syllabus_desc: State the latitudes where diurnal pressure variation is most significant.
concept: Diurnal pressure variation is most pronounced in tropical and subtropical latitudes (roughly 0-30 degrees latitude either side of the equator). NZ at 35-47 degrees S is in the mid-latitude zone where diurnal variation is less pronounced than in the tropics. The reason is that at low latitudes, there's little seasonal variation in day length and the air is often moist and unstable, making convective processes (which respond strongly to diurnal heating) dominant. At higher latitudes, weather is more dominated by synoptic-scale systems (fronts, depressions) that overwhelm the diurnal signal. Between latitudes 0 and 30 degrees, the diurnal pressure wave is particularly strong and well-marked.
exam_tip: Diurnal pressure variation most significant in tropics and subtropics (0-30 degrees latitude). Less pronounced at NZ latitudes (35-47 degrees S).
key_facts: Most significant in tropical and subtropical latitudes (0-30 degrees)
Less pronounced at higher latitudes (NZ is 35-47 degrees S)
Driven by convective processes which are strong at low latitudes
At high latitudes, synoptic systems (fronts, lows) dominate the pressure pattern
Equatorial regions show the strongest diurnal pressure wave
NZ shows moderate diurnal variation

syllabus_desc: Explain how pressure changes affect air density.
concept: Higher pressure = higher density. At the same temperature, air at higher pressure is denser because molecules are more tightly packed. This is why density altitude increases with pressure altitude -- you're starting from an already thinner atmosphere. For aircraft performance, this matters enormously: the same weight of air supports less lift at lower density. On a standard day at sea level, the air is at standard density (1.225 kg/m³). At 5,000 feet, the air is thinner (lower density) and your aircraft's performance is reduced. But on a day when the atmospheric pressure is lower than standard, the air at any given altitude is even thinner, and performance is further reduced.
exam_tip: Higher pressure = denser air = better aircraft performance. Lower pressure = thinner air = worse performance. All else equal.
key_facts: Pressure and density are directly related at constant temperature
Higher pressure = denser air (molecules packed tighter)
Lower pressure = less dense air (molecules spread out)
Density affects lift production (denser air = more lift)
Aircraft performance improves with higher density (higher pressure)
Aircraft performance decreases with lower density (lower pressure)

syllabus_desc: Explain how temperature changes affect air density.
concept: Higher temperature = lower density. At the same pressure, warmer air is less dense because molecules are moving faster and spread out more. This is why hot summer days have worse aircraft performance than cool mornings -- even though the pressure might be the same, the warm air is less dense. For density altitude calculations, both temperature and pressure matter. A hot day at sea level might have worse performance than a cool day at altitude, purely due to density differences. This is why we calculate density altitude (not just pressure altitude) for performance planning.
exam_tip: Higher temperature = lower density = worse performance. Lower temperature = higher density = better performance. All else equal.
key_facts: Temperature and density are inversely related
Hotter air is less dense (molecules spread out)
Cooler air is denser (molecules move slower, closer together)
Affects aircraft performance: cool days = better performance
Hot days = worse performance, even at same pressure altitude
Density altitude combines both pressure and temperature effects

syllabus_desc: Explain how altitude changes affect air density.
concept: Higher altitude = lower density. This is the most obvious one.
