Density decreases approximately exponentially with altitude -- it drops faster at lower altitudes and slower at higher altitudes. At sea level, the air is densest. At 18,000 feet, the air is half as dense. At 35,000 feet (typical high-altitude cruise), the air is only about 25% as dense as sea level. For aircraft performance, this is why your aircraft can't climb forever -- eventually, the air becomes so thin that wings can't generate enough lift. This is why the service ceiling (maximum altitude) exists.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = lower density. Density decreases exponentially with altitude. This is why aircraft can't climb to unlimited altitude.
key_facts: Density decreases with altitude
Decreases approximately exponentially
Sea level: 100% standard density
18,000 ft: ~50% standard density
35,000 ft: ~25% standard density
Service ceiling = altitude where air is too thin for further climb

syllabus_desc: Explain how moisture content of the air affects air density.
concept: This seems counterintuitive: moist air is actually less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. This is because water vapour (H₂O) has a lower molecular weight than the nitrogen and oxygen that make up dry air. So when you add water vapour to air, you're replacing some of the heavier nitrogen/oxygen molecules with lighter water vapour molecules, making the air less dense. The effect is small (a few percent at most) but it's real. In practical terms, a hot, humid day has worse aircraft performance than a hot, dry day because the air is less dense. This is one reason why performance on a humid day at high temperature is particularly poor.
exam_tip: Moist air is less dense than dry air. Water vapour is lighter than N₂/O₂. Humid days = slightly worse performance than dry days.
key_facts: Moisture content affects air density
Moist air is slightly less dense than dry air
Water vapour (H₂O) lighter than N₂ and O₂
Effect is small but measurable
Hot, humid day = worse performance than hot, dry day
One factor in density altitude calculations

syllabus_desc: Define density altitude.
concept: Density altitude is the altitude at which the air density equals the standard atmospheric density for that altitude. In other words, it's what altitude the aircraft 'feels' like it's flying at, based on the actual air density. On a standard day, density altitude equals pressure altitude. But on a hot day or at low pressure, the air is thinner, so the aircraft performs as if it's at a higher altitude than it really is -- the density altitude is higher than the pressure altitude. On a cold day or at high pressure, the aircraft performs as if it's at a lower altitude -- density altitude is lower. For aircraft performance planning, you always use density altitude, not pressure altitude. A small aircraft might not be able to take off from a high-elevation hot-day aerodrome despite the pressure altitude being only, say, 6,000 feet, because the density altitude is 9,000 feet and the aircraft doesn't have enough performance at that effective altitude.
exam_tip: Density altitude = the altitude at which air density equals standard density. What altitude the aircraft 'feels' like it's at. Use this for performance planning, not pressure altitude.
key_facts: Density altitude = effective altitude based on air density
Standard day: density altitude = pressure altitude
Hot day or low pressure: density altitude > pressure altitude (feels higher)
Cold day or high pressure: density altitude < pressure altitude (feels lower)
Use for all aircraft performance calculations
High density altitude limits aircraft performance dramatically

syllabus_desc: Calculate density altitude.
concept: Density altitude is calculated from pressure altitude and outside air temperature (OAT). The formula is: DA = pressure altitude + (120 × (OAT - ISA temp at that altitude)). For example, if your pressure altitude is 5,000 feet and the outside air temperature is 25 degrees C (ISA at 5,000 ft is 5.1 degrees C, so the deviation is +19.9), then DA = 5,000 + (120 × 19.9) = 5,000 + 2,388 = 7,388 feet. Your aircraft will perform as if it's at 7,388 feet, not 5,000 feet. Most aircraft have density altitude performance tables for standard conditions, so you look up performance at the calculated density altitude. You should practice this calculation until you can do it in your head -- it's critical for safe flight, especially at high-elevation hot-day aerodromes.
exam_tip: DA = pressure altitude + (120 × temp deviation from ISA). Every degree above ISA costs about 120 feet of density altitude. Practice until automatic.
key_facts: DA = PA + (120 × (OAT - ISA temp))
DA = pressure altitude + (120 feet per degree C above ISA)
High temperature = high DA (worse performance)
Low temperature = low DA (better performance)
Use calculated DA for all performance tables
At high-elevation hot-day aerodromes, DA can exceed pressure altitude significantly

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces that generate wind: pressure gradient force, Coriolis force, and friction.
concept: Wind is air moving from high pressure to low pressure -- but it doesn't blow straight from H to L because two other forces get involved. The pressure gradient force (PGF) is the driving force: it pushes air from high to low pressure, and the closer the isobars, the stronger the push. Coriolis force is an apparent force caused by the earth's rotation -- in the Southern Hemisphere, it deflects moving air to the left. Coriolis is zero at the equator and maximum at the poles, and it's proportional to wind speed (faster wind = more deflection). When PGF and Coriolis balance, you get the geostrophic wind -- air flowing parallel to the isobars with low pressure to the right (Southern Hemisphere). But near the surface, friction slows the wind down, which reduces Coriolis deflection, causing the wind to blow across the isobars at an angle toward low pressure. Over land, surface wind is typically about 30 degrees backed from the geostrophic wind and roughly 50-60% of its speed. Over sea, it's about 10-15 degrees backed and 70-80% speed (less friction).
exam_tip: Three forces: PGF (drives wind), Coriolis (deflects left in SH), friction (slows near surface). Geostrophic wind blows parallel to isobars. Surface wind backs ~30 degrees over land, ~10-15 degrees over sea.
key_facts: Pressure gradient force: drives air from high to low pressure
Coriolis force: deflects moving air to the LEFT in the Southern Hemisphere
Coriolis is zero at equator, maximum at poles, proportional to wind speed
Geostrophic wind: PGF and Coriolis balanced, flows parallel to isobars
Friction backs surface wind ~30 degrees and reduces speed to ~50-60% over land
Over sea: ~10-15 degrees backed, ~70-80% of geostrophic speed
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between isobar spacing, pressure gradient, and wind speed.
concept: Closely spaced isobars mean a steep pressure gradient and strong winds. Widely spaced isobars mean a weak gradient and light winds. It's that straightforward on a weather map -- and it's the first thing you should look at when assessing wind conditions. On NZ weather maps, isobars are typically drawn at 4 hPa intervals. If you see four isobars packed into a short distance, you're looking at 16 hPa of pressure change over that distance -- that's a strong gradient and strong winds. The geostrophic wind equation shows this mathematically, but in practice you just read the map: tight isobars = windy. Around curved isobars (lows and highs), the gradient wind differs from the geostrophic wind because centripetal acceleration is involved. Around a low, the gradient wind is less than geostrophic (subgeostrophic), and around a high, it's greater than geostrophic (supergeostrophic). This is a CPL-level distinction that helps explain why weather maps sometimes show stronger winds around highs than you'd expect from the isobar spacing alone.
exam_tip: Tight isobars = strong winds. Around curved isobars: gradient wind is subgeostrophic around lows, supergeostrophic around highs.
key_facts: Closely spaced isobars = steep pressure gradient = strong winds
Widely spaced isobars = weak gradient = light winds
NZ weather maps: isobars typically drawn at 4 hPa intervals
Gradient wind around a low: subgeostrophic (less than geostrophic)
Gradient wind around a high: supergeostrophic (more than geostrophic)
Centripetal acceleration modifies the force balance in curved flow

syllabus_desc: State the changes in the magnitude of Coriolis force with latitude.
concept: The Coriolis force varies with latitude. At the equator, it's zero -- there's no Coriolis effect. As you move toward the poles, the Coriolis force increases. At the poles, it reaches its maximum value. This is why winds at the equator blow more easterly (less deflection) while mid-latitude winds are more westerly (more deflection). The mathematical expression is F = 2Ω sin(φ) where Ω is Earth's rotation rate and φ is latitude. The sine term means no effect at the equator (sin 0 = 0) and maximum effect at the poles (sin 90° = 1).
exam_tip: Coriolis force = zero at equator, increases toward poles, maximum at poles. Explains why trade winds are easterly, mid-latitude winds westerly.
key_facts: Coriolis force = 0 at equator
Coriolis force increases toward poles
Maximum Coriolis force at the poles
Force proportional to sin(latitude)
Equatorial winds less deflected (blow easterly)
Polar winds strongly deflected (westerly)

syllabus_desc: State the relationship between wind speed and Coriolis force.
concept: The Coriolis force is proportional to wind speed. Faster winds experience greater Coriolis deflection, slower winds less. A light breeze experiences less deflection than a gale. This is why geostrophic winds (in balance between pressure gradient and Coriolis) blow stronger when isobars are closer together (steeper pressure gradient) -- the stronger wind is needed to balance the larger Coriolis force from that steeper gradient. This relationship is fundamental to understanding how wind speed and direction relate to the pressure pattern.
exam_tip: Coriolis force proportional to wind speed. Faster wind = greater deflection. Slower wind = less deflection.
key_facts: Coriolis force increases with wind speed
F = 2Ω sin(φ) × V where V is wind speed
Geostrophic balance requires stronger wind for steeper pressure gradient
Light winds = small Coriolis effect
Strong winds = large Coriolis effect
Explains pressure-wind-direction relationships

syllabus_desc: State the direction of the Coriolis force relative to the wind direction in the Southern Hemisphere.
concept: In the Southern Hemisphere, the Coriolis force always acts to the right of the wind vector (from the wind's perspective). If wind is blowing north, Coriolis deflects it to the right (eastward). If wind is blowing east, Coriolis deflects it to the right (southward). This right-hand deflection is why low-pressure systems spiral clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere -- Coriolis deflects all winds to the right, creating the clockwise rotation.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere: Coriolis force is always to the right of wind direction. This right-hand deflection creates clockwise wind circulation around lows.
key_facts: Coriolis force acts 90 degrees to the right of wind (Southern Hemisphere)
Northward wind → deflected eastward
Eastward wind → deflected southward
Southward wind → deflected westward
Westward wind → deflected northward
Creates clockwise circulation around lows, anticlockwise around highs

syllabus_desc: State Buys Ballot's Law and explain its application in the Southern Hemisphere.
concept: Buys Ballot's Law states: stand with your back to the wind and low pressure is to your RIGHT in the Southern Hemisphere (left in the Northern Hemisphere). It's a practical tool for estimating where pressure systems are relative to your position when you're airborne. At CPL level, you should be able to apply this fluently: if you're flying and the wind is from the west (hitting your tail on an eastbound heading), low pressure is to your right -- that's to the south. If the wind veers from west to northwest during your flight, the low is moving or the pressure pattern is changing. Buys Ballot's Law applies best at the geostrophic level (above the friction layer) because surface friction causes the wind to cross isobars. Near the surface, low pressure isn't directly to your right but slightly ahead and to the right. In practice, this law helps you maintain situational awareness of the pressure pattern during flight, especially if your weather briefing information is ageing or you're outside radio range for updated QNH.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere: stand with back to wind, low pressure is to your RIGHT. This works best above the friction layer. Near the surface, low pressure is slightly ahead and to the right.
key_facts: Buys Ballot's Law: back to wind, low pressure to your RIGHT (Southern Hemisphere)
Northern Hemisphere: opposite -- low pressure to your LEFT
Works best above the friction layer (geostrophic wind level)
Near surface: friction modifies it -- low is slightly ahead and to the right
Practical use: estimate position of pressure systems relative to aircraft
Helps maintain weather situational awareness during flight
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Define gusts, squalls, veering, and backing, and describe the diurnal variation of surface wind.
concept: Gusts are rapid, short-lived increases in wind speed -- typically the wind fluctuates above and below its mean speed over periods of seconds. They're caused by turbulent eddies mixing faster air from above the friction layer down to the surface. A squall is a more dramatic event: a sudden increase in wind speed of at least 16 kt, reaching 22 kt or more, lasting at least one minute. Squalls are often associated with the passage of cumulonimbus or cold fronts. Veering is a clockwise wind direction change (e.g., 270 to 300 degrees); backing is anticlockwise (e.g., 270 to 240 degrees). In the Southern Hemisphere, wind veers with frontal passage and with altitude through the friction layer. The diurnal variation of surface wind is important for flight planning: during the day, solar heating creates convection that mixes faster upper-level air down to the surface, increasing surface wind speed and making it gusty. After sunset, convection dies off, the air stratifies, and surface wind decreases and steadies. This is why early morning is often calm (good for fog formation) and afternoon is windiest (good for thermal turbulence).
exam_tip: Gusts = short-lived speed fluctuations. Squall = sudden increase of 16+ kt, reaching 22+ kt, lasting 1+ minute. Veering = clockwise, backing = anticlockwise. Surface wind strongest in afternoon, calmest near dawn.
key_facts: Gusts: rapid short-lived wind speed increases (seconds), caused by turbulent mixing
Squall: sudden increase >= 16 kt, reaching >= 22 kt, lasting >= 1 minute
Veering: clockwise wind direction change. Backing: anticlockwise
Wind veers with frontal passage and with altitude (SH)
Daytime: convection mixes upper air down, surface wind increases and gusts
Night: convection stops, surface wind decreases and steadies
source: AFT ATPL Meteorology, AFT CPL Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the gradient wind in the Southern Hemisphere with respect to anticyclonically curved isobars.
concept: Around anticyclonically curved isobars (a high-pressure system's outer circulation), the wind blows anticlockwise (Southern Hemisphere), with the pressure gradient force pointing inward (toward lower pressure) and the Coriolis force pointing outward (the wind pushes the Coriolis force toward the centre). The wind speed is determined by the balance between these forces. Anticyclonically curved isobars produce a balance where wind is weaker than in the geostrophic case because the centrifugal acceleration is inward (adding to Coriolis), so less wind is needed to balance the pressure gradient.
exam_tip: Anticyclonic isobars: wind weaker than geostrophic equivalent. Pressure gradient and Coriolis balanced with centrifugal effects.
key_facts: Anticyclonically curved isobars: centre of curve is high pressure
Wind weaker than geostrophic wind at same pressure gradient
Centrifugal acceleration points inward (toward centre of curve)
Pressure gradient pushes outward, Coriolis pushes inward
Balance includes centrifugal effect, reducing required wind speed

syllabus_desc: Describe the gradient wind in the Southern Hemisphere with respect to cyclonically curved isobars.
concept: Around cyclonically curved isobars (a low-pressure system's outer circulation), the wind blows clockwise (Southern Hemisphere), with the pressure gradient force pointing inward (toward the low's centre) and the Coriolis force pointing outward. The centrifugal acceleration points outward (away from the centre). The wind is stronger than in the geostrophic case because the centrifugal acceleration is outward (opposing Coriolis), so more wind is needed to balance the pressure gradient. In extreme cases (very tight curvature, very low pressure), the centrifugal effect becomes so large that the wind speed is limited by the balance of centrifugal and pressure forces, and Coriolis plays less of a role.
exam_tip: Cyclonic isobars: wind stronger than geostrophic equivalent. Centrifugal acceleration points outward, opposing Coriolis.
key_facts: Cyclonically curved isobars: centre of curve is low pressure
Wind stronger than geostrophic wind at same pressure gradient
Centrifugal acceleration points outward (away from centre)
Pressure gradient points inward, Coriolis outward, centrifugal outward
Balance requires stronger wind to overcome centrifugal effect

syllabus_desc: Describe how to determine approximate wind direction from surface observations: ripples on water, wind lanes, smoke, and windsocks.
concept: When you're airborne and need to estimate wind direction without instruments, these visual cues are invaluable. Ripples on water: small ripples align perpendicular to the wind direction, and wind travels from the rougher side toward the smoother side. Wind lanes (dark streaks on water) align with the wind direction. Smoke drifts directly downwind -- a factory chimney or paddock burn gives you a clear wind direction indicator. Windsocks at aerodromes point downwind -- the angle tells you approximate speed (horizontal = 15+ kt, 45 degrees = about 7-8 kt, drooping = light and variable). On the ground, grass and crops lean downwind. For IFR pilots descending below cloud toward an uncontrolled aerodrome without AWIB, these visual clues help you determine the active runway. The limitation of windsocks is that they only show the wind at their exact location and height -- wind can vary significantly across an aerodrome due to terrain, buildings, and thermal effects. In gusty conditions, the windsock swings erratically, giving an indication of gusts but making it hard to determine the mean direction.
exam_tip: Ripples: perpendicular to wind, rougher side is upwind. Wind lanes: parallel to wind. Smoke: drifts downwind. Windsock: horizontal = 15+ kt. All show local conditions only.
key_facts: Ripples on water: perpendicular to wind, rougher side is upwind
Wind lanes (dark streaks on water): align parallel with wind direction
Smoke drifts directly downwind
Windsock: horizontal ~15+ kt, 45 degrees ~7-8 kt, drooping = light/variable
Windsock shows conditions at its location only -- may not represent whole aerodrome
Useful for determining active runway at uncontrolled aerodromes
source: AFT CPL Meteorology, AIPNZ GEN section

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over the sea.
concept: Over the sea, the surface is about as smooth as it gets -- low swell aside, there's very little to grab at the air and slow it down. Because friction is minimal, the surface wind stays close to the gradient wind in both speed and direction. In the Southern Hemisphere, friction causes the surface wind to back (turn anticlockwise) relative to the gradient wind. The physics: friction slows the wind, which reduces the Coriolis force. With less Coriolis pushing the wind to the left, the pressure gradient force (pointing toward the low, which is to the left of the wind in SH) gains the upper hand and nudges the wind back toward the low. Over the sea, this backing effect is small -- typically around 10 degrees. So if the 850 hPa gradient wind is from the northwest, the surface wind over the ocean will be from roughly west-northwest. Wind speeds over the sea are also only slightly reduced compared to the gradient wind -- maybe 10-15%. This is why ships and coastal stations often experience nearly the same wind that the 850 hPa chart shows, and why forecasters trust upper-level winds fairly well for maritime predictions.
exam_tip: Sea surface: backs ~10 degrees from gradient wind in SH. Small deflection because friction is low. Speed reduction is also minimal compared to gradient wind.
key_facts: Sea surface: smooth, low friction -- smallest deflection of any surface type
Surface wind backs approximately 10 degrees from the gradient wind (SH)
Speed reduction over the sea is minimal -- typically 10-15% below gradient wind
Backing occurs because friction reduces Coriolis force, allowing PGF to turn wind toward low
Maritime surface winds closely match gradient wind compared to land-based surfaces

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over flat to undulating ground.
concept: Flat to undulating terrain -- think the Canterbury Plains, the Waikato, or the Hawke's Bay flats -- gives friction a proper foothold. The irregular ground, crops, scattered trees, and buildings all drag at the lower layers of the atmosphere. In the Southern Hemisphere, this friction slows the wind more than over the sea, reducing Coriolis force further and allowing the pressure gradient to pull the wind back (anticlockwise) toward the low. Over flat to undulating ground, the backing from the gradient wind is typically 20 to 30 degrees. So a gradient westerly becomes a WSW or SW surface wind -- a shift that matters when you're working out which runway to use at an uncontrolled aerodrome without an AWIB. Wind speed is also noticeably reduced: surface winds over flat land are typically 30-50% lighter than the gradient wind above. This is why forecasts for cross-country legs often need adjustment when you're flying over land versus the coast -- the surface friction is doing real work on the low-level air.
exam_tip: Flat to undulating: backs 20-30 degrees from gradient wind in SH. Speed reduction ~30-50%. This is the 'standard' land surface case for exam purposes.
key_facts: Flat to undulating terrain: surface wind backs approximately 20-30 degrees from gradient wind (SH)
Wind speed reduced by approximately 30-50% compared to gradient wind
Greater deflection than over sea due to higher friction from uneven surface
Backing angle depends on surface roughness, stability, and gradient wind speed
Relevant for runway selection at uncontrolled aerodromes without AWIB

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over mountainous regions.
concept: Mountains don't just slow the wind -- they mangle it. Over mountainous terrain, friction effects are extreme and highly variable. The rough, irregular surface creates strong mechanical turbulence that extends through a deep layer, and the deflection of the surface wind from the gradient wind can be 30 to 45 degrees or more. In the Southern Hemisphere, this still shows up as a backing of the wind -- but the variability is enormous. You might see a gradient wind from the northwest, with surface winds at one valley station from the south-southwest and at another just 20 km away from the north-northwest, depending on valley orientation, funnelling effects, and local terrain features. Wind speeds in mountainous areas can be dramatically reduced in sheltered valleys but actually accelerated (sometimes dramatically) through gaps, passes, or over ridgelines due to Bernoulli effects. The nor'wester over the Southern Alps is a classic NZ example -- gradient winds from the northwest are compressed and accelerated as they come over the main divide, producing damaging winds on the lee side. Treat published wind forecasts for mountainous areas as guidance only -- the local effects are often the dominant factor.
exam_tip: Mountains: backs 30-45 degrees or more, but HIGHLY variable. Speed can be reduced in valleys but greatly accelerated through gaps and over ridges. Local effects dominate.
key_facts: Mountainous terrain: surface wind backs 30 degrees or more from gradient wind (SH) -- highly variable
Mechanical turbulence is intense and extends through a deep layer
Wind speed varies enormously: sheltered valleys can be calm while passes experience severe acceleration
Valley orientation, gap funnelling, and lee effects all override simple friction rules
NZ example: nor'wester accelerates dramatically on the lee side of the Southern Alps
Treat mountain wind forecasts as estimates -- local effects often dominate

syllabus_desc: Explain how atmospheric stability affects the depth of the friction layer.
concept: Atmospheric stability has a direct effect on how deep the friction layer grows. Here's the key idea: friction effects are transmitted upward through the atmosphere by turbulent mixing. If the atmosphere resists vertical movement (stable conditions), that mixing is suppressed and the friction layer stays shallow. If vertical motion is promoted (unstable conditions), turbulent eddies can carry momentum exchange up through a much greater depth. In stable conditions -- typical at night or over cold surfaces -- the friction layer might be only 300 to 500 metres deep. The air above that level is effectively decoupled from the surface, and the gradient wind flows largely undisturbed. This is why overnight winds can behave so differently to daytime winds, and why the low-level jet can develop at the top of a shallow stable layer. In unstable conditions -- a hot summer afternoon with strong surface heating, for example -- convective mixing can extend the friction layer up to 1,000 to 2,000 metres or more. Surface wind effects are felt through a much deeper layer, and the gradient wind at, say, 1,500 feet AGL may already be significantly influenced by surface drag. For NZ operations, a stable southerly pushing up from the south will often have a very defined friction layer boundary -- smooth on top, turbulent below.
exam_tip: Stable atmosphere = SHALLOW friction layer (300-500m). Unstable atmosphere = DEEP friction layer (1,000-2,000m+). Stability controls how far turbulent mixing can carry friction effects upward.
key_facts: Stable atmosphere: suppresses vertical mixing -- friction layer is shallow (~300-500m)
Unstable atmosphere: promotes convective mixing -- friction layer is deep (1,000-2,000m+)
Friction effects transmitted upward by turbulent mixing -- stability controls the depth of this mixing
At night (stable): shallow friction layer, gradient wind decoupled from surface relatively close to ground
By day (unstable): deep friction layer, surface effects felt through a much greater depth
Low-level jet can develop at top of a shallow stable friction layer -- gradient wind resumes above

syllabus_desc: Explain how wind strength affects the depth of the friction layer.
concept: Stronger winds create more mechanical turbulence -- the air is moving faster, so it hits surface irregularities harder and generates more vigorous eddies. These eddies carry momentum up through a greater depth, extending the friction layer. The relationship is fairly intuitive: stronger gradient wind = deeper friction layer. In light winds (5 kt gradient or less), the friction layer is typically shallow -- maybe 300 to 500 metres -- because mechanical turbulence is minimal. There's little energy to drive the vertical mixing. In strong winds (30+ kt gradient), the mechanical turbulence can be intense enough to mix friction effects up to 1,000 metres or more, even without any instability. For practical flying: in strong wind conditions, don't assume the gradient wind starts immediately above the circuit. The surface effects may extend much higher than you'd expect, meaning you could still be fighting a significant crosswind component from turbulence effects well into the climb. Strong gusty conditions near the surface during strong gradient winds are a direct result of this energetic mechanical mixing -- eddies formed near the ground are being thrown around by the vigorous flow above.
exam_tip: Stronger wind = DEEPER friction layer (more mechanical turbulence = greater mixing depth). Light wind = shallow friction layer. Wind strength controls turbulent energy available for vertical mixing.
key_facts: Strong winds create more mechanical turbulence, extending the friction layer deeper
Light winds (~5 kt): friction layer typically shallow (~300-500m) due to minimal turbulence
Strong winds (30+ kt): friction layer can extend to 1,000m or more from mechanical mixing alone
Stronger gradient wind = more energetic eddies = greater depth of friction influence
Gustiness at the surface in strong winds is a direct result of energetic mechanical mixing
Do not assume gradient wind begins just above circuit height in strong wind conditions

syllabus_desc: Explain how surface roughness affects the depth of the friction layer.
concept: The rougher the surface, the more drag it applies to the air flowing over it, and the more turbulence it generates. That turbulence is what carries friction effects upward. A smooth surface like the open ocean applies very little drag -- the friction layer stays shallow, maybe 300 to 500 metres. A rough surface like a dense urban area, a native forest, or a mountain range applies enormous drag, generating intense turbulence that can mix friction effects up to 1,000 metres or more. This is why the friction layer depth over a city is significantly deeper than over the surrounding farmland, and why the wind profile through the lower levels differs so much between urban and rural environments. For NZ operations: flying into Queenstown surrounded by mountains versus flying into Hamilton over flat farmland means very different friction layer profiles. The turbulence you encounter on approach to NZQN can be felt through a much deeper layer because the surface roughness is extreme -- the entire Southern Alps is acting as a giant roughness element feeding turbulence into the atmosphere. Surface roughness is also why models and charts showing gradient winds need careful interpretation near complex terrain -- the surface below is actively modifying the wind through the friction layer.
exam_tip: Rough surface (mountains, cities) = DEEPER friction layer. Smooth surface (sea, flat plains) = SHALLOW friction layer. Roughness determines how much drag and turbulence the surface generates.
key_facts: Smooth surfaces (sea, flat plains): low drag, shallow friction layer (~300-500m)
Rough surfaces (urban areas, forests, mountains): high drag, deep friction layer (1,000m+)
Surface roughness determines the intensity of mechanical turbulence and therefore mixing depth
Urban friction layers are deeper than rural because buildings act as roughness elements
NZQN example: mountainous surrounds create deep turbulent friction layer on approach
Gradient wind charts must be interpreted carefully near rough terrain -- surface drag modifies the wind through the friction layer

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the cause and characteristics of the foehn effect and nor'wester in New Zealand.
concept: The nor'wester is New Zealand's most notorious local wind — a classic foehn effect driven by the Southern Alps. Here's how it works: moist air from the Tasman Sea is forced to rise over the Main Divide. As it rises on the windward side, it cools at the saturated adiabatic lapse rate (~0.6°C/100m) and sheds moisture as precipitation. Once it crosses the divide and descends on the lee side (Canterbury Plains), it warms at the dry adiabatic lapse rate (~1°C/100m). Because it rained out its moisture on the way over, the descending air is warmer and significantly drier than the air that originally started rising. The result: hot, dry, gusty winds on the Canterbury Plains, often reaching 50-80 knots or more in severe events, with temperatures dramatically above what you'd expect for the season. Cloud-wise, a classic nor'wester brings a föhn wall — a dramatic line of cloud sitting over the Alps — and a clear, hazy sky over Canterbury. Christchurch temperatures can be 10-15°C above normal during a nor'wester. For aviation: turbulence on the lee side of the Alps can be severe to extreme, rotors may form below the Main Divide, and mountain wave extends well above the cloud tops.
exam_tip: Nor'wester = foehn effect. Moist air rises (saturated lapse rate, loses moisture), descends dry (dry lapse rate, gains heat). Lee side is warmer AND drier than original air. Föhn wall cloud sits over the Alps.
key_facts: Nor'wester: foehn wind on lee side of Southern Alps, affecting Canterbury Plains
Rising air cools at ~0.6°C/100m (saturated); descending air warms at ~1°C/100m (dry)
Net effect: lee side air is warmer and drier than the original windward air mass
Föhn wall: characteristic line of cloud over the Main Divide
Temperatures in Christchurch can be 10-15°C above normal during strong nor'westers
Aviation hazards: severe turbulence, mountain wave, rotors below Main Divide

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and causes of sea breezes in New Zealand.
concept: Sea breezes are a thermally-driven local circulation that develops along New Zealand's extensive coastline on days when synoptic winds are light. The mechanism is simple: land heats faster than the sea during the day, so the air over the land warms and rises, creating a low-pressure area at the surface. Cooler, denser sea air flows inland to fill the gap. In New Zealand, sea breezes typically develop 2-4 hours after sunrise, reach their peak in the early afternoon, and extend inland 20-40 km in moderate conditions. On exposed coasts like Hawke's Bay or the Wairarapa, they can push much further. Sea breezes are usually a welcome cooling influence on hot summer days — except for the pilots flying into them. The sea breeze front (the leading edge of the cool marine air) brings a sharp wind shift, often from calm or northerly to a southerly or southeasterly, and can produce a line of low cloud and poor visibility as marine air is pushed inland. In some NZ coastal locations, the sea breeze reinforces the prevailing wind direction, while in others it opposes it — producing a significant wind shift. Auckland, for example, often sees a sea breeze from the Waitemata converge with a breeze from the Manukau, creating a convergence zone across the isthmus.
exam_tip: Sea breeze: land heats faster, air rises, cool sea air flows inland. Develops mid-morning, peaks afternoon. Sea breeze FRONT brings wind shift, low cloud, reduced visibility. NZ example: Auckland isthmus convergence.
key_facts: Sea breeze driven by differential heating: land heats faster than sea during the day
Surface pressure lower over land → cool marine air flows inland
Typically develops 2-4 hours after sunrise; peaks early afternoon; dies near sunset
Extends 20-40 km inland in moderate conditions, further on exposed coasts
Sea breeze front: sharp wind shift, possible low cloud and reduced visibility
Auckland: sea breezes from Waitemata and Manukau can converge across the isthmus

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and causes of land breezes in New Zealand.
concept: Land breezes are the overnight counterpart to the sea breeze — they develop when the land cools faster than the sea after sunset, reversing the thermal circulation. The sea is now relatively warmer, so air rises over the water and cooler land air flows seaward. Land breezes are generally weaker than sea breezes, typically 5-15 knots, because the temperature difference between land and sea at night is smaller than the daytime differential. They usually begin a few hours after sunset and are strongest in the early morning hours before sunrise. In New Zealand, land breezes are most pronounced when synoptic pressure is high and gradient winds are light — particularly in winter anticyclonic conditions when overnight cooling is strong. On calm winter nights, a land breeze can make coastal harbours feel noticeably cooler, and the flow of cold, dense air from inland valleys can reinforce the land breeze substantially. For aviation, land breezes can cause early-morning fog to form just offshore, and the transition from land breeze to sea breeze during the morning can produce a period of calm with fog or low stratus before the sea breeze fills in. At aerodromes near the coast — like Paraparaumu, Tauranga, or Nelson — the land breeze cycle significantly affects early-morning conditions.
exam_tip: Land breeze: overnight, land cools faster than sea. Cool land air flows seaward. Weaker than sea breeze (5-15 kt). Strongest pre-dawn. Can produce offshore fog. Reverses to sea breeze mid-morning.
key_facts: Land breeze: overnight reversal — land cools faster than sea after sunset
Cool, dense land air flows seaward; air rises over the relatively warmer sea
Weaker than sea breeze: typically 5-15 knots
Strongest in early morning hours before sunrise
Can cause fog to form just offshore in stable, calm conditions
Transition from land to sea breeze mid-morning can produce calm, fog or low stratus

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and causes of valley and mountain winds in New Zealand.
concept: Valley winds are thermally-driven local circulations within mountain terrain, and New Zealand's topography produces them in abundance. During the day, valley slopes heat up faster than the free air at the same altitude, causing air to flow up the valley sides and up the valley itself toward higher ground — this upslope flow is called an anabatic wind, and it contributes to afternoon cumulus and thunderstorm development over mountain ranges. At night the process reverses: slopes cool rapidly by radiation, the air in contact with the slope becomes denser and drains downhill — katabatic flow. This cool, dense air pools in the lowest parts of valleys and basins, producing frost hollows and sometimes radiation fog overnight. In New Zealand, katabatic flows are especially significant in the Canterbury foothills, Central Otago, and mountain basins like the Mackenzie Basin. The Mackenzie Basin is notorious for extreme overnight cooling due to strong katabatic drainage from the surrounding ranges — minimum temperatures there can be 10-15°C colder than nearby coastal stations. At Tekapo, temperatures below -20°C have been recorded. The valley wind cycle is important for agricultural frost forecasting and for predicting overnight fog at inland aerodromes.
exam_tip: Day: anabatic (upslope, triggers afternoon convection). Night: katabatic (downslope, cold air drains to valley floors, causes frost and radiation fog). NZ example: Mackenzie Basin extreme cold pooling.
key_facts: Anabatic (daytime): slopes heat, air rises upslope — triggers afternoon Cu and Cb
Katabatic (overnight): slopes cool, dense air drains downhill into valleys and basins
Cold air pooling creates frost hollows and radiation fog in low-lying areas
Mackenzie Basin (NZ): extreme overnight cooling from katabatic drainage
Katabatic flows are strongest on clear, calm, anticyclonic nights
Important for frost forecasting and overnight fog prediction at inland aerodromes

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and causes of gap winds and channelling effects in New Zealand.
concept: Gap winds occur when pressure differences force air through a narrow passage — a mountain gap, a strait, or a valley aligned with the prevailing wind. The constriction accelerates the flow by the Bernoulli effect, producing winds at the gap exit that can be dramatically stronger than the surrounding open-area winds. New Zealand has several spectacular examples. Cook Strait is the most significant: it acts as a natural funnel between the North and South Islands, and northerly or southerly gradient winds are often accelerated through the Strait to 40-60 knots or more, even when conditions at open-sea stations are relatively moderate. The Kaipara Harbour entrance, the Manawatu Gorge, the gaps through the Kaimanawa and Rimutaka ranges — all are known for localised acceleration. For aviation, gap winds are a serious hazard because the acceleration is highly localised — you can fly through it in 30 seconds. ATIS from Wellington will often be reporting 25 knots while the Cook Strait crossing is 50+. Wind direction through a gap may also be constrained by the valley orientation regardless of the synoptic gradient direction, so the forecast wind may bear little resemblance to what you'll actually experience in the gap.
exam_tip: Gap winds: Bernoulli acceleration through constrictions. Wind can be much stronger at gap exit than in surrounding open areas. NZ example: Cook Strait — northerlies/southerlies accelerate to 40-60+ kt. Direction constrained by gap orientation.
key_facts: Gap wind: air accelerated through a narrow constriction by pressure difference (Bernoulli effect)
Wind at gap exit can far exceed the synoptic gradient wind speed
Cook Strait: NZ's most significant gap wind — accelerates N'ly and S'ly flows to 40-60+ kt
Other NZ examples: Manawatu Gorge, Kaipara entrance, Rimutaka Hill gaps
Wind direction constrained by gap/valley orientation — may differ significantly from forecast
Aviation hazard: intense, localised — ATIS may not reflect gap wind conditions

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and causes of mountain wave and rotor in New Zealand.
concept: Mountain wave (also called lee wave or standing wave) develops when stable air is forced over a mountain barrier. As the air descends on the lee side, its momentum carries it below its equilibrium level, setting up a wave pattern that can extend hundreds of kilometres downwind and up to the tropopause or above. New Zealand, with its sharp, high Main Divide perpendicular to the dominant westerly flow, produces some of the most dramatic mountain wave in the Southern Hemisphere. The Southern Alps are a world-class soaring location for exactly this reason. Wave is identified by lenticular clouds (lens-shaped, stationary-looking) that form at the wave crests, and a rotor zone beneath the first wave crest just downwind of the range. The rotor is a zone of intense, chaotic turbulence at low level — the air is literally rolling. Altitudes in the rotor can be 2,000-5,000 feet AMSL downwind of the Alps. Below the rotor, winds can be reversed (blowing back toward the mountains) even while the general flow is westerly at altitude. For VFR pilots, the lenticular clouds and föhn arch are your visual warning — but wave and rotor can occur with no cloud if the air is dry. Severe turbulence in the rotor zone has caused structural damage to aircraft. A PIREPs from aircraft transiting the South Island in strong nor'westerly conditions should be treated seriously.
exam_tip: Mountain wave: stable air over barrier creates lee waves extending far downwind and high up. ROTOR below first wave crest = severe turbulence. Lenticular cloud marks wave crests. Can occur clear-air (no cloud if dry). NZ: Southern Alps = world-class wave generator.
key_facts: Mountain wave: stable air over barrier creates oscillating lee waves downwind
Wave extends from surface to tropopause+ and hundreds of km downwind
Rotor: zone of severe, chaotic turbulence below first wave crest, lee side of range
Lenticular clouds: lens-shaped stationary clouds mark wave crests
Wave and rotor can occur in clear air if atmosphere is dry (no visible lenticulars)
Southern Alps produce world-class mountain wave due to steep, high, linear barrier

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of the trade winds in the South Pacific.
concept: The trade winds are the dominant surface-level winds in the tropical South Pacific, blowing from the subtropical high pressure belt (around 25-30°S) toward the Intertropical Convergence Zone near the equator. In the Southern Hemisphere, they blow from the southeast — these are the Southeast Trade Winds. They are remarkably consistent in direction and moderate in speed, typically 10-25 knots, and they are the most reliable wind system on Earth. The South Pacific trade wind belt extends from roughly 5°S to 25°S and is strongest in the eastern South Pacific and weaker in the west. The consistency of the trades made them indispensable to early Pacific navigators and still drives ocean current patterns today. For aviation in the tropical South Pacific — flights to Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, the Cook Islands — the Southeast Trades are the baseline wind for flight planning. They bring low-level cumulus and a characteristic trade wind inversion that acts as a lid on convection, producing fair-weather cumulus that tops out at 6,000-8,000 feet with clear skies above. The inversion weakens or breaks down during disturbances when convection can punch through to produce thunderstorm activity.
exam_tip: Southeast Trade Winds: blow from subtropical high toward ITCZ. Southern Hemisphere: SE direction. Reliable 10-25 kt. Trade wind inversion limits Cu to ~6,000-8,000 ft under normal conditions. Weakens during disturbances → convection breaks through.
key_facts: Southeast Trade Winds: surface flow from subtropical high (~25-30°S) toward ITCZ
Southern Hemisphere trades blow from the southeast — highly consistent
Typical strength: 10-25 knots; one of the most reliable wind systems on Earth
Trade wind inversion: acts as lid on convection, limits Cu tops to ~6,000-8,000 ft
Extends from roughly 5°S to 25°S across the South Pacific
Inversion weakens during disturbances, allowing deep convection and thunderstorms

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) and its effects on weather in the South Pacific.
concept: The Intertropical Convergence Zone — the ITCZ — is where the Northeast and Southeast Trade Winds converge near the equator. It's one of the most significant weather-generating systems on the planet. Where the trade winds meet, warm moist air is forced to rise, producing a continuous belt of deep convection, towering cumulonimbus, and heavy precipitation. In the South Pacific, the ITCZ is generally located between 5°N and 10°N but its position shifts seasonally — moving further north in the Northern Hemisphere summer and south (sometimes into the Southern Hemisphere) during the austral summer. The ITCZ produces the most intense and continuous thunderstorm activity on Earth. For aviation in the tropical Pacific, crossing the ITCZ is a significant weather challenge — it can be a relatively narrow band of severe convective activity or, during active phases, a broad zone hundreds of miles wide with embedded CBs, hail, severe turbulence, and lightning. Modern airliners cross the ITCZ using weather radar to thread through gaps. For light aircraft flying between NZ and Pacific Island nations, the ITCZ position and intensity is a critical flight planning consideration. When the ITCZ moves south during La Niña years, its effects can reach as far as Fiji and Tonga.
exam_tip: ITCZ: where N'E and S'E Trades converge near equator. Intense convection, CBs, heavy rain. Moves seasonally N/S. Major hazard for trans-equatorial flights. Can shift south toward Fiji/Tonga in La Niña years.
key_facts: ITCZ: convergence zone where NE and SE Trade Winds meet near the equator
Forced ascent of warm moist air creates intense, continuous deep convection
Produces world's most active thunderstorm activity
Position shifts seasonally — further north in NH summer, can reach Southern Hemisphere in austral summer
Major aviation hazard for trans-Pacific flights: CBs, turbulence, hail, lightning
La Niña can shift ITCZ southward, bringing increased convection to Fiji, Tonga, and beyond

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) and its influence on New Zealand weather.
concept: The South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) is a semi-permanent band of deep convection, cloud, and precipitation that extends diagonally from the Western Pacific warm pool (near Papua New Guinea) southeastward toward French Polynesia, roughly along the 10-20°S latitude band but on a northwest-to-southeast diagonal. It's a defining feature of South Pacific meteorology and doesn't behave like the ITCZ — it's more oriented, more variable, and responds strongly to El Niño/La Niña cycles. During La Niña, the SPCZ intensifies and shifts southwest, bringing more tropical rainfall to the Cook Islands, Tonga, and Fiji, and delivering more frequent deep northerly flows to New Zealand. During El Niño, the SPCZ weakens and shifts northeast toward French Polynesia, which tends to suppress rainfall in island nations and is associated with drought in the western South Pacific. For New Zealand aviation and flight planning to Pacific Island destinations, the SPCZ position and activity level is a major factor. A well-developed SPCZ can produce persistent cloud and rain across a wide swathe of the South Pacific, significantly affecting routing for light aircraft or island-hopping flights. SPCZ activity is a key factor in extended NZ weather forecasts.
exam_tip: SPCZ: diagonal band of convection from W Pacific SE toward French Polynesia. La Niña: intensifies, shifts SW → more rain to Fiji/Tonga/Cooks, more northerlies to NZ. El Niño: weakens, shifts NE → drought in W Pacific islands.
key_facts: SPCZ: semi-permanent diagonal band of deep convection from W Pacific southeastward
Extends roughly NW-SE from Papua New Guinea toward French Polynesia
La Niña: SPCZ intensifies, shifts SW — increased rainfall Fiji/Tonga/Cooks, more N'ly flow to NZ
El Niño: SPCZ weakens, shifts NE — drought in western South Pacific islands
Major factor in flight planning for Pacific Island destinations
Influences extended NZ weather forecasts through modification of upper flow patterns

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and effects of the Roaring Forties in the South Pacific.
concept: The Roaring Forties refer to the latitudinal band between approximately 40°S and 50°S, where strong westerly winds dominate throughout the year with little seasonal variation. This belt exists because there is almost no land in the Southern Hemisphere at these latitudes to impede the flow — the Southern Ocean is essentially an uninterrupted expanse around the globe. Depressions form and track rapidly eastward through this belt, driven by the polar front jet stream above. They are typically deeper, faster-moving, and more vigorous than comparable Northern Hemisphere systems because the uninterrupted fetch allows full development. New Zealand sits on the northern edge of this belt, which means the South Island, and particularly Southland and Fiordland, frequently feel the full force of these westerlies. From a meteorological standpoint, the Roaring Forties produce: persistent swell in the Southern Ocean (significant wave heights of 4-8 metres are common), severe turbulence at low and mid levels in strong systems, and the rapid succession of frontal systems that characterise southern NZ weather. Flights from the South Island to Antarctica must plan through this belt. The 'Furious Fifties' and 'Screaming Sixties' further south are even more extreme.
exam_tip: Roaring Forties: 40-50°S belt of persistent strong westerlies. Almost no land mass = unimpeded flow. Rapid deep depressions track E'ward. Southern Ocean swell 4-8m+ common. NZ South Island sits on northern edge — significant weather impact.
key_facts: Roaring Forties: 40-50°S belt of persistent strong westerlies, nearly unimpeded by land
Southern Ocean: uninterrupted fetch allows depressions to fully develop
Depressions deeper, faster, more vigorous than comparable NH systems
Southland and Fiordland feel the full force of Roaring Forties depressions
Southern Ocean swell: 4-8m+ significant wave height is common
Further south: Furious Fifties (50-60°S) and Screaming Sixties (60°S+) are even more extreme

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of tropical cyclones in the South Pacific and their effects on New Zealand.
concept: Tropical cyclones in the South Pacific typically form between 5°S and 20°S during the southern cyclone season (November to April), when sea surface temperatures exceed 26-27°C and the ITCZ provides the initial disturbance. They develop west of the International Date Line and track initially southwestward before curving southeastward — a path that can bring them toward New Zealand or the island nations. In the South Pacific, they are known as tropical cyclones (not hurricanes or typhoons). Characteristic features include: a defined eye with clear skies and light winds; an eyewall with the most intense convection, strongest winds, and heaviest rain; spiral rainbands extending outward; and a warm core structure throughout. As a tropical cyclone tracks south of about 25°S, it encounters cooler sea surface temperatures and increasing westerly shear, and it begins to undergo extratropical transition (ETT). During and after ETT, the storm's structure transforms — it may briefly intensify before weakening, and its area of gale-force winds can actually expand even as the centre weakens. ETT storms can affect New Zealand directly. Cyclone Gita (2018) underwent ETT and produced severe winds and rain over much of the South Island as it crossed New Zealand. For NZ aviation, ex-tropical cyclones undergoing ETT represent one of the more challenging forecast scenarios.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclones: November-April, form 5-20°S, need SST >26°C. Structure: eye, eyewall, spiral bands, warm core. Extratropical transition (ETT) as it moves south — storm transforms, gale area can EXPAND even as centre weakens. NZ example: ex-Cyclone Gita 2018.
key_facts: Tropical cyclone season S Pacific: November to April
Formation: SST >26-27°C, 5-20°S, west of International Date Line
Structure: clear eye, violent eyewall, spiral rainbands, warm core throughout
Track: initially SW then curves SE toward higher latitudes
Extratropical transition (ETT): begins ~25°S as SST cools and westerly shear increases
ETT: gale area can expand even as central pressure rises — NZ example: ex-Cyclone Gita 2018

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of El Nino and La Nina on New Zealand and South Pacific weather patterns.
concept: El Niño and La Niña are opposite phases of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) — a large-scale coupled ocean-atmosphere phenomenon centred on the tropical Pacific. The Southern Oscillation Index (SOI) measures the difference in surface pressure between Tahiti and Darwin; sustained positive SOI indicates La Niña, sustained negative SOI indicates El Niño. In El Niño conditions: the central and eastern tropical Pacific warms, the Trade Winds weaken, and New Zealand typically experiences more northeasterly and northerly flows, drier-than-normal conditions in the north and east, and reduced tropical cyclone activity in the South Pacific's western regions. The SPCZ shifts northeast. For NZ: drier summers in the north and east, warmer air temperatures in the north. In La Niña conditions: the Trade Winds strengthen, the western tropical Pacific warms further, the SPCZ intensifies and shifts southwest, and New Zealand typically receives more northerlies and tropical moisture. Tropical cyclone activity increases in the western South Pacific, and more ex-tropical cyclones reach New Zealand latitudes. For NZ: wetter conditions in the west and north, increased severe weather risk. ENSO's effects typically unfold over months — not immediate — and the strength and exact pattern varies between events.
exam_tip: El Niño: Trade Winds weaken, central Pacific warms, SPCZ shifts NE. NZ: drier north/east, fewer cyclones. La Niña: Trade Winds strengthen, western Pacific warm, SPCZ intensifies/shifts SW. NZ: wetter, more N'ly flow, more cyclone risk. SOI: +ve = La Niña, -ve = El Niño.
key_facts: ENSO: El Niño-Southern Oscillation — coupled ocean-atmosphere cycle in tropical Pacific
SOI: Tahiti minus Darwin pressure difference. Positive SOI = La Niña. Negative = El Niño.
El Niño: Trade Winds weaken, central Pacific warms, SPCZ shifts NE — NZ drier north/east
La Niña: Trade Winds strengthen, western Pacific warm, SPCZ shifts SW — NZ wetter, more N'ly
La Niña: increased tropical cyclone activity in western South Pacific
ENSO effects develop over months — used in seasonal climate outlooks, not short-range forecasts

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of the Cook Strait and its effects on local winds in central New Zealand.
concept: Cook Strait is the body of water separating the North and South Islands of New Zealand, roughly 23 km wide at its narrowest point. Its orientation — broadly northeast to southwest — combined with the pressure gradient between weather systems to the north and south makes it one of the most consistently windy maritime passages in the world. When there is a north-south pressure gradient (which is most of the time in NZ), Cook Strait channels and accelerates the flow. A northerly gradient wind that might be 20-25 knots in open water reaches 35-50 knots through the Strait. The same happens with southerly flows. The constriction creates a venturi effect — the air must accelerate to maintain continuity of flow through the narrowing. This is further amplified by the terrain: the Rimutaka and Tararua ranges funnel air from the Wellington side into the Strait, and the Marlborough Sounds on the South Island side also channel the flow. Wellington is consistently one of the windiest cities in the world as a result. For aviation: Cook Strait crossings at low altitude in strong gradient conditions are extremely turbulent and gusty. The Strait also generates a strong sea state — the combination of confined fetch, strong winds, and complex tidal flows makes it treacherous for mariners and challenging for floatplane or amphibious operations.
exam_tip: Cook Strait: 23 km wide, NE-SW orientation channels N'ly and S'ly flows. Venturi acceleration — 20-25 kt open sea can be 35-50 kt+ through the Strait. Wellington = consistently windy. Low-altitude crossing in strong gradient = severe turbulence.
key_facts: Cook Strait: ~23 km wide at narrowest, NE-SW orientation, separates NI and SI
Channels and accelerates N-S pressure gradient winds (both northerly and southerly)
Venturi effect: 20-25 kt open water can become 35-50 kt+ through the Strait
Rimutaka/Tararua ranges on N side further funnel flow into the Strait
Wellington: consistently among the world's windiest cities
Aviation: low-altitude crossing in strong gradient conditions — severe turbulence and gusts

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of subtropical anticyclones and their influence on New Zealand weather.
concept: Subtropical anticyclones are large, slow-moving high-pressure systems that sit permanently (in a statistical sense) in the subtropics of both hemispheres — in the Southern Hemisphere, the Tasman/Australian High and its eastward extension form a semi-permanent feature. These systems are the result of subsiding air in the descending limb of the Hadley Cell. The sinking air warms and dries as it descends (adiabatic warming), producing stable, clear conditions beneath and suppressing precipitation. For New Zealand, the position of the subtropical high is the key driver of extended settled weather. When a large anticyclone sits directly over or just north of NZ, it brings dry, sunny, stable conditions with light winds. The South Pacific subtropical high typically extends across the Tasman Sea and is most pronounced in summer. Fronts moving from the west are blocked by the high or deflected to the south, giving extended fine spells. The blocking effect — when a high becomes particularly persistent and blocks the normal progression of frontal systems — is responsible for drought conditions in some years. For aviation: anticyclones mean excellent conditions for VFR flight, but subsidence inversions (a stable layer where the descending air pools) can trap radiation fog and haze beneath, particularly overnight and in the morning.
exam_tip: Subtropical anticyclone: subsiding air from Hadley Cell descending limb. Warm, stable, dry conditions. When sitting over NZ = extended fine weather, fronts blocked or deflected south. Subsidence inversion: traps fog and haze in the morning. Blocking high = drought risk.
key_facts: Subtropical anticyclone: large high caused by subsiding air in Hadley Cell descending limb
Subsiding air warms (adiabatic) and dries — stable, clear, precipitation-suppressed conditions
South Pacific subtropical high: semi-permanent, strongest in summer over Tasman/SW Pacific
When positioned over NZ: extended fine weather, fronts blocked or deflected south
Blocking high: persistent anticyclone prevents normal frontal progression — drought risk
Aviation: excellent VFR but watch for subsidence inversion trapping fog and haze

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the general circulation pattern affecting New Zealand and the South Pacific in the mid-latitudes.
concept: New Zealand sits in the mid-latitudes of the Southern Hemisphere, a zone dominated by the westerly wind belt and the procession of mobile anticyclones, fronts, and depressions that track from west to east. The pattern is driven by the temperature contrast between the equatorial tropics and the polar regions — this contrast generates the polar front jet stream at upper levels, which steers surface weather systems. In the Southern Hemisphere, the westerly flow is more pronounced and less interrupted by land masses than in the Northern Hemisphere, so the wave pattern is less amplified and systems move more quickly. The typical NZ weather sequence is: anticyclone — pre-frontal northerlies — frontal passage (west or southwest wind shift, rain) — post-frontal southerlies — next anticyclone. This cycle repeats roughly every 5-10 days during active westerly periods. In the winter, the westerlies intensify and move north, bringing more frequent frontal systems to New Zealand. In summer, the subtropical high expands and the westerlies shift south, resulting in more settled conditions especially in the north. The jet stream position is the key upper-level driver — when it passes directly over NZ, surface systems are vigorous and fast-moving; when it dips well south, NZ basks in a northerly ridge.
exam_tip: NZ mid-latitude weather sequence: anticyclone → pre-frontal northerlies → frontal passage → post-frontal southerlies → anticyclone. Cycle: ~5-10 days in active westerly. Winter: westerlies intensify, move N. Summer: subtropical high expands, westerlies move S. Jet stream steers the lot.
key_facts: NZ sits in Southern Hemisphere westerly belt — mid-latitude cyclone track zone
Westerly flow stronger and less interrupted by land than NH equivalent
Typical weather sequence: high → N'ly → frontal passage → S'ly → next high (~5-10 days)
Jet stream at upper levels steers surface weather systems E'ward
Winter: westerlies shift north, more frequent frontal systems over NZ
Summer: subtropical high expands south, westerlies shift south, NZ more settled

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of orographic uplift on precipitation distribution in New Zealand.
concept: Orographic uplift occurs when air is forced to rise over a topographic barrier. In New Zealand, the Main Divide of the Southern Alps is the dominant orographic feature, and its effect on precipitation is dramatic and well-documented. The prevailing westerly flow brings moist air off the Tasman Sea; the Alps force that air upward, causing cooling, condensation, and heavy precipitation on the windward (west) side. Milford Sound, on the Fiordland coast, receives over 6,000 mm of rain per year — one of the highest rainfalls in the world. The Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers persist largely because of the enormous orographic precipitation feeding them year-round. The leeward (east) side — Canterbury Plains — receives as little as 600-700 mm per year, sitting in the rain shadow. Christchurch airport is famously drier than the West Coast despite being only 150 km away. For aviation, orographic uplift means: windward approaches can have very low cloud bases and poor visibility in rain; leeward (east) approaches are typically clearer. However, the descending air on the lee side produces the nor'wester hazards previously described. Pilots transiting the Southern Alps must plan for dramatically different conditions on each side of the Main Divide — it's common for VFR to be impossible on the West Coast while CAVOK conditions prevail at Christchurch.
exam_tip: Orographic uplift: moist westerly hits Alps, forced rise, heavy rainfall on windward (west) side. Rain shadow on lee (east) side — Canterbury gets <700mm vs Milford 6,000mm+. West Coast = low cloud/poor vis; East = typically clearer but nor'wester hazards.
key_facts: Orographic uplift: forced ascent of moist air over terrain barrier causes condensation and rain
West Coast (windward): extreme precipitation — Milford Sound 6,000+ mm/year
Canterbury Plains (leeward/rain shadow): 600-700 mm/year
Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers maintained by massive orographic precipitation input
Aviation: windward approaches can have very low cloud/poor vis; leeward typically clearer
Dramatic weather contrast between West Coast and Canterbury — can differ by two weather systems

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of monsoon circulations and their influence on Pacific Island weather.
concept: The monsoon is a large-scale seasonal reversal of wind direction driven by the differential heating of land and sea. The classic monsoon is associated with the Asian subcontinent, but a weaker monsoon trough affects the northern areas of Australia and Papua New Guinea — the 'Australian monsoon' or 'Indo-Australian monsoon.' During the Southern Hemisphere summer (December to March), deep heating over Australia and Papua New Guinea creates a continental low that draws moist, unstable air from the north — a southward extension of the Asian summer monsoon. This brings the wet season to northern Australia, PNG, and the western Pacific island nations. The monsoon trough (the surface pressure trough marking the monsoon boundary) shifts south of the equator in austral summer, bringing convective rainfall and tropical disturbances. It retreats north again in the austral winter, leaving the dry season. For Pacific Island aviation, the monsoon season means: frequent afternoon and evening thunderstorms, poor visibility in heavy rain, the risk of embedded convection, and the increased probability of tropical cyclone formation. For NZ pilots flying to Pacific Islands, understanding the seasonal regime (monsoon wet season vs trade wind dry season) at the destination is essential flight planning knowledge.
exam_tip: Monsoon: seasonal reversal of wind direction, land-sea heating contrast. Indo-Australian monsoon: wet season Dec-Mar in northern Australia/PNG/western Pacific Islands. Monsoon trough moves south of equator in austral summer. Aviation: frequent afternoon CBs, embedded convection.
key_facts: Monsoon: seasonal wind reversal caused by land-sea differential heating
Indo-Australian monsoon: wet season December to March in northern Australia, PNG, western Pacific
Monsoon trough moves south of equator in austral summer bringing heavy convective rainfall
Dry season: austral winter when monsoon trough retreats north
Wet season aviation: frequent afternoon CBs, poor visibility in rain, embedded convection risk
Essential knowledge for Pacific Island flight planning: determine which season you're flying in

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of tropical disturbances and their significance for South Pacific aviation.
concept: Tropical disturbances are organised areas of convection and cloudiness in the tropics that have not yet developed into tropical cyclones. They include tropical depressions (organised circulation, maximum winds <34 kt), tropical disturbances (less organised, the pre-depression stage), and easterly waves (ripples in the trade wind flow that propagate westward). They are significant because they are the precursors of tropical cyclones and because, even without developing into cyclones, they bring periods of severe convective weather, heavy rain, and low visibility to Pacific Island destinations. In the South Pacific, tropical disturbances typically form within or near the SPCZ during the cyclone season (November to April). Satellite imagery is the primary tool for tracking them — a curved band of dense overcast and deep convection is the visual signature. For aviation purposes, any organised tropical disturbance in the vicinity of your destination demands careful attention. Even a 'mere' tropical depression can produce cumulonimbus tops to FL500+ and rainfall rates that overwhelm instrument approaches. Islands like Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa are regularly affected by multiple disturbances each season without them necessarily becoming named cyclones. Pre-departure satellite analysis and pilot briefing are non-negotiable for Pacific Island operations.
exam_tip: Tropical disturbance: organised convection that may develop into a cyclone. Easterly waves: westward-propagating ripples in Trade Wind flow — precursors to cyclones. Even non-cyclone disturbances bring CB to FL500+, heavy rain. Satellite imagery is the tracking tool.
key_facts: Tropical disturbance: organised convection/cloudiness, pre-cyclone stage
Tropical depression: organised circulation, maximum winds <34 knots
Easterly waves: westward-propagating troughs in Trade Wind flow — cyclone precursors
Disturbances produce CBs to FL500+, heavy rain, and poor visibility even without becoming cyclones
Form primarily within or near the SPCZ during November to April
Aviation: satellite analysis essential before any Pacific Island flight during cyclone season

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the structure and characteristics of a tropical cyclone.
concept: A tropical cyclone is an intense rotating low-pressure system of tropical origin. Its energy comes from the release of latent heat as warm, moist air rises and condenses in towering cumulonimbus clouds organised around a central eye. The key structural features are: the eye — a roughly circular area of light winds and reduced cloud (sometimes clear sky) at the centre, typically 20-60 km in diameter; the eyewall — the ring of the most intense convection, strongest winds, and heaviest precipitation immediately surrounding the eye; spiral rainbands — curved bands of convection spiralling inward toward the centre, separated by relatively clearer slots; the warm core — the entire system is defined by a warm temperature anomaly throughout the troposphere, which distinguishes it from extratropical systems. Surface winds spiral inward counterclockwise in the Southern Hemisphere (clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere) and upward in the eyewall. The horizontal scale of a tropical cyclone is typically 400-800 km diameter for gale-force winds, though the eye and eyewall are much smaller. Intensity is measured on the Australian Tropical Cyclone Intensity Scale (Categories 1-5), with Category 5 having wind gusts exceeding 280 km/h. The eyewall is the most dangerous zone for any aircraft that might inadvertently enter it.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclone structure: eye (calm, clear), eyewall (most intense — strongest winds, heaviest rain), spiral rainbands. Warm core throughout troposphere. Southern Hemisphere: winds rotate counterclockwise. Australian scale: Cat 1-5; Cat 5 >280 km/h gusts.
key_facts: Eye: calm centre, 20-60 km diameter, clear or partly clear sky, light winds
Eyewall: ring of most intense CBs, strongest winds, heaviest precipitation
Spiral rainbands: curved convective bands spiralling inward toward centre
Warm core: positive temperature anomaly throughout troposphere (unlike extratropical lows)
Southern Hemisphere: surface winds spiral counterclockwise inward
Australian scale: Cat 1-5; Cat 5 = gusts >280 km/h; gale-force winds extend 400-800 km diameter

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the formation conditions and life cycle of a tropical cyclone.
concept: Tropical cyclone formation requires a specific set of conditions, sometimes called the necessary ingredients. Sea surface temperature must exceed approximately 26-27°C through a depth of at least 50 metres — this provides the energy (water vapour) to fuel the convection. There must be a pre-existing disturbance or area of organised convection (easterly wave, tropical depression, or ITCZ disturbance). The system must be far enough from the equator that the Coriolis force is sufficient to impart rotation — generally more than 5° of latitude from the equator. Vertical wind shear must be low — strong shear would tilt the storm's vertical structure and disrupt the warm core. And the low-level convergence and upper-level divergence must be aligned to allow persistent deep convection. The life cycle: formation (disturbance organises), intensification (wind speeds increase, eye develops as the system strengthens), mature stage (maximum intensity, well-defined eye), and dissipation (moving over cooler water or land, or encountering increasing wind shear). Dissipation over NZ latitudes involves extratropical transition. Intensification can be rapid in the right conditions — a weak tropical depression can become a Category 4 cyclone in 24 hours given the right ocean heat and low shear.
exam_tip: Formation requirements: SST >26-27°C, pre-existing disturbance, >5° from equator (Coriolis), LOW vertical wind shear, low-level convergence/upper-level divergence. Rapid intensification possible in 24 hours. Dissipation: cool water, land, or high shear.
key_facts: SST requirement: >26-27°C to at least 50m depth — provides energy
Pre-existing disturbance required: easterly wave, tropical depression, or ITCZ active area
Must be >5° latitude from equator for sufficient Coriolis force to create rotation
Low vertical wind shear essential — high shear tilts the system and disrupts warm core
Life cycle: formation → intensification → mature → dissipation
Rapid intensification: possible — Cat 4 from depression in 24 hrs with right conditions

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the aviation hazards associated with tropical cyclones.
concept: Tropical cyclones present multiple, simultaneous, severe aviation hazards — no single one is the worst. Extreme winds in and near the eyewall can exceed 100 knots at the surface and are accompanied by violent turbulence; a light aircraft would be destroyed in seconds. The eyewall CBs extend from near the surface to well above FL500 — penetrating one in an aircraft is equivalent to flying into an extreme thunderstorm multiplied many times over. Heavy precipitation can cause engine flameout in jet aircraft and dramatically reduces visibility. Hail is present in the eyewall and in the more vigorous rainbands. Severe icing is possible at altitude due to the enormous moisture content. Even the spiral rainbands, which appear 'less severe' on radar, contain severe convective activity and turbulence. The sea state beneath a mature cyclone — 10-20 metre waves in extreme events — is incompatible with any ditching or float-equipped aircraft operation. Tornadoes can form in the spiral rainbands of land-affecting cyclones. For the forecaster and dispatcher, a cyclone's hazards extend outward: gale-force winds (>34 kt) exist 200-400 km from the centre; destructive winds (>48 kt) exist 100-200 km from the centre. No aircraft should operate within 300-500 nm of a tropical cyclone's centre.
exam_tip: Cyclone hazards: extreme winds (>100 kt eyewall), violent turbulence, CBs to FL500+, heavy rain (engine flameout risk), hail, severe icing, tornadoes in bands. Even rainbands are hazardous. Stay 300-500 nm from centre. Sea state incompatible with ditching.
key_facts: Eyewall: extreme winds >100 kt, violent turbulence, CBs to FL500+
Heavy precipitation: can cause jet engine flameout; near-zero visibility
Hail present in eyewall and vigorous rainbands
Severe icing at altitude due to extreme moisture content
Tornadoes can develop in spiral rainbands when cyclone affects land
Safe distance: no aircraft within 300-500 nm of a tropical cyclone centre

topic: 20.14 -- Local Winds – New Zealand and the South Pacific
syllabus_desc: Describe the extratropical transition of tropical cyclones and its significance for New Zealand aviation.
concept: Extratropical transition (ETT) is the process by which a tropical cyclone loses its warm-core tropical characteristics and transforms into an extratropical (mid-latitude) system as it moves poleward into regions of lower sea surface temperature and increasing westerly vertical wind shear. For New Zealand, ETT is arguably more significant than the cyclone in its tropical form — because it's the ETT phase that brings these systems close to or over New Zealand. The process: as the system moves south past about 25-30°S, the warm core begins to erode, the tight symmetrical eye structure breaks down, and the cloud and rain distribution becomes more asymmetric (more like a frontal system). The central pressure may rise (weakening) but the overall wind field — the area covered by gale-force and strong winds — can actually expand dramatically. A system that was a Category 3 cyclone (destructive wind area perhaps 200 km radius) might transition to a large, vigorous extratropical low with gale-force winds over an area 1,000 km wide. For NZ aviation, this means: the arrival of an ex-tropical system often brings widespread gale warnings, heavy rain, severe turbulence, and low cloud over a large area. These systems are harder to forecast precisely than either a stable tropical cyclone or a normal mid-latitude depression. Ex-Cyclone Gita (2018) is the benchmark NZ case.
exam_tip: ETT: tropical cyclone transforms to extratropical low as it moves south. Warm core erodes, asymmetric structure develops. Key: gale area can EXPAND even as centre weakens. Ex-tropical systems harder to forecast. Ex-Cyclone Gita (2018) = NZ benchmark case.
