key_facts: ETT begins as cyclone moves south past ~25-30°S into cooler water and westerly shear
Warm core erodes, eye breaks down, cloud/rain distribution becomes asymmetric
Central pressure may rise (weakening) but gale-force wind area can expand dramatically
Ex-tropical systems often cover a larger area with gales than the original cyclone
ETT systems difficult to forecast precisely — major NZ aviation risk
Ex-Cyclone Gita (2018): underwent ETT affecting the South Island with severe winds and rain

syllabus_desc: Define humidity, relative humidity, absolute humidity, and specific humidity.
concept: Water vapour is an invisible gas — when you see cloud or fog, you're seeing liquid water droplets or ice crystals, not the vapour itself. Absolute humidity is the mass of water vapour per unit volume of air (g/m³) — how much water is actually present. Specific humidity is the mass of water vapour per unit mass of moist air (g/kg) — useful because it doesn't change with pressure changes (unlike absolute humidity). Relative humidity (RH) is the one you see in weather products. It's the ratio of the actual vapour pressure to the saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage. RH = 100% doesn't mean the air is 'full' — it means the air is at the threshold of condensation at that temperature. The critical thing about RH: it depends on temperature. If you cool a parcel of air without adding or removing any moisture, the RH rises because the saturation vapour pressure falls with temperature. This is exactly what happens in clouds, fog, and dew formation. Warm the same air and the RH falls — which is why morning fog burns off as the ground heats up. For practical flying, RH above about 90% in the boundary layer is a red flag for fog, low cloud, and dew formation.
exam_tip: RH = (actual vapour pressure / saturation vapour pressure) × 100%. RH rises as temperature falls (no moisture change needed). RH 100% = saturation. Specific humidity doesn't change with altitude (unlike RH). >90% RH = fog/low cloud risk.
key_facts: Absolute humidity: mass of water vapour per unit volume (g/m³)
Specific humidity: mass of water vapour per unit mass of moist air (g/kg) — conserved with altitude
Relative humidity: actual vapour pressure / saturation vapour pressure × 100%
RH rises as temperature falls — same moisture content, lower saturation threshold
RH 100% = saturation — threshold for condensation
RH >90% in boundary layer = significant fog and low cloud risk

syllabus_desc: Define dew point temperature and describe the relationship between dew point and relative humidity.
concept: The dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure and constant moisture content) for saturation to occur — for RH to reach 100%. It's a direct measure of the moisture content of the air: a high dew point means a lot of water vapour is present; a low dew point means the air is dry. The spread between temperature and dew point (T - Td) tells you how close to saturation the air is. A spread of 2°C or less in the surface layer is a warning for fog or low stratus — particularly if radiative cooling is occurring overnight. A spread of 10°C or more in a moist airmass suggests that significant lifting will be required before cloud forms. In aviation products: METARs report temperature and dew point (e.g. 18/16 means temp 18°C, dew point 16°C — a spread of only 2°C, high fog risk). TAFs and weather forecasts often specify dew point as a proxy for visibility risk. When lifting a parcel, it cools at the dry adiabatic lapse rate (~1°C/100m) while the dew point drops at only ~0.2°C/100m. Cloud base forms where temperature equals dew point — you can estimate this as (T - Td) ÷ 0.8 × 100m, or approximately 125 ft per degree of T-Td spread.
exam_tip: Dew point: temperature at which RH reaches 100% (saturation). Spread (T - Td): small spread = near saturation = fog/cloud risk. Cloud base estimate: (T - Td) × 125 ft. METAR example: 18/16 = 2°C spread = high fog/low cloud risk.
key_facts: Dew point: temperature to which air must cool for saturation (RH = 100%)
High dew point = high moisture content; low dew point = dry air
Spread (T - Td): ≤2°C = fog/low cloud risk, especially with overnight cooling
Cloud base height estimate: (T - Td) × 125 ft (or ÷ 0.8 × 100m)
METAR: temperature/dew point reported as e.g. '18/16' (2°C spread = near saturation)
Lifting parcel: temp falls ~1°C/100m, dew point falls ~0.2°C/100m — they converge at cloud base

syllabus_desc: Describe the process of evaporation and condensation and the latent heat exchanges involved.
concept: Evaporation is the change of state from liquid water to water vapour. It requires energy — specifically, latent heat of evaporation (~2,500 J/g at 20°C). This energy is absorbed from the surroundings, which is why evaporation has a cooling effect. Evaporation rate depends on temperature (higher temp = faster evaporation), wind speed (replaces saturated surface air with drier air), and relative humidity (drier air = faster evaporation). Condensation is the reverse — water vapour changing to liquid. Latent heat is released into the surrounding air during condensation. This release of latent heat is enormously significant in atmospheric dynamics: it's the energy source that drives thunderstorms, intensifies depressions, and fuels tropical cyclones. Inside a cloud, every droplet that forms releases heat to the surrounding air. This additional heat makes the rising air parcel warmer than a dry parcel would be at the same level, reducing its density and allowing it to continue rising — this is why the saturated adiabatic lapse rate is slower than the dry adiabatic rate. For aviation: evaporation of fuel on your skin feels cold (latent heat absorbed from you). Ice sublimating from a cold aircraft surface cools it further. Heavy rain evaporating in dry air below cloud base creates virga — and evaporative cooling can trigger strong downdrafts beneath thunderstorms.
exam_tip: Evaporation: liquid → vapour, ABSORBS latent heat (cooling effect). Condensation: vapour → liquid, RELEASES latent heat (warming effect). Latent heat release in clouds is what fuels thunderstorms and allows SALR < DALR. Virga = rain evaporating before reaching surface.
key_facts: Evaporation: liquid → vapour; absorbs latent heat ~2,500 J/g — cooling effect
Condensation: vapour → liquid; releases latent heat — warms surrounding air
Latent heat release in clouds: energy source for thunderstorms, cyclones, and frontal depressions
Latent heat release is why SALR < DALR — condensing air parcels are warmer than dry parcels
Virga: precipitation evaporating before reaching surface — evaporative cooling drives downdrafts
Evaporation rate increases with temperature, wind speed, and lower relative humidity

syllabus_desc: Describe the process of sublimation and its role in ice crystal formation in clouds.
concept: Sublimation is the direct change of state between ice and water vapour without passing through the liquid phase — in either direction. In the atmosphere, sublimation occurs when ice crystals evaporate directly to vapour (deposition is the reverse — water vapour depositing directly onto a surface as ice, as in frost formation). Ice nuclei are particles (dust, clay, biological particles) that water vapour can deposit on directly at temperatures below 0°C to form ice crystals. This is distinct from the freezing of liquid water droplets. In mixed-phase clouds — where both supercooled liquid droplets and ice crystals coexist — the Bergeron-Findeisen process operates: ice crystals are more efficient at attracting water vapour than liquid droplets (because the saturation vapour pressure over ice is lower than over liquid water at the same temperature). So ice crystals grow at the expense of the surrounding liquid droplets. This is the primary precipitation formation mechanism in mid-latitude clouds — including the clouds that produce most of NZ's rainfall. A cloud may form as liquid droplets, glaciate (convert to ice crystals) in its upper parts, and then produce precipitation via the Bergeron process. For icing: a cloud top glaciated to ice crystals generally doesn't cause serious structural icing, but the mixed-phase region below it is the most hazardous zone for in-flight icing.
exam_tip: Sublimation: direct ice ↔ vapour change (no liquid stage). Frost = deposition (vapour → ice). Bergeron-Findeisen: ice crystals grow at expense of supercooled droplets (lower saturation pressure over ice). Main precipitation mechanism in NZ mid-latitude clouds. Mixed-phase cloud = worst icing zone.
key_facts: Sublimation: direct change between ice and vapour (no liquid intermediate)
Deposition: reverse — vapour deposits directly as ice (frost formation)
Ice nuclei: particles on which vapour deposits to form ice crystals at sub-zero temperatures
Bergeron-Findeisen: ice crystals grow at expense of supercooled droplets — saturation pressure lower over ice
Primary precipitation mechanism in mid-latitude clouds including NZ rainfall producers
Mixed-phase cloud (ice + supercooled droplets) = most hazardous zone for structural icing

syllabus_desc: Describe the concept of saturation and the factors that determine the saturation vapour pressure.
concept: Saturation occurs when the rate of evaporation from a liquid surface equals the rate of condensation — the air is holding as much water vapour as possible at that temperature. The saturation vapour pressure (SVP) is the partial pressure of water vapour in saturated air. The critical relationship is that SVP increases strongly with temperature — roughly doubling for every 10°C rise. This is why warm air can 'hold more moisture' (though strictly speaking, it's that warm air requires more vapour pressure before it becomes saturated). At 0°C, the SVP is about 6.1 hPa. At 30°C, it's about 42 hPa. So tropical air at 30°C can contain nearly seven times more water vapour at saturation than air at 0°C. This has enormous implications: tropical weather releases vastly more latent heat when moist air ascends and condenses, fuelling the intense thunderstorms and cyclones of the tropics. SVP is also slightly different over ice vs liquid water at the same temperature — lower over ice — which is why the Bergeron-Findeisen process works. For aviation and for forecasting: the temperature-SVP relationship is why moist tropical airmasses are so energetically unstable, and why the same amount of moisture leads to much more dramatic weather in the tropics than at higher latitudes.
exam_tip: SVP increases strongly with temperature — roughly doubles per 10°C. At 30°C, SVP is ~7x higher than at 0°C. SVP over ice < SVP over liquid at same temp (drives Bergeron-Findeisen). Warm air = higher SVP threshold = more moisture before saturation.
key_facts: Saturation: evaporation rate = condensation rate — air at maximum moisture for that temperature
Saturation vapour pressure (SVP): partial pressure of water vapour in saturated air
SVP roughly doubles for every 10°C temperature rise
At 0°C: SVP ≈ 6.1 hPa. At 30°C: SVP ≈ 42 hPa (nearly 7× higher)
SVP over ice < SVP over liquid water at same sub-zero temperature (Bergeron-Findeisen basis)
Tropical moist air: high SVP threshold = massive latent heat release on condensation = intense weather

syllabus_desc: Describe the lifting condensation level and how it determines cloud base height.
concept: The Lifting Condensation Level (LCL) is the altitude at which a parcel of air, lifted from the surface, becomes saturated — where its temperature equals its dew point and cloud begins to form. It marks the base of any convective cloud that develops from surface heating. The LCL can be estimated from surface temperature and dew point: as the parcel rises, temperature falls at the dry adiabatic lapse rate (~1°C/100m) and the dew point falls more slowly at ~0.2°C/100m. They converge at approximately (T - Td) × 125 ft above the surface, or dividing the spread in Celsius by 0.8 gives height in hundreds of metres. For a T-Td spread of 10°C: LCL ≈ 1,250 ft AGL. For 5°C: ≈ 625 ft AGL. This is why, on a very humid day when temperature and dew point are close, you see very low-based cumulus, while on a dry spring day the cumulus bases can be at 6,000-8,000 ft. The LCL applies to surface-based convection. It is distinct from the Level of Free Convection (LFC) — the level above the LCL where the parcel becomes warmer than its environment and convection becomes self-sustaining. For most convective situations the LCL and LFC are reasonably close, but in a capped atmosphere they can be widely separated.
exam_tip: LCL = cloud base for surface-based convection. Height ≈ (T - Td) × 125 ft. Humid day (small spread) = low-based Cu. Dry day (large spread) = high-based Cu. LCL ≠ LFC — LFC is where convection becomes self-sustaining (warmer than environment).
key_facts: LCL: altitude where lifted surface parcel reaches saturation — marks base of convective cloud
Height estimate: (T - Td) × 125 ft AGL, where T and Td are surface values in °C
Small T-Td spread = low LCL = low cloud base (humid conditions)
Large T-Td spread = high LCL = high cloud base (dry conditions)
LCL applies to surface-based convection only
LFC (Level of Free Convection) is where parcel becomes warmer than environment — above LCL in capped situations

syllabus_desc: Describe the role of hygroscopic and other condensation nuclei in cloud and fog formation.
concept: Pure water doesn't condense easily into droplets without a surface to condense on — the physics of pure droplet nucleation requires supersaturation of several hundred percent, which essentially never occurs in the real atmosphere. Instead, condensation happens readily on condensation nuclei (CN) — tiny particles of dust, sea salt, combustion products, clay minerals, and other aerosols that are present in enormous concentrations in the lower atmosphere. Hygroscopic particles (salt, sulphate, some combustion products) attract water vapour at relative humidities below 100% — sea salt can begin absorbing moisture at RH as low as 75-80%. This is why maritime air can produce haze and even fog before RH technically reaches 100%. The size and nature of condensation nuclei determines the cloud droplet size distribution: clean maritime air (fewer, larger nuclei) produces fewer, larger droplets and tends to have lower optical density; polluted continental air (vast numbers of tiny nuclei) produces vast numbers of very small droplets, high optical density, and suppresses precipitation (each droplet is smaller and less likely to fall as rain — hence 'brown clouds' over cities, and why industrial smoke can suppress rainfall downwind). Condensation nuclei are essential for all cloud and fog formation at atmospheric conditions. In clean air over remote ocean, the supply of nuclei may actually be the limiting factor in cloud formation.
exam_tip: Condensation nuclei (CN): essential for cloud/fog formation — pure air requires massive supersaturation. Hygroscopic nuclei (sea salt): absorb moisture at RH <100% (even 75-80%). Maritime air (fewer, larger droplets) vs continental/polluted air (more, smaller droplets — suppresses rain).
key_facts: Condensation nuclei (CN): particles essential for droplet formation at atmospheric RH
Without CN, condensation requires supersaturation of hundreds of % — never occurs naturally
Hygroscopic CN (sea salt, sulphates): attract moisture at RH as low as 75-80%
Maritime air: fewer, larger CN → fewer, larger droplets → lower cloud optical density
Polluted continental air: vast numbers of tiny CN → huge numbers of tiny droplets → rain suppression
CN supply can be the limiting factor for cloud formation in very clean remote ocean air

syllabus_desc: Define atmospheric stability and instability and describe how they are determined.
concept: Atmospheric stability refers to the tendency of the atmosphere to resist or encourage vertical motion. It's determined by comparing the environmental lapse rate (ELR) — how temperature actually changes with altitude in the atmosphere at a given time and place — with the appropriate adiabatic lapse rate for a parcel being displaced. If you lift a parcel and it becomes colder than its surroundings, it's denser and will sink back — the atmosphere is stable. If you lift a parcel and it's warmer than its surroundings, it's less dense and will continue to rise — the atmosphere is unstable. The DALR is ~1°C/100m (for unsaturated air). The SALR varies from about 0.4°C/100m in warm, moist tropical air to about 0.9°C/100m in cold dry conditions, averaging around 0.6°C/100m. Stability rules: ELR < SALR = absolutely stable (all parcels sink back). SALR < ELR < DALR = conditionally unstable (stable if dry, unstable if saturated). ELR > DALR = absolutely unstable (rare in free atmosphere, common in superadiabatic surface layers on hot sunny days). For aviation: stable air = smooth flight, stratiform cloud, poor visibility in fog and haze. Unstable air = turbulence, cumuliform cloud, good visibility. Conditional instability is the most common condition and the most important for understanding thunderstorm development.
exam_tip: Stability: compare ELR with lapse rates. ELR < SALR = absolutely stable. SALR < ELR < DALR = conditionally unstable. ELR > DALR = absolutely unstable. Stable = smooth/stratiform/poor vis. Unstable = turbulent/cumuliform/good vis.
key_facts: Stability: ELR vs adiabatic lapse rates — determines if displaced air parcel sinks back or continues rising
DALR: ~1°C/100m (unsaturated parcels). SALR: ~0.4-0.9°C/100m (saturated, varies with temp/moisture)
Absolutely stable: ELR < SALR — all parcels sink back when displaced
Conditionally unstable: SALR < ELR < DALR — stable if dry, unstable once saturated
Absolutely unstable: ELR > DALR — rare in free atmosphere, occurs in superadiabatic surface layers
Stable air: smooth, stratiform cloud, haze/fog. Unstable: turbulence, cumuliform cloud, good vis

syllabus_desc: Describe the processes that increase atmospheric stability.
concept: Stability increases when the temperature profile becomes less steep — when the difference in temperature between the surface and higher levels decreases. The main processes are: subsidence — when air descends in an anticyclone, it warms adiabatically. Upper levels warm more than lower levels (because the subsiding air has descended further), creating a subsidence inversion that increases stability and often creates a lid on convection. Radiative cooling at the surface — on clear nights, the ground cools rapidly by radiation, chilling the air immediately above it. Upper levels cool much less. The resulting inversion near the surface dramatically increases low-level stability. Warm air advection at upper levels — if warm air is being brought in aloft while the surface remains cool, the lapse rate decreases. Cold air advection at the surface (or passage of a cold pool) — cold air beneath warmer air above creates an isothermal or inversion layer. Moisture reduction also plays a role — if upper-level air dries out (less latent heat available), it becomes more stable to saturated parcels. For NZ aviation, overnight radiation cooling and subsidence in anticyclones are the most common stability-increasing mechanisms, and both are the primary causes of radiation fog and low stratus formation.
exam_tip: Stability increases via: (1) Subsidence — upper levels warm more than surface → inversion. (2) Radiative cooling at surface overnight → surface inversion. (3) Warm air advection aloft. (4) Cold air advection at surface. All reduce the lapse rate.
key_facts: Subsidence: descending air warms adiabatically; upper layers warm more → subsidence inversion
Radiative cooling at surface: clears sky allows surface to cool, creating low-level inversion
Warm air advection aloft: warm air at upper levels over cooler surface reduces lapse rate
Cold air advection at surface: cold surface layer under warm air = inversion
All processes reduce the ELR → more stable atmosphere
NZ: overnight radiation cooling and anticyclonic subsidence = primary fog/stratus triggers

syllabus_desc: Describe the processes that decrease atmospheric stability (increase instability).
concept: Instability increases when the temperature profile steepens — when lower levels warm or upper levels cool, increasing the lapse rate. The main processes are: surface heating — daytime solar radiation heats the ground, which then heats the air immediately above it. The low-level air warms quickly while upper levels remain relatively cool, steepening the lapse rate. This is why convective activity peaks in the afternoon. Cold air advection at upper levels — cold air advected over a relatively warm surface increases the lapse rate. Radiative cooling of cloud tops — cloud tops cool overnight by emitting longwave radiation upward, while the cloud base stays relatively warm near the surface. This steepens the lapse rate through the cloud layer and can trigger nocturnal convection. Moistening the lower atmosphere — as the lower atmosphere moistens, the SALR becomes more relevant and conditional instability increases. Orographic lifting — forcing an unstably-stratified air mass over terrain releases the instability. For NZ aviation, surface heating in summer is the dominant mechanism for afternoon thunderstorm development, particularly over inland areas and mountain ranges. The combination of surface heating, orographic uplift, and a moist airmass from a northerly flow is the classic NZ thunderstorm setup.
exam_tip: Instability increases via: (1) Surface heating (steepens lapse rate). (2) Cold air advection aloft. (3) Cloud top cooling (triggers nocturnal convection). (4) Lower-level moistening. NZ afternoon thunderstorm: surface heating + orographic lift + moist N'ly.
key_facts: Surface heating: ground warms air from below, steepening lapse rate → peak instability mid-afternoon
Cold air advection aloft: cold upper air over warm surface steepens lapse rate
Cloud top cooling: radiative cooling of cloud tops increases lapse rate through cloud → nocturnal convection
Lower-level moistening: reduces SALR threshold for instability in conditionally unstable air
Orographic lifting: releases latent instability in a moist, marginally stable air mass
NZ summer thunderstorm: surface heating + N'ly moist flow + orographic uplift

syllabus_desc: Describe temperature inversions, their types, and their effects on aviation.
concept: A temperature inversion is a layer in which temperature increases with altitude — the reverse of the normal situation. Inversions are extremely stable because any parcel displaced upward into the inversion finds itself colder and denser than its surroundings and is immediately pushed back down. This makes inversions highly effective lids on convection and vertical mixing. Types: Radiation inversion — forms overnight as the surface cools by radiation, chilling the air above it. Strongest at dawn, erodes quickly once solar heating begins. Subsidence inversion — forms when sinking air in an anticyclone compresses and warms aloft. Typically found at 3,000-8,000 ft in a high-pressure system. Frontal inversion — at a warm front, warm air overrides cold air, creating an inversion at the frontal surface. Marine inversion — common in coastal areas where cool maritime air underlies warmer continental air. Aviation effects: inversions trap haze, smoke, and pollution below them (city smog), creating a distinct visibility horizon with haze below and clear air above. They trap radiation fog and prevent its dispersal until solar heating is strong enough to erode them from below. They mark the top of turbulent mixing — smooth air exists above an inversion. They can create a wind shear level — the wind regime often changes markedly across an inversion.
exam_tip: Inversion: temperature increases with height. Highly stable — suppresses vertical mixing. Types: radiation (surface overnight), subsidence (anticyclone, 3,000-8,000 ft), frontal, marine. Aviation: traps haze/fog/pollution, marks turbulence top, wind shear layer.
key_facts: Temperature inversion: temp increases with altitude — extremely stable layer
Acts as lid on convection, vertical mixing, and fog dispersal
Radiation inversion: surface cooling overnight — strongest at dawn, burns off with heating
Subsidence inversion: anticyclonic sinking warms aloft — typically 3,000-8,000 ft
Frontal inversion: warm air overriding cold at warm front
Aviation: traps haze/fog below, marks top of turbulent layer, often coincides with wind shear

syllabus_desc: Describe convective available potential energy (CAPE) and its significance for thunderstorm development.
concept: Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) is a measure of the energy available to a rising parcel of air — the amount of buoyancy it has throughout its ascent through an unstable atmosphere. It's calculated as the integrated area on a thermodynamic diagram between the parcel temperature and the environmental temperature from the Level of Free Convection (LFC) to the Equilibrium Level (EL) where the parcel temperature again equals the environment. More CAPE = more energy available for convective updrafts = stronger thunderstorms. CAPE values roughly: 0-1,000 J/kg = weak convection (light Cu, limited thunderstorm potential). 1,000-2,500 J/kg = moderate instability, thunderstorm development likely with triggering. 2,500-3,500 J/kg = significant instability, severe thunderstorms possible. >3,500 J/kg = extreme instability, severe to violent thunderstorms likely. In NZ, CAPE values rarely reach tropical extremes — values of 1,000-2,000 J/kg are considered high for NZ conditions. CAPE is used in NZ MetService severe weather forecasting and is available on skew-T log-P diagrams (upper air soundings). A large CAPE value is necessary but not sufficient for thunderstorm development — you also need a trigger (lifting mechanism) and sufficient moisture. A capping inversion can prevent CAPE from being released until a strong trigger breaks the cap, then releasing accumulated CAPE explosively.
exam_tip: CAPE: energy available to rising parcel. 0-1,000 J/kg = weak. 1,000-2,500 = moderate. 2,500-3,500 = significant. >3,500 = extreme. High CAPE + trigger + moisture = thunderstorm. Capping inversion can hold CAPE until explosive release.
key_facts: CAPE: Convective Available Potential Energy — integrated buoyancy of rising parcel from LFC to EL
Higher CAPE = more energy for updrafts = stronger potential thunderstorms
<1,000 J/kg: weak convection. 1,000-2,500: moderate. 2,500-3,500: significant. >3,500: extreme
NZ: 1,000-2,000 J/kg considered high — rarely reaches tropical extremes
CAPE necessary but not sufficient — also need trigger (lift) and moisture
Capping inversion suppresses CAPE release — when cap breaks, explosive thunderstorm development

syllabus_desc: Describe the international cloud classification system and the four cloud families.
concept: Clouds are classified by the altitude of their base and by their physical form — a system developed by Luke Howard in the early 19th century and formalised by the World Meteorological Organisation. The four families are: high clouds (bases above 20,000 ft in mid-latitudes), comprising cirrus, cirrostratus, and cirrocumulus; middle clouds (bases 6,500-20,000 ft), comprising altostratus and altocumulus; low clouds (bases below 6,500 ft), comprising stratus, stratocumulus, and nimbostratus; and clouds with vertical development, comprising cumulus and cumulonimbus which may have bases in the low cloud range but tops extending into the middle or high cloud levels. The basic form descriptors are: stratus (layer, from the Latin for 'spread out'), cumulus (heap, Latin for 'heap or pile'), cirrus (high, wispy, from Latin for 'curl of hair'), and nimbus (rain-producing). Combinations of these give the full genus names: nimbostratus (layer rain cloud), cumulonimbus (towering convective rain cloud), altostratus (middle-level layer cloud), etc. In the tropics, the altitude ranges shift upward because the tropopause is higher. In polar regions they shift downward. For aviation, the most operationally significant clouds are cumulonimbus (all phases of flight), nimbostratus (IFR conditions, icing), and fog/low stratus (departure and approach).
exam_tip: 4 cloud families: high (>20,000 ft): Ci, Cs, Cc. Middle (6,500-20,000): As, Ac. Low (<6,500): St, Sc, Ns. Vertical development: Cu, Cb. Stratus=layer, Cumulus=heap, Cirrus=high wispy, Nimbus=rain-producing.
key_facts: High clouds (>20,000 ft): Cirrus (Ci), Cirrostratus (Cs), Cirrocumulus (Cc)
Middle clouds (6,500-20,000 ft): Altostratus (As), Altocumulus (Ac)
Low clouds (<6,500 ft): Stratus (St), Stratocumulus (Sc), Nimbostratus (Ns)
Vertical development: Cumulus (Cu), Cumulonimbus (Cb) — base low, tops can be very high
Latin roots: Stratus=layer, Cumulus=heap, Cirrus=curl/high, Nimbus=rain
Altitude ranges shift upward in tropics, downward in polar regions

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of stratiform cloud.
concept: Stratiform cloud forms by the widespread, gentle lifting of stable air — the contrast to the vigorous vertical convection that produces cumuliform cloud. The three main mechanisms for forming stratiform cloud are: frontal lifting (warm air is forced to rise gradually over a cold air mass at a warm front, producing a characteristic sequence of cloud types spreading ahead of the surface front — cirrus thinning to cirrostratus, then altostratus, then nimbostratus with rain); orographic lifting (moist stable air forced gently over high ground produces layer cloud that can persist for extended periods on windward slopes and coasts); and mixing and turbulence in a stable, saturated boundary layer (sea fog advected inland can lift to become low stratus; nocturnal cooling can produce fog or stratus in valley floors that lifts to become a low stratus deck by morning). Stratiform cloud is characterised by: horizontal extent — it can cover thousands of square kilometres as a uniform layer; gradual bases — not sharply defined; precipitation if thick enough (nimbostratus produces continuous rain or snow); moderate to poor visibility both beneath the cloud in rain and within the cloud itself; and a monotonous appearance that is no less hazardous than the dramatic cumuliform variety. IFR conditions beneath stratiform cloud can persist for days in some NZ situations, particularly in southerly air streams or in stagnant anticyclones with radiation fog.
exam_tip: Stratiform cloud: stable air, widespread gentle lift. Mechanisms: frontal lift, orographic lift, boundary layer mixing/cooling. Characteristics: extensive horizontal coverage, gradual bases, continuous precipitation (if thick), prolonged IFR. Not dramatic but can persist for days.
key_facts: Stratiform cloud: stable air lifted gently over wide area
Formation mechanisms: frontal lifting, orographic lifting, boundary layer cooling/mixing
Warm front cloud sequence: Ci → Cs → As → Ns (spreads ahead of front)
Extensive horizontal coverage — can persist for days
Produces continuous rain or snow if thick enough (nimbostratus)
IFR conditions beneath can be prolonged — particularly in southerly or stagnant anticyclone

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of cumuliform cloud.
concept: Cumuliform cloud forms by buoyant convection — unstable air rising in thermals or forced upward by surface heating, orographic lifting, or convergence zones. The key difference from stratiform cloud is the vigour and vertical extent of the vertical motion. The typical development sequence: surface heating creates thermals that lift surface air to the LCL where cumulus humilis (small, flat-bottomed, cauliflower-topped cumulus) form. As heating intensifies through the afternoon, the cumulus grow vertically into cumulus mediocris and then cumulus congestus (towering cumulus, sometimes called 'TCu'). If the atmosphere is sufficiently unstable and moisture is sufficient, cumulus congestus can break through the capping level and develop into cumulonimbus with the familiar anvil top. The anvil forms as the rising air hits the tropopause and spreads out horizontally in the stable stratosphere. Cumuliform cloud is characterised by: sharply-defined flat bases (all at the LCL); vigorous vertical development with cauliflower tops; turbulence inside and nearby; showery precipitation (heavy and localised); and brief duration compared to stratiform cloud (individual cells may last 30-60 minutes). Good visibility outside the cloud but dangerous inside — turbulence, icing, and lightning risk all present in cumulonimbus.
exam_tip: Cumuliform cloud: unstable air, vigorous convection. Sequence: Cu humilis → Cu mediocris → Cu congestus (TCu) → Cb. Flat bases at LCL. Anvil = cloud hitting tropopause. Sharp bases, cauliflower tops, showery precipitation, turbulence. Good vis outside, hazardous inside Cb.
key_facts: Cumuliform cloud: unstable air rising in buoyant thermals or forced uplift
Development sequence: Cu humilis → Cu mediocris → Cu congestus (TCu) → Cb
Flat, sharply defined bases at LCL; cauliflower/billowing tops
Anvil top forms as rising air spreads at tropopause (stable stratosphere)
Showery precipitation: heavy and localised; brief duration per cell (30-60 min typical)
Hazards inside Cb: severe turbulence, heavy icing, lightning, possible hail

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and formation of cirrus and other high cloud.
concept: Cirrus is the quintessential high cloud — thin, wispy, fibrous strands of ice crystals at altitudes above 20,000 ft (and often above 30,000 ft in the tropics). It forms where the atmosphere at high altitude becomes saturated with respect to ice — either by gradual uplift ahead of a warm front, by outflow from thunderstorm anvils, by jet stream activity generating wave or turbulence, or by aircraft contrails expanding. The key characteristic is that cirrus is composed entirely of ice crystals, giving it a silky, fibrous appearance and sometimes producing optical phenomena like halos and sun dogs. Cirrostratus is cirrus that has spread out into a thin, milky veil covering much or all of the sky — it produces a characteristic halo around the sun or moon and is a classic warm front precursor (the 22° halo). Cirrocumulus is rare — a fine, white, granular appearance ('mackerel sky') at high level. High cloud typically affects aviation less severely than low or middle cloud, but: cirrus layers can produce significant clear-air icing in jet aircraft, especially when supersaturated ice crystal layers are present; anvil cirrus associated with thunderstorms extends far (100+ nm) downwind of the storm and indicates associated turbulence; and cirrostratus is a reliable precursor to deteriorating weather.
exam_tip: Cirrus: ice crystals >20,000 ft, wispy/fibrous. Cirrostratus: thin veil → 22° halo (warm front precursor). Cirrocumulus: mackerel sky. High cloud = ice crystals only. Anvil Ci extends 100+ nm from Cb. Cirrostratus halo = deteriorating weather coming.
key_facts: Cirrus: ice crystals above 20,000 ft, thin wispy streaks — fibrous appearance
Formed by: warm front uplift, Cb anvil outflow, jet stream waves, expanding contrails
Cirrostratus: thin veil of ice cloud producing 22° halo — classic warm front precursor
Cirrocumulus: granular high cloud — 'mackerel sky' — rare
All high cloud = ice crystals only (not supercooled water)
Anvil cirrus extends 100+ nm downwind of parent Cb — indicates associated turbulence

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and aviation significance of altocumulus and altostratus.
concept: Altostratus (As) is a grey or bluish-grey layer cloud of the middle level (6,500-20,000 ft), composed of water droplets and ice crystals. It looks like a thick, featureless grey sheet — you can often see the sun through thin altostratus as a pale disc (no halo, which distinguishes it from cirrostratus). Altostratus thickens as a warm front approaches, eventually obscuring the sun completely and merging into the lower nimbostratus where continuous rain begins. For aviation: altostratus means you're flying in IFR-transitional conditions; icing risk is significant in the liquid and mixed-phase portions; and turbulence is light to moderate from the lifting mechanism. Altocumulus (Ac) is a middle-level heap cloud appearing as patches, sheets, or layers of white or grey cloudlets — larger than cirrocumulus (you can see them with the naked eye clearly), with some shading in the larger elements. Altocumulus castellanus (Acc) is a significant type: it has turret-like tops rising from a common base, indicating instability in the middle troposphere. Acc in the morning is a reliable indicator of afternoon thunderstorm development — the instability at mid-level that produces Acc will intensify with surface heating. Altocumulus lenticularis (wave cloud) forms at mid-levels in the crests of mountain waves and is lens-shaped or almond-shaped.
exam_tip: Altostratus: grey featureless middle-level layer, sun = pale disc (no halo). Thick As → Ns transition, continuous rain. Icing risk. Altocumulus castellanus (Acc) in morning = afternoon thunderstorm warning. Altocumulus lenticularis = mountain wave cloud.
key_facts: Altostratus: grey/blue-grey middle-level layer; sun visible as pale disc (no halo — unlike Cs)
Thickening As → Ns = approaching warm front; icing risk in liquid/mixed-phase portions
Altocumulus: patches/layers of white/grey cloudlets; larger than Cc (visible with naked eye)
Altocumulus castellanus (Acc): turret-like tops = mid-level instability
Acc in morning = afternoon thunderstorm development likely (mid-level instability + surface heating)
Altocumulus lenticularis: lens-shaped, stationary — mountain wave cloud

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and aviation significance of stratus and stratocumulus.
concept: Stratus (St) is the lowest and most operationally impactful of the layer clouds — a uniform grey layer with a fairly well-defined base that produces drizzle or light rain. It forms by the cooling of a moist layer near the surface — either by radiative cooling overnight (radiation stratus/fog lifts to stratus in the morning), by advection of warm moist air over a cold surface (sea fog advected over land), or by turbulent mixing in a moist boundary layer that lifts the saturation level to a low altitude. Bases can be anywhere from just above the surface to a few thousand feet. For pilots, stratus and low cloud are the number-one cause of inadvertent IMC in VFR flight — it looks like there's still plenty of room and then suddenly there isn't. Stratocumulus (Sc) is the most common cloud type globally — a low-level cloud in broken or overcast lumpy rolls or patches, white to grey, with clear breaks between elements. It typically has a base between 1,000-6,500 ft. In NZ, stratocumulus forms readily in the boundary layer under anticyclones (especially in winter) and in onshore flows from the Tasman and Pacific. It is usually benign — light icing risk, light to moderate turbulence from mixing, and a base that fluctuates with heating. In frontal zones, stratocumulus can become extensive and lower significantly ahead of approaching fronts.
exam_tip: Stratus: uniform grey layer, drizzle or light rain, forms by surface cooling/advection — can base to just above surface. #1 VFR inadvertent IMC hazard. Stratocumulus: most common cloud globally, lumpy, 1,000-6,500 ft, benign but restricts VFR. Anticyclone + boundary layer = Sc.
key_facts: Stratus: uniform grey layer, lowest cloud type, drizzle or light rain
Formation: radiative cooling, moist air advection over cold surface, boundary layer mixing
Bases can be at surface (fog) to a few thousand feet — can lower to zero visibility
VFR hazard: gradual encroachment — #1 cause of inadvertent IMC for VFR pilots
Stratocumulus: most common cloud globally — lumpy rolls/patches, 1,000-6,500 ft base
Sc common in NZ anticyclones and onshore flows — base fluctuates with diurnal heating

syllabus_desc: Describe the three stages of cumulonimbus development: cumulus stage.
concept: A cumulonimbus goes through a well-defined life cycle of three stages. The cumulus (or developing) stage begins with the growth of a cumulus cloud from a thermal — a rising column of warm, moist air. In this initial stage, the dominant feature is a vigorous updraft throughout the entire cloud. The updraft can reach 20-30 kt in a moderate storm and the cloud grows rapidly upward, with the characteristic cauliflower appearance of a towering cumulus congestus. There is no precipitation reaching the ground during this stage — all precipitation is held aloft by the updraft. The entire cloud interior is ascending. Updraft speeds increase with height through the cloud. The cloud top grows at a visible rate, sometimes several thousand feet per minute in explosive development. From the ground, you can watch the cumulus congestus evolve in real time. This stage lasts approximately 10-20 minutes before the storm transitions to the mature stage. From an aviation perspective: the cumulus stage is the warning sign. A rapidly growing TCu should be treated with respect — it may become a significant thunderstorm within 20-30 minutes. In NZ summer, the transition from TCu to Cb can happen very quickly in high-CAPE environments. Do not position yourself beneath or downwind of a rapidly growing cumulus congestus.
exam_tip: Cumulus stage: all UPDRAFT throughout, no precipitation at surface (held aloft), rapidly growing TCu. Lasts ~10-20 min. Warning: TCu growing rapidly = mature Cb in 20-30 min. Do not fly beneath or downwind.
key_facts: Cumulus stage: entirely updraft throughout the cloud
No precipitation reaching ground — all held aloft by updraft
Updraft speeds: 20-30 kt in moderate storm, higher in severe
Rapid upward growth — cloud top may ascend thousands of feet per minute
Lasts approximately 10-20 minutes
Aviation warning: rapidly growing TCu = Cb developing — maintain clearance

syllabus_desc: Describe the mature stage of cumulonimbus development.
concept: The mature stage is the most intense and hazardous phase of the thunderstorm. It begins when precipitation reaches the surface for the first time — a reliable indicator of mature stage onset. The key feature is the coexistence of both updraft and downdraft within the cloud. The precipitation that was building in the cumulus stage has grown heavy enough to drag air down with it, forming the downdraft. This downdraft of cool, moist, precipitation-laden air descends through the cloud and spreads out at the surface as the gust front. Meanwhile, the updraft — now reaching speeds of 40-100+ kt in a severe storm — continues drawing warm moist air upward. At upper levels, the rising air spreads out at the tropopause forming the characteristic anvil. The mature stage is when all the severe weather occurs: heavy to extreme precipitation (possibly hail), frequent lightning (caused by charge separation between updraft and downdraft regions), severe to extreme turbulence (adjacent updraft and downdraft creating severe windshear internally), severe icing (mix of liquid water and ice crystals throughout), and possible tornadoes. The gust front at the surface can produce a sudden wind shift and speed increase (microburst, macroburst) well ahead of the main precipitation. Mature stage lasts 20-40 minutes. The anvil cirrus is spreading out kilometres ahead of the storm — indicating the approximate location of turbulence and icing aloft.
exam_tip: Mature stage: precipitation reaches surface (indicator), BOTH updraft AND downdraft. Most hazardous — all severe weather here: heavy rain/hail, lightning, severe turbulence, severe icing, gust front, possible tornadoes. Lasts 20-40 min. Anvil spreading = turbulence/icing aloft.
key_facts: Mature stage begins: precipitation first reaches surface
Both updraft AND downdraft coexist — most intense stage
Updraft: 40-100+ kt in severe storms. Downdraft forms gust front at surface
All severe weather: extreme precipitation, hail, lightning, severe turbulence, severe icing
Gust front: cool surface outflow from downdraft — wind shift/speed increase well ahead of storm
Lasts 20-40 minutes. Anvil extends 100+ nm downwind indicating turbulence/icing

syllabus_desc: Describe the dissipating stage of cumulonimbus development.
concept: The dissipating stage occurs when the downdraft spreads throughout the entire cloud interior, cutting off the warm moist inflow that was feeding the updraft. Without a supply of warm, moist air from below, the updraft weakens and eventually ceases. The cloud is now dominated entirely by downdraft and the remaining precipitation that was held aloft drains out. The anvil may persist long after the vigorous cloud tower has dissipated, as high-level ice crystal cloud spreads over a large area. The dissipating stage is characterised by: weakening precipitation (often just light rain or drizzle at the surface), weakening and eventually absent lightning, weakening turbulence (though light turbulence may persist), and a gradual decrease in cloud tops. The cloud takes on a ragged, soft appearance rather than the hard cauliflower texture of the mature stage. A single-cell thunderstorm dissipates completely in about 45 minutes to 1 hour from initiation to final dissipation. However, for multicell and supercell storms, new cells can develop adjacent to the dissipating cell, triggered by the gust front, resulting in a storm system that persists for hours. For aviation: a dissipating Cb is significantly less dangerous than a mature one, but residual turbulence, icing in the anvil, and lightning risk remain. An apparently 'dying' storm should still be circumnavigated by a safe margin.
exam_tip: Dissipating stage: downdraft throughout entire cloud, updraft cutoff. Weakening precipitation, lightning, turbulence. Single cell: total life ~45-60 min. Anvil persists long after cell dissipates. Multicell: new cells triggered by gust front — storm can persist hours.
key_facts: Dissipating stage: downdraft takes over throughout — cuts off warm inflow from below
Updraft ceases, anvil persists but vigorous tower collapses
Weakening: precipitation, lightning, turbulence — ragged cloud appearance
Single-cell total life: ~45-60 minutes from initiation to dissipation
Multicell: gust front triggers new cells adjacent — storm system can persist for hours
Dissipating Cb: still hazardous — maintain safe circumnavigation distance

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of nimbostratus and its aviation significance.
concept: Nimbostratus (Ns) is the classic 'bad weather' cloud — a thick, dark grey, featureless layer that produces continuous, moderate to heavy rain or snow. It is a deep cloud, often extending from near the surface to 10,000-15,000 ft or more, and is associated with warm fronts and occluded fronts. Unlike cumulonimbus, nimbostratus doesn't have the dramatic vertical structure and violent convection — the precipitation it produces is steady and continuous rather than intense and showery. For aviation: nimbostratus is one of the most practically challenging clouds. Its depth means you will be in IMC from soon after entering the cloud base through to well above typical flight levels for light aircraft. The entire depth of nimbostratus is a significant icing environment — the cloud contains both supercooled water droplets and ice crystals throughout. The rain below nimbostratus reduces visibility significantly, and that rain may be falling through a cold layer, causing it to freeze on contact with aircraft surfaces (freezing rain — the most severe icing hazard). Turbulence in nimbostratus is typically light to moderate — there is lifting but it's gentle and widespread. Precipitation under Ns can obscure the ground in rain and low cloud right to the surface. IFR conditions often persist for 12-24 hours in a well-developed frontal nimbostratus situation.
exam_tip: Nimbostratus: thick dark grey layer, continuous moderate-heavy rain/snow, warm/occluded front. Deep cloud (surface to 10,000-15,000 ft). Significant icing throughout. Risk of freezing rain below. Light-moderate turbulence. IFR for 12-24+ hrs in frontal Ns.
key_facts: Nimbostratus: thick, dark grey featureless layer cloud with continuous moderate-heavy precipitation
Associated with warm and occluded fronts
Depth: near-surface to 10,000-15,000 ft — entire depth = IMC + icing environment
Icing: supercooled water + ice crystals throughout the cloud
Freezing rain hazard: rain falling through cold air below cloud can freeze on contact
IFR conditions can persist 12-24 hours in frontal Ns

syllabus_desc: Describe the types and aviation hazards of in-cloud turbulence.
concept: Turbulence inside cloud depends entirely on the cloud type. In stratiform cloud (stratus, nimbostratus, altostratus), turbulence is typically light to moderate, caused by the gentle lifting mechanism and boundary layer mixing. Unpleasant but generally not structurally threatening. In cumuliform cloud, turbulence is a different matter. Inside a cumulus congestus or cumulonimbus, updraft and downdraft forces can reach tens to hundreds of knots with extreme spatial gradients between adjacent up and down cells. A standard CAT (Clear Air Turbulence) classification scale applies: light (slight erratic changes in attitude/altitude, comfortable for passengers), moderate (definite strains against restraint, unsecured objects move), severe (large abrupt changes in altitude/attitude, may cause momentary loss of control), extreme (aircraft practically impossible to control, may cause structural damage). Inside an active Cb, extreme turbulence is possible without warning. The most hazardous zone is the interface between updraft and downdraft cores. Turbulence can also be encountered: in clear air beneath the base of a Cb (from gust front outflow); in the anvil (severe clear-air turbulence from the shear zone at cloud top); and adjacent to but outside the cloud in the compensating downflow around vigorous Cu/Cb. The practical rule: maintain at least 5 nm lateral and 5,000 ft vertical clearance from any active Cb.
exam_tip: In-cloud turbulence: stratiform = light to moderate. Cumuliform = severe to extreme possible. Scale: light/moderate/severe/extreme. Extreme Cb turbulence = possible structural damage. Hazardous zones: updraft/downdraft interface, anvil, gust front outflow, clear air beside active Cb.
key_facts: Stratiform cloud turbulence: light to moderate — caused by gentle lifting mechanism
Cumuliform turbulence: severe to extreme — updraft/downdraft shear zones
Turbulence scale: light, moderate, severe, extreme (severe = possible loss of control)
Extreme turbulence in Cb can cause structural damage
Hazardous zones: updraft/downdraft interface, anvil, gust front below cloud, clear air adjacent to Cb
Clearance: maintain 5 nm lateral and 5,000 ft vertical from active Cb

syllabus_desc: Describe orographic cloud and wave cloud formation and their aviation significance.
concept: Orographic cloud forms when moist air is forced to rise over terrain. The simplest form is cap cloud or banner cloud — a cloud that forms on the windward side of a peak and streams off the lee side, appearing stationary while air moves through it at speed. The cap cloud can descend very low on high peaks and restrict VFR flight through mountain passes. Orographic stratus forms when gentle stable uplift of moist air produces a persistent layer cloud draped over hills and ranges — common in NZ's ranges in onshore northwesterly and northeasterly flows. Mountain wave cloud (altocumulus lenticularis) forms at the crests of standing waves downwind of barriers in stable, moist conditions. The lenticular cloud is stationary-looking despite the air flowing through it rapidly. It's lens-shaped or almond-shaped and has very smooth edges (unlike the cauliflower tops of convective cloud). Multiple wave crests may produce multiple lenticular layers stacked at different altitudes. For aviation: orographic cloud restricts VFR flight over and through mountain areas. It can produce icing. The cap cloud may extend through a pass at zero visibility. In NZ, the Alps, Kaimanawa ranges, Tararua, Rimutaka, and Coromandel Peninsula all produce significant orographic cloud. The fundamental hazard is that terrain below the cloud can be invisible — VFR into orographic cloud has been fatal for NZ pilots repeatedly.
exam_tip: Orographic cloud: moist air forced over terrain. Cap cloud: over peaks — appears stationary, air moves through at speed. Orographic stratus: draped over ranges. Wave cloud (lenticular): stationary, smooth-edged, at wave crests. VFR hazard: terrain invisible below cloud.
key_facts: Orographic cloud: forms when moist air is forced to rise over terrain
Cap cloud: forms on windward peak side, streams off lee — appears stationary, icing risk
Orographic stratus: persistent layer draped over ranges in stable onshore flow
Lenticular (wave) cloud: smooth, lens-shaped, stationary-looking at wave crests
Multiple wave crests = multiple stacked lenticular layers
NZ VFR hazard: terrain beneath orographic cloud is invisible — fatal accident history

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of fog and its classification.
concept: Fog is essentially cloud with its base at the surface — cloud in which visibility is reduced to less than 1,000 metres (IMO definition). In aviation, the critical thresholds are: fog (visibility <1,000 m), thick fog (<200 m), and dense fog (<50 m). The classification of fog types is based on the formation mechanism. Radiation fog forms by overnight radiative cooling of the surface and the air immediately above it, until the dew point is reached. It needs clear skies, light winds, and moist air — the classic anticyclonic night. Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves over a cooler surface and is chilled to its dew point from below — sea fog is the classic example. Orographic or upslope fog forms when moist air rises over terrain until cooled to saturation — effectively orographic cloud with a base at the terrain surface. Steam fog (sea smoke) forms when cold air moves over warm water and rapid evaporation produces visible wisps above the water surface. Frontal fog forms in precipitation zones where rain falling into cool, near-saturated air produces further evaporation and eventual saturation at the surface. For NZ: radiation fog is most common in autumn and winter in inland basins (Waikato, Mackenzie Basin, Marlborough valleys). Advection fog (sea fog) affects coastal aerodromes, particularly on the east coasts in spring when seas are still cold.
exam_tip: Fog: visibility <1,000 m. Types: radiation (overnight cooling, clear sky, light wind), advection (warm moist air over cold surface — sea fog), orographic/upslope (rising moist air), steam fog (cold air over warm water), frontal (precipitation zone). NZ: radiation fog inland autumn/winter; sea fog east coasts spring.
key_facts: Fog: cloud with base at surface, visibility <1,000 m (aviation definition)
Radiation fog: overnight radiative cooling, clear sky, light wind, moist air — common inland NZ
Advection fog: warm moist air over cold surface — sea fog on NZ east coasts in spring
Orographic fog: moist air forced up terrain, base at surface — mountain passes
Steam fog: cold air over warm water — evaporation produces wisps above surface
Frontal fog: precipitation zone evaporation saturates near-surface air

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, characteristics, and dispersal of radiation fog.
concept: Radiation fog is the most common type in inland New Zealand and is particularly significant for morning operations at aerodromes in the Waikato, Canterbury foothills, Marlborough, and Central Otago. Formation: on a clear night with light winds and moist surface air, the ground cools rapidly by emitting longwave radiation to space. The air in direct contact with the ground cools conductively, lowering its temperature to the dew point. The fog forms from the surface upward and deepens through the night and into the pre-dawn hours. The key ingredients: clear sky (clouds act as a radiation blanket, preventing surface cooling), light winds (2-5 kt ideal — enough mixing to deepen the fog layer but not enough to destroy the surface inversion), and moist surface air. Strong winds disrupt the temperature inversion and prevent fog formation. No wind at all prevents the mixing needed to deepen the fog from a thin surface frost layer into a significant fog bank. Dispersal: radiation fog typically disperses in the morning as solar radiation heats the surface. The fog 'lifts' — it doesn't actually disappear, it rises as stratus as the surface inversion is destroyed from below. Once it lifts above about 1,000 ft AGL it is typically classified as low stratus rather than fog. Full dispersal requires sufficient solar heating — in mid-winter, thick radiation fog may persist all day, particularly in sheltered valleys where the sun angle is low.
exam_tip: Radiation fog: clear sky + light winds (2-5 kt) + moist air + overnight cooling. Deepens toward dawn. Dispersal: solar heating lifts it to stratus, then dissipates. Mid-winter fog can persist all day. NZ: Waikato, Marlborough, Central Otago most affected.
key_facts: Radiation fog: clear sky, light winds (2-5 kt), moist surface air, calm night
Forms at surface, deepens upward through the night
No wind = no mixing (surface frost only). Strong wind = inversion destroyed (no fog)
Dispersal: solar heating destroys surface inversion — fog lifts to low stratus then dissipates
Mid-winter: thick fog may persist all day in sheltered valleys with low sun angle
NZ locations: Waikato, Canterbury foothills, Marlborough valleys, Central Otago

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, characteristics, and aviation significance of advection fog.
concept: Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves horizontally over a colder surface, chilling the air from below until it reaches its dew point. It differs fundamentally from radiation fog in that it can form in any wind strength, does not require clear skies, and does not require darkness — it can form in the middle of the day if the advected air is warm enough and the surface cold enough. The most common form is sea fog, where warm, moist subtropical air moves over cold ocean upwelling zones or cold coastal waters. On the east coast of New Zealand's North Island, particularly in spring and early summer, cool southerly sea currents keep sea surface temperatures low while the air is warmer and moist — ideal advection fog conditions. This sea fog is then advected onto the coast by onshore winds and can penetrate 20-50 km inland. For coastal aerodromes like Napier, Gisborne, and Wellington, sea fog arriving with an onshore flow is a significant operational hazard because it can arrive rapidly — a CAVOK aerodrome can go to zero visibility in 20-30 minutes. Advection fog is typically thicker and more persistent than radiation fog — it doesn't require solar heating to maintain it, and it won't necessarily dissipate at midday. Dispersal occurs when wind direction changes (warmer or drier air brought in), wind speed increases sufficiently to mix it out, or when the advection ceases.
exam_tip: Advection fog: warm moist air over cold surface. Can form any time, day or night, any wind strength. Sea fog most common — NZ east coasts spring/summer. Arrives rapidly — CAVOK to zero visibility in 20-30 min. Persistent — won't clear with solar heating alone.
key_facts: Advection fog: warm moist air moving over cold surface — chilled to dew point from below
Can form day or night, in any wind strength, without clear sky
Sea fog: subtropical warm air over cold coastal upwelling zones
NZ: east coast North Island spring/summer — cold SST + warm moist air
Arrival at coast: rapid — CAVOK to near-zero in 20-30 min
Persistent — not cleared by solar heating alone; requires wind change or cessation of advection

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of wind on fog formation and dispersal.
concept: Wind has a critical but non-linear relationship with fog formation and dispersal. For radiation fog: the optimum wind speed for formation is 2-5 knots. At this speed, gentle turbulent mixing spreads the cooling effect of the surface through a deeper layer — creating a significant fog bank rather than just a thin surface frost. If winds are calm, the cooling is confined to a very shallow surface layer, producing heavy frost or a very thin surface fog that barely reaches knee height. If winds exceed about 7-10 knots, the turbulent mixing is strong enough to erode the surface temperature inversion — the temperature gradient needed for fog to form is destroyed. So both calm and strong winds prevent fog formation; light winds enable it. For advection fog, the relationship differs: some wind is necessary to advect the warm moist air over the cold surface, but once the fog has formed, increasing wind speed can deepen it (more mixing through the moist layer) and also eventually disperse it (if wind brings in drier air or is strong enough to mix the fog out). For dispersal of existing fog: light to moderate winds (15-20 kt) will often mix the fog out if the air above the fog layer is significantly drier or warmer. Very strong winds destroy any inversion and mix the fog vigorously. Wind direction change bringing in drier or warmer air is often more effective at fog dispersal than wind speed alone.
exam_tip: Radiation fog optimal wind: 2-5 kt. Calm = frost only (no mixing). >7-10 kt = inversion destroyed (no fog). Advection fog needs some wind to develop. Dispersal: wind change to drier/warmer air most effective. Strong winds mix fog out. Fog formation is highly wind-speed sensitive.
key_facts: Radiation fog: optimal formation wind 2-5 kt (enough mixing, not enough to destroy inversion)
Calm: only surface frost — no mixing to deepen fog
Wind >7-10 kt: turbulence destroys surface inversion — radiation fog cannot form
Advection fog: requires some wind to bring warm moist air over cold surface
Fog dispersal: wind direction change to drier/warmer air most effective
Strong winds (>15-20 kt) can mix and disperse existing fog if air aloft is drier

syllabus_desc: Describe the aviation hazards of cumulonimbus and the recommended pilot actions.
concept: Cumulonimbus is the single most dangerous meteorological hazard for aviation. The hazards are multiple and simultaneous: severe to extreme turbulence throughout the cloud, with updraft and downdraft velocities exceeding 100 kt in a severe storm — forces that can exceed the structural limits of any aircraft; severe icing from the abundant supercooled water droplets and the mixed-phase environment, which can accumulate ice at rates of 25-50 mm per minute in the worst cases; lightning — each Cb produces thousands of lightning discharges; the cloud has an electrostatic charge separation that is lethal to un-bonded aircraft and can induce transient voltage spikes in avionics; hail, which can be found in the updraft above the freezing level and in the downdraft as it descends, and hailstones up to 100 mm diameter have been recorded in Australian and NZ super-cell storms; windshear and microburst below the cloud base in the gust front zone — a rapidly descending column of cold air can produce a microburst of 80+ kt outflow winds at the surface, deadly to aircraft on approach or departure; heavy rain that can cause engine flame-out in extreme cases and reduces visibility to near-zero; and tornadoes associated with supercell CBs. No aircraft has immunity to all of these hazards. The recommended action is simple: circumnavigate all active CBs by at least 20 nm in any direction, and do not fly beneath the base of an active CB. If caught in a CB, maintain wings level, set a penetration speed, do not chase altitude — let the autopilot or trim take the structural loads.
exam_tip: Cb hazards: severe/extreme turbulence, severe icing, lightning, hail, microburst/windshear, heavy rain, tornadoes. Circumnavigate by 20 nm minimum. Do NOT fly beneath. If caught: wings level, penetration speed, don't chase altitude.
key_facts: Cb turbulence: severe to extreme — updraft/downdraft to 100+ kt, can exceed structural limits
Severe icing: abundant supercooled water, accumulation rates 25-50 mm/min in worst cases
Lightning: thousands of discharges per storm — avionics damage risk
Hail: up to 100+ mm diameter in supercell storms — at any altitude within storm
Microburst: downdraft outflow 80+ kt at surface — fatal windshear on approach/departure
Action: circumnavigate 20 nm minimum; if caught — wings level, penetration speed, don't chase altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and hazards of embedded thunderstorms.
concept: An embedded thunderstorm is a cumulonimbus that is embedded within, or concealed by, a surrounding mass of stratiform cloud — typically nimbostratus or altostratus. This is arguably more dangerous than an isolated CB in clear air, because the pilot cannot see the CB until they're in it, or in the case of an instrument approach in IMC, may not be able to detect it at all without airborne weather radar. Embedded CBs form when large-scale lifting (frontal or trough-related) creates widespread stratiform cloud, within which embedded convective elements develop. They are most common in the warm sector of a depression, in occluded fronts, and in the convergence zones of tropical weather systems. The visual indicators that would warn a VFR pilot are obscured by the surrounding cloud. For IFR flight, the risks are: penetrating a CB that was assumed to be non-convective cloud, with all the Cb hazards (turbulence, icing, lightning, hail) arriving without warning; the turbulence in embedded CBs can be severe to extreme even though the surrounding cloud is giving only moderate turbulence; and ice accumulation can be dramatically faster within the embedded CB than in the surrounding cloud. The safest approach: treat any region of active precipitation within frontal or large-scale cloud as potentially embedded CB, use airborne weather radar on its 'tilt' mode to identify the embedded cells, and deviate around them.
exam_tip: Embedded Cb: Cb hidden within surrounding stratiform cloud — most dangerous CB type because invisible. Warm sector, occlusions, tropical convergence zones. Airborne weather radar essential. Treat heavy precipitation areas in IMC as potentially embedded Cb.
key_facts: Embedded Cb: cumulonimbus concealed within surrounding stratiform cloud (Ns or As)
Cannot be seen visually — no warning before entering the Cb
Common in: warm sector of depressions, occluded fronts, tropical convergence zones
All Cb hazards present: extreme turbulence, severe icing, lightning, hail — without warning
Ice accumulation within embedded Cb much faster than surrounding cloud
Detection: airborne weather radar (tilt mode); treat heavy precipitation in IMC as potential embedded Cb

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and formation of squall lines and their aviation significance.
concept: A squall line is an organised line of thunderstorms, typically aligned parallel to a cold front or ahead of it. It forms when the cold front's outflow, combined with low-level wind shear, organises individual convective cells into a linear band. Squall lines can extend for hundreds to over a thousand kilometres, with the individual CB cells embedded along the line. They move as a coherent system rather than as individual cells. The leading edge of a squall line is often marked by a shelf cloud or arc cloud — a lowering of cloud at the base of the line's gust front — and the passage of a squall line brings an abrupt wind shift, a sudden and dramatic increase in wind speed, heavy precipitation, and possible hail. The transition from pre-squall clear air to the squall line itself can take minutes. For aviation, squall lines are one of the more insidious convective hazards because: they can block a route completely — there are no easy gaps; the cells within the line are continuously regenerating; the line may move at 40-60 kt, rapidly closing a previously usable gap; the weather on the far side of the line may be clear, tempting pilots to attempt a transit; and weather radar is essential for penetrating or circumnavigating a squall line safely. In NZ, squall lines are less common than in continental interiors but do occur with vigorous cold fronts.
exam_tip: Squall line: organised line of CBs ahead of or along cold front. Shelf/arc cloud at leading edge = gust front. Abrupt wind shift, heavy precipitation, hail on passage. No easy gaps — can block entire route. Move at 40-60 kt. Radar essential.
key_facts: Squall line: organised linear band of CBs ahead of or along a cold front
Can extend hundreds to 1,000+ km; cells continuously regenerating along the line
Leading edge: shelf cloud / arc cloud from gust front
Passage: abrupt wind shift, rapid wind speed increase, heavy precipitation, hail
Moves at 40-60 kt — gaps can close rapidly
Aviation: radar essential; do not attempt transit without complete picture

syllabus_desc: Classify the types of precipitation and describe their formation
concept: Precipitation forms when water droplets or ice crystals in clouds grow large enough to fall to Earth's surface. The main types are rain (liquid droplets >0.5 mm), drizzle (fine droplets <0.5 mm falling from stratus), snow (ice crystals that coalesce below 0 degrees C throughout), sleet (rain that refreezes en route, or a mix of rain and snow), freezing rain (rain that supercools and freezes on contact with sub-zero surfaces), and hail (ice pellets formed by repeated updraft cycling in Cb clouds). The Bergeron-Findeisen process explains most precipitation: ice crystals grow at the expense of supercooled water droplets, then aggregate or fall as snow, melting to rain if the air below 0 degrees C is warm enough. In NZ, orographic lifting on the Southern Alps and Tararua Range frequently triggers heavy precipitation, while freezing rain is a notable hazard in Southland and Canterbury during cold southerly outbreaks.
exam_tip: Know the difference between freezing rain (liquid that freezes on contact) and sleet (already frozen before reaching the surface) — they are frequently confused in exam questions.
key_facts: Rain: droplets >0.5 mm; drizzle: <0.5 mm, from stratus clouds
Hail forms in Cb updrafts — ice pellets grow with each updraft cycle
Freezing rain is supercooled liquid that freezes on impact — severe icing hazard
Snow requires sub-zero temperatures throughout the column (or partial melt = sleet)
Bergeron-Findeisen process: ice crystals grow faster than supercooled droplets at -10 to -20 degrees C
Orographic precipitation is common on NZ's west coasts and ranges

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of precipitation on aviation operations
concept: Precipitation affects aviation in several important ways. Reduced visibility in heavy rain, drizzle, or snow makes visual navigation and approach difficult and can drop below minima unexpectedly. Freezing rain and freezing drizzle deposit clear ice rapidly on all exposed surfaces — it is arguably the most dangerous icing condition because it can overwhelm aircraft protection systems faster than any other type. Hail in or near Cb clouds can cause structural damage to airframes, crack windscreens, and damage engines — even above cloud tops, hail can be ejected by strong updrafts. Heavy rain degrades engine performance in some piston engines through manifold flooding and reduces propeller efficiency. Standing water on runways leads to aquaplaning, degraded braking, and crosswind control difficulties. Finally, windshear associated with precipitation-cooled downdrafts (microbursts) presents a serious threat during approach and departure.
exam_tip: Freezing rain produces clear ice and is the most critical icing type — it can appear on the ground even before you depart if the surface is below 0 degrees C and rain is falling.
key_facts: Heavy rain/drizzle significantly reduces in-flight visibility
Freezing rain produces clear (glaze) ice at high rates — very hazardous
Hail causes structural damage — can occur above Cb cloud tops
Standing water causes aquaplaning and reduced braking effectiveness
Precipitation-driven microbursts cause severe low-level windshear
Heavy rain can enter carburettors and cause engine roughness or power loss

syllabus_desc: Describe the measurement and reporting of precipitation
concept: Precipitation intensity is reported in METARs and SPECIs using standard descriptors: light (-), moderate (no qualifier), or heavy (+). Drizzle is coded DZ, rain RA, snow SN, snow grains SG, ice pellets PL, hail GR (>5 mm), small hail/snow pellets GS (<5 mm), and freezing precipitation uses the prefix FZ (e.g. FZRA for freezing rain, FZDZ for freezing drizzle). Shower precipitation — convective and intermittent — carries the SH prefix (e.g. SHRA, SHSN). Thunderstorm precipitation uses the TS prefix (e.g. TSRA). Rainfall amounts are measured in mm using rain gauges; snowfall in cm. A simple but important rule: if precipitation is occurring at the reporting station, it appears in the weather group of the METAR. Forecasters also use area rainfall forecasts and significant weather charts to indicate where and what intensity precipitation is expected over NZ.
exam_tip: In METARs, FZ prefix = freezing, SH = shower, TS = thunderstorm — these can combine, so FZRA, SHRA, and TSRA are common exam codes.
key_facts: Light (-), moderate (no prefix), heavy (+) are the three intensity levels in METARs
FZRA = freezing rain; FZDZ = freezing drizzle — both are severe icing hazards
SH prefix = shower (convective); TS prefix = thunderstorm-associated
GR = hail (>5 mm); GS = small hail or snow pellets
Rainfall measured in mm; snowfall in cm depth or water equivalent
Precipitation at the station is encoded in METAR weather group

syllabus_desc: Define visibility and describe its measurement for aviation purposes
concept: Meteorological visibility is the greatest distance at which an observer can see and identify a prominent dark object against a bright background in daylight conditions. For aviation, the relevant measure is prevailing visibility — the greatest distance seen over at least half the horizon — which is reported in METARs in metres (up to 9999 m, where 9999 means 10 km or more). Runway Visual Range (RVR) is used at precision approach runways and measures the distance over which a pilot on the runway centreline can see the runway lights or surface markings; it is measured by transmissometers and reported in metres for Cat II/III operations. In NZ, ATIS broadcasts at controlled aerodromes include prevailing visibility, and pilots should cross-check this against any applicable minima before committing to an approach. Visibility is distinct from cloud base — you may have 10 km visibility with a 200 ft overcast, or 50 m visibility with a high cloudless sky.
exam_tip: Prevailing visibility is not the minimum — it is the greatest distance seen over at least half the horizon. Minimum visibility is reported separately as the lowest directional visibility if it differs significantly.
key_facts: Prevailing visibility: greatest distance seen over at least half the horizon
Reported in metres in METARs; 9999 = 10 km or more
RVR = Runway Visual Range, measured by transmissometers at precision runways
RVR is relevant for Cat II/III approaches and departure minima
Visibility and cloud base are separate — one can be high while the other is low
NZ METARs use 4-digit metre coding (e.g. 0800 = 800 m, 5000 = 5 km)

syllabus_desc: Define fog and describe the conditions required for its formation
concept: Fog is defined as a visibility restriction to less than 1000 metres caused by water droplets suspended in the air at ground level. When visibility is between 1000 m and 5000 m the condition is classified as mist. Fog forms when the surface air is cooled to its dew point — the temperature at which it becomes saturated — and condensation nuclei allow droplet formation. Three primary cooling mechanisms drive fog formation: radiative cooling of the surface on calm, clear nights (radiation fog); advection of warm moist air over a cool surface (advection fog); and adiabatic cooling as air rises up terrain (hill/upslope fog). Fog requires adequate moisture (relative humidity near 100%), a cooling mechanism, and usually light winds (calm to about 5 kt). Winds above around 15 kt tend to mix the surface layer and prevent fog formation, though strong winds can produce low stratus instead. In NZ, fog is most common in inland valleys of both islands, particularly the Waikato, Manawatu, and Canterbury Plains during autumn and winter.
exam_tip: The key distinction: fog = visibility <1000 m; mist = 1000–5000 m. Both involve water droplets at the surface, but fog meets IFR criteria at most aerodromes.
key_facts: Fog: visibility < 1000 m due to surface-level water droplets
Mist: visibility 1000–5000 m (same droplets, less dense)
Three ingredients: moisture, cooling to dew point, and condensation nuclei
Light winds (5 kt or less) favour fog; strong winds cause mixing and prevent fog
Most common in NZ inland valleys during autumn and winter
Can form rapidly at night as surface temperature falls through the dew point

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of haze, smoke, dust and other phenomena on visibility
concept: Visibility can be reduced by phenomena other than fog and precipitation. Haze is a suspension of extremely fine dry particles (dust, salt crystals, or smoke) that scatters light and reduces visibility without moisture — it often produces a characteristic yellowish or grey tinge to the sky. In NZ, haze from Australian dust storms crossing the Tasman occasionally reduces visibility significantly. Smoke from controlled burns, wildfires, or industrial sources creates localised visibility reductions that can be severe and patchy. Dust and sand (coded DU and SA in METARs) occur during dry, windy conditions, particularly in Canterbury and Hawke's Bay. Volcanic ash (VA) presents an extreme hazard to aviation — ash particles damage engines and airframes and can cause engine flameout. In NZ, volcanic activity from the Taupo Volcanic Zone is a real consideration; VAAC Wellington issues volcanic ash advisories for the region. METAR codes for these phenomena: HZ (haze), FU (smoke), DU (dust), SA (sand), VA (volcanic ash).
exam_tip: Haze is dry — no moisture involved. This distinguishes it from mist (water droplets). In METARs, HZ = haze, FU = smoke — both reduce visibility without precipitation.
key_facts: Haze: dry particles scattering light — no moisture; gives sky a grey/yellow tinge
Smoke (FU in METARs) from fires can produce very patchy, severe visibility reductions
Volcanic ash (VA) is extremely hazardous — damages engines and airframes
VAAC Wellington issues ash advisories for NZ volcanic zone eruptions
Dust and sand (DU/SA) occur in dry Canterbury and Hawke's Bay during strong winds
Haze does not show on weather radar; visibility effects can be underestimated

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, characteristics and dissipation of radiation fog
concept: Radiation fog forms on calm, clear nights when the Earth's surface loses heat rapidly by long-wave radiation, cooling the near-surface air to its dew point. Clear skies are essential — cloud cover acts like a blanket and prevents radiative cooling. Light winds (2–5 kt) help mix a slightly deeper fog layer; completely calm conditions produce a very shallow dew frost or light mist.
