The fog typically begins in valleys and low-lying areas after midnight and thickens toward dawn as the surface continues to cool. After sunrise, solar heating warms the surface and fog burns off — usually from the edges inward — dissipating by mid-morning in NZ summer but sometimes persisting all day in winter. Radiation fog can form very quickly once conditions are right, and can produce zero visibility in less than 30 minutes. Classic NZ locations: Waikato, Canterbury Plains, Manawatu, and Hawke's Bay inland valleys on autumn nights after clear days.
exam_tip: Radiation fog requires calm/light winds, clear skies, and high moisture — it forms overnight and dissipates after sunrise. If any of those three conditions is missing, radiation fog cannot form.
key_facts: Forms on calm, clear nights — radiative surface cooling to dew point
Light winds (2–5 kt) help deepen the layer; calm = very shallow or dew only
Begins in valleys and hollows after midnight; thickest near dawn
Dissipates after sunrise as solar heating warms surface from below
Can develop very rapidly — zero visibility in under 30 minutes
Most common in NZ inland valleys in autumn and winter

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, characteristics and dissipation of advection fog
concept: Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves horizontally over a cooler surface — the air is cooled to its dew point from below. Unlike radiation fog, it does not require calm conditions or clear skies, and can occur in moderate winds (up to about 15 kt). It is persistent and can form over wide areas very quickly. Common NZ examples include: moist northerly flows advecting over the cold Southland or Fiordland coastal waters in winter, and warm Australian air crossing the cooler Tasman Sea before arriving over coastal areas. Sea fog — a type of advection fog — forms when warm humid air flows from warm to cold ocean currents. Advection fog does not dissipate after sunrise the way radiation fog does, because it is continuously fed by the moving air mass. Dissipation requires a change in wind direction, drying of the source air mass, or the surface warming sufficiently to eliminate the temperature contrast.
exam_tip: Advection fog can occur in winds up to ~15 kt and does not disappear at sunrise — this is the key difference from radiation fog, which requires calm and dissipates with solar heating.
key_facts: Warm moist air advects over a cooler surface — cooled to dew point from below
Can occur in moderate winds (up to ~15 kt)
Does not clear at sunrise — is fed continuously by the moving air mass
Sea fog is a form of advection fog common in NZ coastal areas
Widespread coverage possible — can affect hundreds of kilometres of coastline
Dissipates when wind direction changes or temperature contrast is removed

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, characteristics and dissipation of frontal fog
concept: Frontal fog (also called precipitation fog or warm-front fog) forms ahead of a warm front where warm rain falls from aloft into cooler, drier sub-frontal air. The rain saturates and cools the surface air, sometimes producing fog over a wide area in advance of the front. It can also form in the frontal zone itself where mixing of the warm and cold air masses creates saturation. Frontal fog is associated with deteriorating weather, so it often occurs in combination with other hazards — low cloud, rain, and reducing visibility simultaneously. In NZ, frontal fog ahead of northwest-moving fronts arriving from the Tasman is a recognised hazard for the lower South Island and Southland. Unlike radiation fog, frontal fog is not confined to calm nights — it can form any time the frontal weather pattern is in place. It dissipates once the front passes and the air mass changes.
exam_tip: Frontal fog is caused by warm rain evaporating into and saturating cooler surface air ahead of a warm front — it is not radiation or advection fog, and it occurs with frontal weather, not clear nights.
key_facts: Forms when warm rain falls into cooler drier air ahead of a warm front
Can cover wide areas ahead of and at the frontal boundary
Occurs in combination with cloud, rain, and other frontal hazards
Not restricted to calm conditions — can form in moderate winds
Common in southern NZ ahead of fronts moving up from the Southern Ocean
Clears once the front passes and air mass changes

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of hill fog and upslope fog
concept: Hill fog forms when low cloud envelops hills and mountains — technically the cloud base is at or below terrain level. From the ground, it appears as fog; from the air, it is low stratus or stratocumulus capping the ridgelines. Upslope fog is a closely related phenomenon where moist air is forced to rise up a slope, cools adiabatically, and reaches saturation — forming cloud/fog on the windward face of terrain. Both are common in NZ given its mountainous terrain and prevailing moist westerly airflow. The Tararua, Ruahine, and Kaimai Ranges frequently have their tops obscured by upslope fog in northwesterly or westerly flows, and the ranges around Queenstown and Wanaka are regularly affected in southerly flows. Hill fog is not forecast as easily as valley fog and can form rapidly when moist low-level airflow encounters terrain. Pilots operating VFR near ranges must be alert to rapidly rising terrain with cloud sitting on the tops.
exam_tip: Hill fog is cloud at terrain level — not a different type of fog meteorologically, but it has serious implications for terrain clearance. It can form rapidly when the cloud base drops to ridge height.
key_facts: Hill fog = low cloud at terrain level — the cloud base is at or below the ridgeline
Upslope fog forms as moist air rises adiabatically and cools to dew point
Common on NZ's many mountain ranges in moist westerly or southerly flows
Can form rapidly as the cloud base drops — particularly hazardous for VFR pilots
Windward slopes experience upslope fog; leeward slopes usually clear
Not easily distinguished from radiation or advection fog from the air

syllabus_desc: Describe the atmospheric conditions required for airframe icing
concept: Airframe icing occurs when an aircraft passes through visible moisture (cloud, rain, drizzle, or fog) at temperatures at or below 0 degrees C. Two ingredients are essential: temperature at or below freezing AND visible water droplets. The water must be in liquid (or supercooled liquid) form — flying through ice crystals alone at altitude generally does not cause accretion on the airframe, though it can block pitot systems. Supercooled water droplets — droplets that remain liquid below 0 degrees C due to the absence of suitable freezing nuclei — are the primary cause of in-flight icing. They exist mainly between 0 and -20 degrees C, with the highest concentration of supercooled liquid water (SLW) typically found between 0 and -10 degrees C. The risk of severe icing is greatest in this band. In NZ, icing conditions are common in frontal cloud, orographic cloud over the Alps and ranges, and in Cb cloud at all levels — pilots must check icing SIGMETs and obtain current pilot reports (PIREPs) before flight.
exam_tip: You need both temperature at or below 0 degrees C AND visible moisture. Dry cold air at -40 degrees C produces no airframe icing. The peak SLW zone is 0 to -10 degrees C.
key_facts: Two conditions required: temperature <= 0 degrees C AND visible moisture (liquid water)
Supercooled liquid water (SLW) droplets remain liquid below 0 degrees C
Peak SLW concentration between 0 and -10 degrees C — highest icing risk zone
Ice crystals alone (high cloud/cirrus) generally do not cause airframe accretion
Icing can occur in cloud, rain, drizzle, freezing fog, and freezing precipitation
Check SIGMET for icing and PIREPs before flight in NZ winter/frontal conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe clear (glaze) ice, including conditions of formation and characteristics
concept: Clear ice, also called glaze ice, is the most dangerous type of airframe icing. It forms when large supercooled water droplets (typically in freezing rain or cumuliform cloud with high water content) strike the airframe. Only part of each droplet freezes immediately on impact — the rest flows back along the surface before freezing, creating a smooth, dense, transparent coating that conforms closely to the airframe shape. This makes it very hard to detect visually, extremely difficult to remove with mechanical de-icing boots (it tends to flow around them), and adds significant weight. It accumulates most rapidly and adheres most firmly. Clear ice tends to form at temperatures close to 0 degrees C and is most commonly associated with freezing rain and large-droplet supercooled conditions. The smooth contour means it can seriously alter the aerofoil profile before a pilot realises how much ice has accumulated.
exam_tip: Clear ice is the most hazardous type — smooth, hard, heavy, and difficult to detect. It forms near 0 degrees C with large droplets (freezing rain). The 'glaze' appearance tells you the droplets partially flowed before freezing.
key_facts: Most dangerous icing type — dense, smooth, transparent, and hard
Forms near 0 degrees C from large supercooled droplets or freezing rain
Droplets partially flow before freezing — creates smooth runback coating
Hard to see, hard to remove with boots, accumulates quickly
Does not fracture easily — pneumatic de-icing boots less effective
Significantly degrades aerofoil performance even in thin layers

syllabus_desc: Describe rime ice, including conditions of formation and characteristics
concept: Rime ice forms when small supercooled water droplets freeze almost instantly on contact with the airframe, trapping air between them. The result is a white, opaque, granular, and brittle deposit that builds forward on leading edges, instrument probes, and aerials. Because the droplets freeze immediately, they form a rougher, more porous structure than clear ice and are lighter per unit volume due to trapped air. Rime ice tends to form at colder temperatures (around -10 to -20 degrees C) and in stratiform cloud with smaller droplet sizes. While less dense than clear ice, even a thin layer of rime on the leading edge significantly disrupts airflow and increases stall speed. It is easier to detect than clear ice (visible white deposits) and pneumatic de-icing boots are generally effective because the brittle structure fractures under expansion. In NZ, rime ice is commonly encountered in stratus and stratocumulus cloud during winter frontal passages over the South Island ranges.
exam_tip: Rime ice is white, opaque, and brittle — smaller droplets at colder temperatures. It looks like the frost on your freezer. Boots work better on rime than on clear ice because it fractures.
key_facts: White, opaque, granular, brittle deposit — small droplets freeze instantly
Forms at colder temperatures (-10 to -20 degrees C) in stratiform cloud
Lighter than clear ice due to trapped air; visible on leading edges and probes
Disrupts airflow and raises stall speed even in thin layers
Pneumatic boots effective — brittle structure fractures on boot expansion
Most common in NZ stratus/stratocumulus during cold frontal passages

syllabus_desc: Describe mixed ice, including conditions of formation and characteristics
concept: Mixed ice is a combination of clear and rime ice that forms when the temperature and droplet size conditions vary — which is common in real-world icing encounters. It occurs when an aircraft flies through cloud layers with varying droplet sizes or temperatures near 0 degrees C, or when it passes in and out of regions with supercooled large droplets and smaller droplets. The result is an irregular, rough deposit that combines the worst characteristics of both types: some of the density and adhesion of clear ice, and the roughness and airflow disruption of rime ice. Mixed ice is particularly hazardous because its irregular surface greatly increases drag and reduces lift — and it is difficult to predict or model. Pneumatic de-icing boots may partially shatter it, but the remaining clear ice residue can be hard to remove. Pilots are most likely to encounter mixed ice in active frontal cloud or towering cumulus where temperature and moisture content fluctuate rapidly.
exam_tip: Mixed ice combines both types — irregular, rough, and unpredictable. It forms where droplet size and temperature are variable, typically in frontal or convective cloud. Assume the worst from mixed ice.
key_facts: Combination of clear and rime ice from varying droplet size/temperature
Irregular, rough texture — worst characteristics of both ice types
Higher drag penalty than either type alone due to surface roughness
Common in active frontal cloud and towering cumulus
De-icing boots partially effective — clear ice component may remain
Most unpredictable icing type in terms of accumulation rate and shape

syllabus_desc: Describe hoar frost, including conditions of formation and characteristics
concept: Hoar frost forms by deposition — water vapour in the air transitions directly to ice crystals on a surface that is at or below 0 degrees C without passing through the liquid phase (sublimation in reverse). It produces a white, crystalline, feathery deposit on cold surfaces exposed to moist air. For pilots, the most common scenario is a cold-soaked aircraft (one that has been at altitude in sub-zero temperatures) descending into warmer, moist surface air — the cold skin of the aircraft causes condensation and immediate freezing of moisture, creating hoar frost on the fuselage, wing surfaces, and windscreen. Frost on wing upper surfaces must be removed before flight because even a fine crystalline layer disrupts the boundary layer airflow and can significantly raise stall speed. Pre-departure frost checks are mandatory in NZ for commercial operations. The NZ CAA requires that frost, ice, and snow be removed from critical surfaces before flight — a clean aircraft is a fundamental airworthiness requirement.
exam_tip: Hoar frost forms on cold surfaces by deposition (vapour directly to ice). A cold-soaked aircraft descending into warm moist air will collect hoar frost. It must be removed from wing surfaces before flight.
key_facts: Deposition process: vapour to ice directly (no liquid phase)
White, crystalline, feathery deposit on cold surfaces
Cold-soaked aircraft descending into warm moist air is classic scenario
Even thin frost on wing upper surface raises stall speed significantly
Must be removed from all critical surfaces before flight under NZ CAA rules
Can form on aircraft parked overnight when surface temperature drops below dew point

syllabus_desc: Describe freezing precipitation icing, including conditions of formation and characteristics
concept: Freezing precipitation — freezing rain (FZRA) and freezing drizzle (FZDZ) — represents the most severe in-flight icing scenario. It occurs when rain or drizzle falls from a warm layer aloft through a sub-zero surface layer without freezing until it strikes an aircraft or the ground. The droplets are supercooled (liquid at below-zero temperatures) and are typically large, containing a great deal of liquid water. On contact with a cold airframe, they spread and freeze as clear (glaze) ice, accumulating at extremely rapid rates. A few minutes of exposure to freezing rain can produce enough ice to overwhelm any aircraft protection system. Freezing rain can also form a layer of ice on runways and taxiways, severely degrading braking. The conditions for freezing rain in NZ commonly occur ahead of warm fronts when a warm air layer overrides cold surface air — particularly in Southland, Otago, and the South Island during winter. SIGMET and special observation (SPECI) issuance is triggered by freezing precipitation.
exam_tip: Freezing rain is the worst icing scenario — large supercooled droplets, rapid clear ice accumulation, can exceed any aircraft protection system. A few minutes can be life-threatening.
key_facts: Rain or drizzle that remains liquid below 0 degrees C and freezes on contact
Produces clear (glaze) ice at very high accumulation rates
Large droplet size = high liquid water content = most severe icing type
Can overwhelm even certified ice protection systems in minutes
Creates ice on runways and taxiways — braking severely degraded
Common ahead of warm fronts in Southland and Otago during winter

topic: Icing Effect on Lift and Stall
syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of icing on lift and stall characteristics
concept: Ice accretion on wings and tailplane surfaces has a profound effect on aerodynamic performance. Even a thin layer of ice — as little as 0.5 mm of rough ice on the leading edge — disrupts the smooth boundary layer airflow, causing it to separate from the surface earlier. This reduces the maximum lift coefficient (CL max) and lowers the angle of attack at which stall occurs. The stall speed increases, sometimes dramatically. The shape of the stall itself can change — it may become sharper and give less warning (reduced buffet before stall). Ice on the horizontal stabiliser (tailplane icing) is particularly dangerous because it can cause the tailplane to stall when flaps are extended, resulting in an uncontrollable nose-down pitch. Ice contamination is asymmetric in many cases (one wing icing more than the other due to differing leading edge geometry), adding a roll tendency. NZ CPL pilots flying into icing conditions must understand that performance degradation is non-linear: a little ice causes a lot of aerodynamic change.
exam_tip: Ice on the leading edge raises stall speed and reduces CLmax. Tailplane icing is especially dangerous because it can cause a sudden uncontrollable nose-down pitch when flaps are deployed.
key_facts: Even 0.5 mm of rough ice on leading edge significantly reduces CLmax
Stall speed increases; angle of attack at stall decreases
Stall warning may reduce or disappear — less pre-stall buffet
Tailplane ice stall causes sudden uncontrollable nose-down pitch on flap extension
Asymmetric icing can add a roll tendency towards the iced wing
Aerodynamic degradation is non-linear — small ice = large performance loss

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of icing on drag
concept: Ice accretion increases aerodynamic drag significantly. Rough ice (rime or mixed) creates a highly irregular surface that disrupts the boundary layer and increases skin friction drag and pressure drag. Even smooth clear ice changes the effective aerofoil profile, adding frontal area. The drag increase compounds with lift loss — to maintain level flight, the pilot must increase power and angle of attack, which in turn increases the wetted area exposed to icing conditions. Studies have shown that a modest ice buildup can increase total drag by 20–40%, and in severe icing, drag can more than double. This substantially reduces cruise speed, range, and fuel efficiency. For turbo-prop and piston aircraft without significant excess power reserves, even moderate icing can render level flight impossible — the aircraft simply cannot overcome the drag at any achievable power setting. Pilots should monitor airspeed carefully in icing conditions: an unexplained airspeed decrease is often the first indication of ice accumulation.
exam_tip: Ice increases drag by 20–40% even in moderate conditions. A dropping airspeed for constant power setting is a classic early sign of ice accumulation — don't wait for visual confirmation.
key_facts: Rough ice increases skin friction and pressure drag simultaneously
Even modest ice can increase total drag by 20–40%
Drag increase forces higher angle of attack, exposing more surface to icing
Severe icing can exceed aircraft power reserves and prevent level flight
Unexplained airspeed reduction at constant power = suspect ice accumulation
Drag and lift effects compound — performance degradation is worse than the sum of each

topic: Icing Effect on Weight and CG
syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of icing on aircraft weight and centre of gravity
concept: Ice accumulation adds weight to the aircraft, and that weight is added in locations that may shift the centre of gravity outside approved limits. Ice collects primarily on leading edges, propeller spinner and blade leading edges, engine inlets, pitot probes, aerials, and control surface gaps and hinges. The weight added by ice can be substantial — a light aircraft can accumulate tens of kilograms of ice in moderate icing conditions within minutes. This increases stall speed (heavier aircraft stalls at higher speed), reduces fuel endurance, and degrades rate of climb. The location of the ice accumulation matters too: heavy ice on a wing's leading edge could shift the CG aft (if on the rear fuselage) or affect longitudinal balance if ice distribution is uneven. Ice on control surfaces can restrict or make controls heavy. Sudden ice shedding — particularly from propellers — can cause an abrupt shift in weight and vibration, and propeller ice shed can strike and damage the fuselage.
exam_tip: Ice adds weight (reduces performance) and may shift CG outside limits. Propeller ice shed is a secondary hazard — the ice can strike and damage the fuselage. Never exceed limits.
key_facts: Ice adds weight — even a thin layer adds significant mass on larger surfaces
Added weight raises stall speed and reduces climb rate and endurance
Ice on control surfaces can restrict movement or make controls abnormally heavy
Uneven ice distribution can shift CG outside approved limits
Propeller ice shed causes vibration and may strike and damage the fuselage
Weight from ice is in addition to all other aerodynamic penalties

topic: Icing Effect on Engines and Propellers
syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of icing on engines and propellers
concept: Icing affects aircraft engines and propellers through several mechanisms. Ice can block engine air intakes, reducing airflow and causing power loss. On piston engines, ice inside the carburettor (carburettor ice — covered separately under 20.28.14) is a major concern. On turbine engines, inlet icing can damage compressor blades when ice sheds and ingests into the engine; engine anti-ice (EAI) systems must be activated before entering icing conditions, not after ice has formed. Propeller icing disrupts the blade's aerofoil section, reduces thrust efficiency, and — critically — the asymmetric nature of propeller ice shedding generates sudden vibration. Large chunks of shed ice can strike the fuselage with significant force. Propeller de-ice systems (electrical heating elements or fluid anti-ice) cycle the de-ice to shed ice symmetrically and reduce vibration. In NZ, pilots of piston aircraft without prop de-ice operating near icing conditions must avoid those conditions — the loss of thrust from a partially iced propeller can be severe.
exam_tip: Activate engine anti-ice BEFORE entering icing conditions, not after — turbine engine compressor damage from ingested ice shed is sudden and can be catastrophic. Propeller ice sheds unevenly, causing vibration.
key_facts: Ice blocks engine air intakes, reducing airflow and power
Turbine engine inlet ice can shed into compressor and cause blade damage
Engine anti-ice must be ON before entering icing, not after ice has formed
Propeller ice disrupts blade aerofoil and reduces thrust
Propeller ice sheds asymmetrically — causes vibration; shed ice can strike fuselage
Piston aircraft without prop de-ice should avoid icing conditions

topic: Icing Effect on Instruments and Systems
syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of icing on aircraft instruments and systems
concept: Ice affecting aircraft instruments can cause insidious and misleading instrument indications. A blocked pitot tube causes the ASI to read the last indicated airspeed (in effect, acting like an altimeter — reading increasing airspeed as altitude increases and decreasing as altitude decreases), or to show zero depending on static venting. A blocked static port alone causes erroneous readings on ASI, altimeter, and VSI simultaneously. Ice blocking the static source while the pitot is clear causes ASI to overread on climb and underread on descent. AOA sensors can freeze and give erroneous stall warnings or no warning at all. Propeller anti-icing fluid or de-ice boot bleed air loss may go unnoticed. Windscreen icing impairs forward visibility on approach. Engine instruments may give erroneous readings if sensor probes ice over. In NZ, all aircraft flown IFR in icing conditions must be certificated and equipped for flight in icing — this includes heated pitot/static, carburettor heat or engine inlet anti-ice, and appropriate airframe protection.
exam_tip: Blocked pitot tube: ASI behaves like an altimeter (reads high climbing, low descending). Blocked static port: affects ASI, altimeter, and VSI simultaneously. These are critical instrument failures.
key_facts: Blocked pitot tube makes ASI behave like an altimeter — very dangerous
Blocked static port gives erroneous ASI, altimeter, and VSI readings
AOA sensors can freeze — giving false stall warning or none at all
Windscreen icing impairs forward visibility for approach
Heated pitot/static is required for NZ IFR flight in icing conditions
Engine instrument probes can ice over and give erroneous readings

syllabus_desc: Describe the intensity classification of in-flight icing
concept: In-flight icing is classified by intensity to describe the rate and severity of accumulation. Trace icing: ice is perceptible but accumulation rate is slightly greater than sublimation. De-icing and anti-icing systems are adequate — not a hazard unless encountered for extended periods. Light icing: accumulation is slightly hazardous if encountered for extended periods. De-ice and anti-ice systems adequately manage the accumulation. Moderate icing: accumulation rate is such that even short encounters become potentially hazardous and use of de-icing and anti-icing equipment or diversion is necessary. Severe icing: accumulation rate is such that anti-icing and de-icing systems fail to reduce or control the hazard — immediate diversion is essential. These intensity classifications are used in PIREPs and AIRMETs/SIGMETs. Severe icing SIGMETs are issued in NZ by MetService when conditions threaten all aircraft, including those certified for flight in icing.
exam_tip: Moderate icing = anti-ice systems required, diversion considered. Severe icing = immediate diversion essential, anti-ice systems cannot cope. Know the four levels: trace, light, moderate, severe.
key_facts: Four levels: trace, light, moderate, severe
Trace: perceptible but not hazardous for short exposures
Light: hazardous if prolonged — de-ice systems manage adequately
Moderate: short encounters hazardous — diversion may be needed
Severe: immediate diversion required — protection systems overwhelmed
Classifications used in PIREPs, AIRMETs, and SIGMETs

syllabus_desc: Describe the freezing level and its significance for flight planning
concept: The freezing level (also called the zero degree isotherm) is the altitude at which the ambient temperature is 0 degrees C. At and below this level, temperatures are above freezing; above this level, temperatures are sub-zero. It is significant because airframe icing can potentially occur at altitudes at or above the freezing level in cloud or precipitation. The freezing level is not always a single, clean horizontal surface — it varies with time of day, season, latitude, and proximity to fronts. In NZ summer, the freezing level over the North Island may be at 10,000–14,000 ft, while in winter it can descend to near sea level in Southland during cold southerly outbreaks. Freezing rain can occur below the freezing level (because the rain falls from a warm layer aloft into a sub-zero surface layer). The freezing level is forecast and reported in meteorological products including the Significant Weather Chart, SIGWX, and GRID (upper wind and temperature charts). Pilots should identify the freezing level on pre-flight planning to determine which altitudes carry icing risk.
exam_tip: The freezing level is where temperature = 0 degrees C. Icing risk is at and above this level in cloud. Freezing rain can occur BELOW the freezing level — this is the exception and the most dangerous scenario.
key_facts: Freezing level = altitude of 0 degrees C isotherm
Icing risk exists at and above the freezing level in cloud/precipitation
Freezing rain can occur BELOW the freezing level — warm layer aloft feeds supercooled drops
Freezing level varies with season, latitude, and frontal activity
NZ summer: FL 100–140 typically; winter can be near sea level in Southland
Forecast on Significant Weather Charts and GRID upper-air products

syllabus_desc: Describe the most dangerous icing conditions for aircraft
concept: The most dangerous icing conditions combine maximum supercooled liquid water content with large droplet sizes and temperatures close to 0 degrees C — conditions producing clear (glaze) ice at rapid accumulation rates. Freezing rain is the acknowledged worst scenario: large supercooled droplets, very high liquid water content, produces clear ice faster than most protection systems can cope. Supercooled Large Droplet (SLD) icing — from freezing drizzle or freezing rain — is now recognised as an airworthiness certification standard (FAA 14 CFR Part 25 Appendix O) because conventional airframe certification missed these conditions. SLD icing can form on aft wing surfaces and regions behind the protected leading edge zone, in areas where de-ice boots or bleed air does not reach. Flying in or near embedded Cb clouds, large cumulonimbus with strong updrafts, also creates severe icing — the updraft maintains a large pool of supercooled water. The combination of thunderstorm turbulence and severe icing in this environment is extremely dangerous. In NZ, pilots should treat any active frontal cloud with temperatures between 0 and -10 degrees C as a potential severe icing zone.
exam_tip: Freezing rain = worst icing. Supercooled Large Droplets (SLD) are a newer certification concern — they ice areas behind the boot zone. Near 0 degrees C + frontal cloud + large droplets = maximum hazard.
key_facts: Freezing rain: worst case — large SLW droplets, very rapid clear ice accumulation
Supercooled Large Droplet (SLD) icing ices beyond the protected leading edge zone
Temperature near 0 degrees C + cloud/rain = highest icing risk
Embedded Cb combines icing AND turbulence — doubly dangerous
SLD now a separate airworthiness certification condition (FAA/EASA)
Treat 0 to -10 degrees C frontal cloud as potential severe icing environment

syllabus_desc: Describe carburettor icing, including conditions of formation and effects
concept: Carburettor icing is caused by two simultaneous effects inside the carburettor throat. First, the venturi effect accelerates airflow and causes a pressure drop, which produces a temperature drop (adiabatic cooling) of up to 20 degrees C. Second, fuel evaporation in the carburettor causes further cooling through latent heat absorption of up to 15 degrees C. Together, these can reduce the local temperature by 30 degrees C or more below the ambient OAT. This means carburettor icing can occur at OATs as high as +30 degrees C if the air is moist — it does not require below-zero outside temperatures. Icing is most likely with high humidity, OAT between +10 and +25 degrees C, and at low power settings (reduced fuel flow gives a greater fraction of evaporative cooling). In aircraft, the first sign of carburettor icing is an unexplained drop in RPM (fixed-pitch prop) or manifold pressure (constant-speed prop), sometimes with rough running. Carburettor heat should be applied at the first sign of icing or as a precautionary measure during descent. Full hot air should be applied — partial heat can actually worsen icing by melting ice partially and allowing refreezing.
exam_tip: Carburettor icing can occur at OATs up to +30 degrees C in humid conditions — it does not need below-zero outside temperature. Apply full carb heat, not partial — partial heat can worsen the problem.
key_facts: Caused by venturi cooling + fuel evaporation cooling — up to 30 degrees C below OAT
Can occur at OAT up to +30 degrees C in high humidity
Most likely: OAT +10 to +25 degrees C, high humidity, low power setting
First sign: unexplained RPM drop (fixed-pitch) or MAP drop (CS prop)
Apply FULL carburettor heat — partial heat can refreeze melted ice water
Precautionary carb heat recommended during all descents with reduced power

syllabus_desc: Describe de-icing and anti-icing systems used on aircraft
concept: Aircraft ice protection systems are classified as anti-icing (prevent ice forming) or de-icing (remove ice after it forms). Pneumatic de-icing boots are rubber bladders on wing and stabiliser leading edges that inflate periodically to crack and shed accumulated ice — they are de-icing systems (ice must form before they work). Thermal anti-icing uses engine bleed air or electrical heating to keep leading edges above freezing — preventing ice formation. Heated pitot probes are standard on almost all aircraft and use electrical heating elements. Propeller de-ice uses either cycled electrical heating elements embedded in each blade, or isopropyl alcohol fluid anti-ice systems that spread fluid along the blade. Windscreen heat prevents ice and mist on forward vision panels. Engine anti-ice (EAI) heats the engine inlet to prevent ice ingestion into turbine engines. Important rule: anti-icing systems must be activated before entering icing conditions; de-icing boots should not be cycled before sufficient ice builds up (thin ice layers may not fracture cleanly, leaving a bumpy residue). In NZ, aircraft operating IFR or above the freezing level in cloud must carry appropriate ice protection for the conditions.
exam_tip: Anti-icing prevents ice (must be ON before icing). De-icing removes ice that has formed (boots). Don't cycle boots on a very thin ice layer — let a small amount build up first so it fractures cleanly.
key_facts: Anti-icing: prevents ice (thermal, bleed air, heated probes) — activate BEFORE icing
De-icing: removes ice after formation (pneumatic boots, some prop systems)
Boots: inflate to crack and shed ice — must have enough ice thickness to work
Cycled too early, boots leave a rough residue that is hard to remove
Heated pitot probes are standard — essential for reliable airspeed in cloud
NZ IFR aircraft above freezing level in cloud require appropriate ice protection

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions necessary for thunderstorm development
concept: Three ingredients are necessary for thunderstorm development: instability (the atmosphere must be conditionally or absolutely unstable, so that a lifted air parcel is warmer than its surroundings and continues to rise), moisture (sufficient water vapour to sustain deep convection and produce precipitation), and a lifting mechanism (something to initiate the initial ascent). Lifting mechanisms include surface heating (diurnal convection), orographic lifting (terrain forcing), frontal lifting (convergence at a front), and upper-level divergence associated with troughs and jet streams. In NZ, all four mechanisms operate regularly. The Taupo Volcanic Plateau and central North Island experience frequent afternoon convective thunderstorms in summer driven by surface heating. Orographic thunderstorms develop on the western flanks of the Southern Alps when an unstable air mass is forced aloft. Frontal thunderstorms are common in cold fronts along the West Coast. When the three ingredients combine, convection can rapidly become severe — CAPE (Convective Available Potential Energy) is the key parameter forecasters use to predict thunderstorm intensity.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm needs three things: instability, moisture, and a lifting trigger. If any one is absent, deep convection cannot develop. NZ has all three triggers in play regularly.
key_facts: Three ingredients: instability + moisture + lifting mechanism
Lifting triggers: surface heating, orographic forcing, frontal convergence, upper divergence
CAPE is the key parameter for forecasting thunderstorm intensity
NZ summer afternoon convection over Taupo Plateau and ranges is common
Cold frontal thunderstorms are common on the NZ West Coast
All three ingredients must be present — absence of any one prevents thunderstorm development

syllabus_desc: Describe the cumulus stage of thunderstorm development
concept: The cumulus stage is the initial growth phase of a thunderstorm. It begins when a parcel of air is lifted past its Level of Free Convection (LFC) and becomes positively buoyant, accelerating upward. The towering cumulus builds rapidly, with the updraft strengthening as latent heat released by condensation adds further buoyancy. At this stage, the entire cell is dominated by updrafts — there is little or no precipitation reaching the surface yet, and what forms is carried upward or evaporates. The tops of developing cumulus grow rapidly, potentially at rates exceeding 5000 ft per minute in unstable conditions. The cell has not yet reached its mature stage and precipitation has not begun to fall in significant amounts. In NZ, towers can grow rapidly over the central North Island volcanic plateau and over the South Island ranges in summer. Pilots should treat rapidly growing cumulus with the same caution as mature thunderstorms — the transition from cumulus to mature stage can occur in minutes.
exam_tip: Cumulus stage = all updraft, no significant precipitation at the surface yet. The cell is growing rapidly — treat it as a hazard even before it matures. Vertical growth exceeding 5000 fpm is possible.
key_facts: All updrafts — no significant downdraft or surface precipitation yet
Begins at LFC when parcel becomes positively buoyant
Latent heat release accelerates vertical growth
Tops may grow faster than 5000 ft per minute
Transition to mature stage can occur within minutes
NZ volcanic plateau and ranges: rapid summer cumulus development is common

syllabus_desc: Describe the mature stage of thunderstorm development
concept: The mature stage is the most intense and dangerous phase of a thunderstorm. It begins when precipitation reaches the surface and a downdraft develops alongside the updraft. The cell now contains both powerful updrafts (sometimes exceeding 100 kt) and cold, precipitation-driven downdrafts — the two airstreams coexisting but creating intense shear between them. Heavy rain, hail, lightning, and severe turbulence are all most likely at this stage. The cumulonimbus top may penetrate the tropopause and spread into the characteristic anvil shape as the rising air spreads out in the stable stratosphere. Electrical activity is at its peak — a mature cell can produce hundreds of lightning discharges per hour. At the surface, the gust front (outflow boundary) from the downdraft can produce severe windshear extending tens of kilometres ahead of the visible cell. A mature NZ thunderstorm can produce hail exceeding 2 cm in diameter and tornadoes in extreme cases. This stage is the greatest hazard to aircraft — avoid by at least 20 nm and avoid flying beneath the anvil.
exam_tip: Mature stage = simultaneous updraft and downdraft, precipitation reaching surface, maximum intensity. This is when lightning, hail, severe turbulence, and microbursts are most likely. Do not penetrate.
key_facts: Simultaneous updraft and downdraft — most intense phase
Updrafts may exceed 100 kt; heavy rain and hail at the surface
Lightning activity peaks during the mature stage
Anvil top forms as rising air spreads in stratosphere
Gust front extends tens of km ahead of the cell
Never penetrate a mature thunderstorm — avoid by 20 nm minimum

syllabus_desc: Describe the dissipating stage of thunderstorm development
concept: The dissipating stage begins when the downdraft spreads throughout the entire cell, cutting off the warm moist inflow that fuelled the updraft. Without the supply of energy, convection weakens and the cell begins to decay. Precipitation weakens and becomes more stratiform. Lightning activity decreases. The anvil cloud may persist for some time after the main convective tower has collapsed. The gust front from the original mature stage may persist and potentially trigger new convective cells at a distance from the original storm. While the dissipating cell is less violent than the mature stage, it can still produce moderate turbulence, and its decaying precipitation and stratiform cloud may lower visibility. For multi-cell storms or squall lines, the dissipation of one cell often triggers the development of a new cell on the storm's flanks — so individual cell dissipation does not mean the overall storm system is weakening.
exam_tip: Dissipating stage = downdraft dominates, updraft cut off — cell is dying. Turbulence and precipitation still possible. But the gust front from a dissipating storm can trigger new cells nearby.
key_facts: Downdraft dominates — cuts off the warm moist updraft inflow
Precipitation weakens; lightning activity decreases
Anvil cloud persists after main tower collapses
Gust front may trigger new cells in multi-cell or squall line situations
Moderate turbulence still possible in dissipating stage
Individual cell dissipation does not mean the overall storm system has ended

syllabus_desc: Describe the different types of thunderstorms and their characteristics
concept: Thunderstorms are classified by their structure and formation mechanism. Single-cell (air-mass) thunderstorms are isolated cells driven by diurnal surface heating — they develop, mature, and dissipate in 30–60 minutes and are relatively short-lived and localised. Multi-cell storms consist of a cluster of cells in different stages of development — as one cell dissipates, another develops on the inflow flank, allowing the storm system to persist for hours. Squall lines are linear bands of thunderstorms, often 100–300 km long, typically associated with cold fronts or pre-frontal convergence lines. Supercell thunderstorms are the most dangerous type — a rotating, long-lived cell with a persistent organised mesocyclone (rotating updraft). Supercells are rare in NZ but do occur; they produce the most severe hail, strongest tornadoes, and most extreme turbulence. For NZ pilots, frontal multi-cell storms along the West Coast and pre-frontal squall lines tracking northeast are the most common organised thunderstorm hazards.
exam_tip: Know the four types: single-cell (short-lived), multi-cell (self-sustaining cluster), squall line (linear, frontal), supercell (rotating, most severe). Supercells are rare in NZ but do occur.
key_facts: Single-cell: short-lived (30–60 min), isolated, driven by surface heating
Multi-cell: cluster of cells in different stages — can persist for hours
Squall line: linear band along cold front or convergence line
Supercell: rotating (mesocyclone) — most severe, longest-lived, most hazardous
NZ most common: frontal multi-cell and squall lines on the West Coast
Supercells rare in NZ but do occur, particularly in the North Island

syllabus_desc: Describe the turbulence hazards associated with thunderstorms
concept: Thunderstorms generate turbulence through several mechanisms, all of which can be severe or extreme. Inside the storm, wind shear between updraft and downdraft columns creates violent turbulence that can break up aircraft — certified penetration of active Cb is generally not endorsed for normal aircraft. At the gust front, the cold outflow from the downdraft undercuts warm surface air, creating a sharp wind shift and low-level windshear that can reach from the surface up to several thousand feet. Clear Air Turbulence (CAT) can occur in the anvil cloud and for tens of kilometres around the storm due to wind shear at the storm's outskirts. Convective turbulence above the tropopause can occur where overshooting tops punch into the stratosphere. The standard avoidance distance is at least 20 nm from a mature cell and preferably not below or through an anvil. In NZ, storms over the Taupo Plateau and Waikato can be particularly hazardous due to orographic enhancement of updraft strength.
exam_tip: Avoid thunderstorms by at least 20 nm. Turbulence exists inside, at the gust front, in the anvil, and above the tropopause. Never fly under the anvil — hail and turbulence extend well beyond the visible cloud.
key_facts: Internal turbulence: wind shear between updraft and downdraft columns
Gust front turbulence: cold outflow undercuts warm air — severe low-level shear
Anvil turbulence: extends tens of km around and beyond the visible anvil
CAT possible near storm outflow boundaries above the tropopause
Standard avoidance: 20 nm from active cell; do not fly under the anvil
NZ Taupo/Waikato storms can produce particularly strong updrafts due to orographic lift

syllabus_desc: Describe the lightning hazards associated with thunderstorms
concept: Lightning is an electrical discharge that equalises the charge separation between regions within a cloud, between clouds, or between the cloud and the ground. For aviation, both cloud-to-ground and intra-cloud lightning are hazards. A lightning strike on an aircraft typically causes a current pulse through the airframe, which can damage avionics, temporarily blind the crew (flash blindness), induce voltage transients in electrical wiring, and in some cases damage composite structure or control surfaces. Modern aircraft are certified to withstand lightning strikes through bonding, surge suppressors, and design practices. However, even properly certified aircraft can suffer some avionics degradation after a strike. Navigation aids, communication radios, and flight management computers can be temporarily upset. Additionally, the static electrical environment near a thunderstorm causes static interference on ADF/NDB receivers (the well-known practice of ADF pointing toward a storm). NZ pilots must log any lightning strike event and have the aircraft inspected before further flight.
exam_tip: ADF deflects toward lightning — this is a classic exam question. After any lightning strike, the aircraft must be inspected before further flight. Flash blindness is a short-term cockpit hazard.
key_facts: Lightning causes current pulses that can damage avionics and composite structure
Flash blindness is a temporary but dangerous cockpit hazard
ADF/NDB needle deflects toward lightning discharge — unreliable near storms
Modern aircraft certified for lightning strike protection
Any lightning strike requires inspection before further flight under NZ CAA requirements
Static charge builds on aircraft near storms — brush discharge from static wicks

syllabus_desc: Describe the hail hazard associated with thunderstorms
concept: Hail forms when supercooled water droplets are carried repeatedly through the freezing zone by strong Cb updrafts, adding successive layers of ice. Hailstone size is an indicator of updraft strength — larger hail requires stronger updrafts to suspend it. Golf ball-sized hail requires updraft speeds exceeding 100 kt. For aircraft, hail presents a serious structural hazard — it can crack windscreens, dent leading edges and fuselage skin, destroy propellers, and damage engine fan blades. Hail can occur well away from the visible storm — it has been reported up to 20 nm from the parent cell, carried by the upper-level outflow and falling through clear air. Flying beneath an anvil cloud is therefore dangerous even in apparently clear air. Weather radar returns from large hail can be misleading — hail in the dry-hail zone may not show high radar reflectivity values. In NZ, severe hailstorms occasionally affect Canterbury, Manawatu, and Waikato, and hail aloft is a regular hazard at higher altitudes near frontal Cb cloud.
exam_tip: Hail can be encountered up to 20 nm from a storm cell — in apparently clear air beneath the anvil. Never assume the area is safe just because it looks clear. Weather radar may underestimate hail intensity.
key_facts: Hail forms by repeated updraft cycling through the freezing zone
Hailstone size reflects updraft strength — golf ball hail needs >100 kt updraft
Can occur 20 nm from the parent cell, beneath the anvil in clear air
Windscreens, leading edges, propellers, and engine fan blades are vulnerable
Weather radar may underestimate hail intensity (dry hail zone effect)
NZ severe hailstorms common in Canterbury and Manawatu in summer

syllabus_desc: Describe the windshear and gust front hazards from thunderstorms
concept: The gust front is the leading edge of cold, dense air that flows outward from the Cb downdraft along the surface. When the cold outflow undercuts warmer surface air, a sharp wind shift, gusty surface winds, and a rapidly rising band of turbulence are produced — the classic 'roll cloud' or 'shelf cloud' visible at the leading edge of the outflow. Low-level windshear at the gust front is one of the most serious threats to aircraft on approach or departure. An aircraft penetrating the gust front during approach encounters a sudden headwind increase (airspeed rises, the aircraft pitches up and balloons above the glidepath), followed by the headwind converting to a tailwind on the downwind side (sudden airspeed loss, aircraft sinks below glidepath). This sequence — known as a microburst on a small scale — can be fatal below 1000 ft AGL. In NZ, gust fronts can extend 20–40 km ahead of an approaching thunderstorm. MetService issues Low Level Windshear Alerts (LLWS) for major NZ airports when the risk is significant.
exam_tip: Gust front = cold outflow from Cb downdraft ahead of the storm. It creates a wind shift and low-level windshear. On approach: first a headwind (balloon above glidepath) then tailwind (drop below) — extremely dangerous below 1000 ft.
key_facts: Cold outflow from downdraft spreads along surface ahead of the storm
Produces sharp wind shift, gusty winds, and roll/shelf cloud
Low-level windshear: headwind then tailwind during approach crossing gust front
Can extend 20–40 km ahead of the visible storm
Microburst: small-scale intense downburst — 4 km diameter, >45 kt speed change
MetService issues LLWS alerts for major NZ airports

syllabus_desc: Describe the microburst hazard from thunderstorms
concept: A microburst is a small-scale but intense downdraft that spreads outward in all directions when it hits the surface — like water from a tap hitting a flat surface. It is defined as a downburst with horizontal extent of 4 km or less and surface wind speed increase of at least 45 kt. Microbursts typically last 5–15 minutes and can occur with minimal visible precipitation (dry microburst) or with heavy rain (wet microburst). The wind shear produced is severe: an aircraft approaching into a microburst first encounters an increasing headwind (performance improves), then a strong downdraft (aircraft descends), then an increasing tailwind (airspeed drops rapidly). The energy loss in a full microburst encounter can exceed aircraft climb performance and result in ground impact even with maximum power applied. In NZ, wet microbursts are more common than dry, typically associated with intense Cb cells. MetService uses Doppler radar to identify microburst signatures. If a microburst is reported or suspected on the approach path, all aircraft should hold or divert.
exam_tip: Microburst sequence on approach: headwind (performance improves) → downdraft → tailwind (performance collapses). The total energy loss can exceed climb capability — fatal below 1000 ft AGL.
key_facts: Microburst: intense downburst <4 km diameter, >45 kt wind speed change
Lasts 5–15 minutes; can be dry (little rain) or wet (heavy rain)
Approach sequence: headwind gain then downdraft then tailwind loss
Total wind shear can exceed aircraft climb capability
Doppler radar detects microburst signatures — MetService uses this for NZ airports
If reported on approach path — go around and hold or divert

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of embedded thunderstorms on flight operations
concept: Embedded thunderstorms are Cb cells hidden within layers of stratiform cloud — they cannot be seen by the crew because they are visually indistinguishable from the surrounding cloud. They are among the most dangerous weather phenomena in aviation because a pilot flying IFR in an area of widespread stratocumulus or nimbostratus may inadvertently enter an active Cb without any visual warning. Embedded Cb is most common ahead of and within warm fronts, where extensive stratiform cloud co-exists with residual convection. The only reliable detection methods are airborne weather radar or Doppler detection from the ground — visual avoidance is impossible. Forecasters identify the risk of embedded Cb in SIGMETs issued as EMBD CB. In NZ, frontal systems crossing the South Island and moving northeast regularly produce embedded Cb conditions. Pilots operating IFR in areas with SIGMET warnings for EMBD CB should treat the entire frontal cloud zone as containing embedded thunderstorm risk — routing around the system where possible.
exam_tip: Embedded Cb cannot be seen visually — they lurk inside layer cloud. The only way to detect them is with airborne weather radar. A SIGMET for EMBD CB means avoid the entire affected area if possible.
key_facts: Cb cells hidden inside stratiform cloud — impossible to detect visually
Most common in warm frontal cloud where stratiform and convective co-exist
Airborne weather radar is the only reliable detection method in flight
Forecast/reported as EMBD CB in SIGMETs
Common in NZ frontal systems crossing the South Island
Treat entire SIGMET EMBD CB zone as hazardous — route around if possible

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of weather radar in detecting and avoiding thunderstorms
concept: Weather radar detects precipitation by transmitting microwave pulses and measuring the reflected energy (reflectivity) from precipitation particles. High reflectivity (shown in red on colour displays) indicates heavy precipitation and is a strong indicator of thunderstorm intensity. Radar displays precipitation intensity in dBZ — values above 40 dBZ indicate heavy rain; above 50 dBZ indicate severe thunderstorm with hail likely. However, radar has important limitations: the beam tilts upward with distance, so at long range the radar samples high altitudes and may miss surface precipitation. The 'cone of silence' directly below the radar is also a gap in coverage. Dry hail may not show high reflectivity. Radar does not detect turbulence, windshear, or lightning directly. In the cockpit, colour weather radar allows crews to contour around areas of highest intensity — the standard technique is to avoid areas showing red/magenta and to not use gaps between cells as penetration routes (the gaps between cells often contain severe turbulence). In NZ, MetService's network of weather radars provides real-time coverage; pilots can access radar imagery via MetFlight and ATIS/VOLMET broadcasts.
exam_tip: Radar shows precipitation intensity — not turbulence or wind. Do not fly through gaps between radar returns as these areas often contain severe turbulence. Dry hail can be present without high radar returns.
key_facts: Radar measures reflectivity (dBZ) from precipitation — not turbulence directly
>40 dBZ = heavy rain; >50 dBZ = severe Cb with likely hail
Beam elevation angle means radar underestimates intensity at long range
Gaps between storm cells on radar often contain severe turbulence — do not penetrate
Dry hail may not show high radar returns
NZ weather radar network accessed via MetFlight; real-time coverage for pre-flight

syllabus_desc: Describe thunderstorm avoidance techniques
concept: The fundamental rule is simple: avoid thunderstorms entirely. Attempting to penetrate or navigate through a thunderstorm — even in a turbulence-certified aircraft — is not acceptable practice for any transport or commercial operation. Standard avoidance distances: maintain at least 20 nm from any active Cb cell; do not fly beneath the anvil at any distance from the cell; if in a line of cells, do not attempt to thread between them unless the gap is at least 40 nm wide (ideally go around the whole system). On approach, if a Cb is within 5 nm of the field or on the approach path — go around and hold or divert. If caught inadvertently in a Cb: engage autopilot, reduce to turbulence penetration speed, maintain level flight attitude (do not chase altitude — let it fluctuate), reduce power to prevent structural overstress, and exit on the same heading if possible. Activating ice protection, fastening seatbelts, and turning cockpit lighting up to prevent lightning flash blindness are also immediate actions.
exam_tip: Avoid by 20 nm minimum; never fly under the anvil. If caught inside a Cb: maintain attitude not altitude, reduce to turbulence penetration speed, do not chase altitude deviations.
key_facts: Avoid by at least 20 nm from active Cb
Never fly under the anvil — hail and turbulence extend well beyond the cell
Gaps between cells: do not thread unless >40 nm wide
Approach: if Cb within 5 nm of field — go around and hold or divert
If caught inside: reduce to turbulence penetration speed, maintain attitude not altitude
Increase cockpit lighting to reduce flash blindness risk from lightning

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of squall lines
concept: A squall line is a line or band of active thunderstorms typically extending hundreds of kilometres, oriented roughly perpendicular to the steering-level wind. It often develops 50–300 km ahead of a cold front (a pre-frontal squall line) or along the front itself. Squall lines are self-sustaining because the gust front from the leading edge of the line continuously lifts new warm moist air into the line of cells — effectively acting as a surface boundary that regenerates convection. They can move rapidly (sometimes 40–60 kt) and can produce continuous bands of severe weather — hail, severe turbulence, strong surface winds, and lightning — along their entire length. Attempting to find a gap through a squall line is extremely hazardous. Routing around the end of the line is the only safe strategy. In NZ, squall lines form ahead of vigorous cold fronts approaching from the Tasman, and are forecast in SIGMETs as SQL or LINE CONVEC. The MetService Significant Weather Chart and Pilot Briefing will indicate the presence and movement of squall lines.
exam_tip: A squall line is a self-sustaining band of Cb cells — not a temporary cluster. Do not attempt to penetrate or find gaps. Fly around the ends. They can move at 40–60 kt and are forecast in SIGMETs.
key_facts: Line of Cb cells extending hundreds of km, often pre-frontal
Self-sustaining — gust front continuously triggers new convection
Can move rapidly (40–60 kt) — outrunning requires careful planning
Covers entire length with severe hazards — no safe gaps
Only safe option: fly around the ends of the line
Forecast in SIGMETs as SQL or LINE CONVEC; shown on Significant Weather Chart

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and hazards of supercell thunderstorms
concept: A supercell is a long-lived, highly organised thunderstorm with a persistent rotating updraft called a mesocyclone. The rotation is caused by wind shear in the environment — the wind speed and direction change with altitude in a way that causes the updraft to tilt and rotate. Supercells produce the most severe weather of any thunderstorm type: giant hail (>5 cm diameter), the strongest tornadoes (EF4–EF5), extreme windshear, very large precipitation areas, and the most turbulent in-cloud conditions. A supercell can persist for many hours and travel hundreds of kilometres. For aviation, the hazard zone around a supercell extends much further than a standard Cb — avoidance distances should be increased to at least 40 nm, and ideally the system should be avoided entirely by going around upwind of it. Supercells are relatively rare in NZ but do occur, particularly in the North Island in spring and summer when jet stream wind shear and surface instability combine.
exam_tip: Supercell = rotating updraft (mesocyclone). Produces the worst weather of any thunderstorm — giant hail, strongest tornadoes, extreme turbulence. Rare in NZ but avoid by 40 nm minimum.
key_facts: Rotating updraft (mesocyclone) driven by environmental wind shear
Produces largest hail, strongest tornadoes, and most extreme turbulence
Long-lived — can persist for many hours over hundreds of km
Aviation hazard zone: at least 40 nm from the storm
Rare in NZ but occur in North Island in spring/summer
Go around well upwind — downwind side has hazards from anvil and outflow

syllabus_desc: Describe the meteorological products used for thunderstorm forecasting and reporting
concept: Pilots have access to several meteorological products specifically for thunderstorm planning in NZ. SIGMETs (Significant Meteorological Information) are issued for hazardous phenomena including Cb, severe turbulence, severe icing, and volcanic ash — they are operationally mandatory advisories issued by MetService. A SIGMET for CB or EMBD CB indicates a specific area of thunderstorm activity with defined boundaries and validity times. Area Forecasts (ARFOR) include significant weather sections covering convective activity. The Significant Weather Chart (SIGWX) provides a graphical overview of expected significant weather at levels from surface to FL 250. TAFs and METARs include TS (thunderstorm) prefixes for precipitation associated with thunderstorms. ATIS broadcasts at major NZ airports will include reference to nearby thunderstorm activity. Pilot Reports (PIREPs) are a crucial real-time source — request them from ATC if operating near potential thunderstorm areas. MetFlight provides a consolidated pre-flight briefing including all of these products.
exam_tip: SIGMETs for CB are mandatory advisories — pilots must obtain and consider them. CB or EMBD CB SIGMETs define the affected area. PIREPs from other aircraft give the best real-time picture.
key_facts: SIGMETs are mandatory for Cb, EMBD CB, severe turbulence, severe icing, VA
ARFOR significant weather section includes convective activity forecast
SIGWX chart shows CB symbols over affected areas (up to FL 250)
METARs/TAFs use TS prefix for thunderstorm precipitation
PIREPs are the most current real-time information — request from ATC
MetFlight is the primary NZ pre-flight briefing source for all these products

syllabus_desc: Describe the surface weather conditions associated with a thunderstorm passage
concept: As a thunderstorm passes over or near an aerodrome, the surface weather changes rapidly and dramatically. Before the storm, surface pressure falls and winds back (shift anti-clockwise) ahead of the approaching gust front. At gust front arrival: sudden wind shift (often 180 degrees), gusts of 30–50 kt or more, rapid drop in temperature as the cold outflow arrives, brief sharp pressure rise (pressure jump), and the start of heavy precipitation. During the storm: heavy rain or hail, extremely poor visibility, severe turbulence, lightning, and often dangerous windshear. After the storm passes: winds shift again, temperature drops further (cold air mass behind), pressure rises, precipitation ceases, and visibility improves. These rapid changes within minutes make operations during a thunderstorm passage at an aerodrome very hazardous. Ground operations must be suspended, refuelling stopped, and aircraft secured or hangared. NZ aerodromes issue SPECIs when significant weather changes occur.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm passage sequence at a surface station: pressure falls → wind shift → gust front → rapid temperature drop → heavy rain/hail → pressure rises → clears. All of this can happen in 15–30 minutes.
key_facts: Pre-storm: pressure falls, winds back (shift anti-clockwise) ahead of gust front
Gust front arrival: sudden wind shift, strong gusts, temperature drop, pressure jump
During: heavy precipitation, extreme turbulence, lightning, windshear, poor visibility
Post-storm: pressure rises, temperature further drops, wind shifts, clears
All changes can occur within 15–30 minutes — highly dangerous for aerodrome ops
SPECI issued by NZ aerodromes for significant weather changes during thunderstorm

syllabus_desc: Describe the static electricity hazards from thunderstorms
concept: Thunderstorms develop intense electrical fields due to charge separation within the Cb — positive charge accumulates in the upper cloud, negative charge in the lower cloud, and positive charge is induced on the Earth's surface below. Even before a lightning discharge, the strong electrical field in and around a Cb can affect aircraft systems. Static electricity on the airframe builds up from precipitation and particle collision, causing St Elmo's Fire — a visible blue/white corona discharge from aircraft extremities (wingtips, propeller tips, aerials, pitot probes). While visually dramatic, St Elmo's Fire itself is not structurally harmful, but it indicates the aircraft is in an intense electrical environment near a Cb. More seriously, the static charge interferes with ADF/NDB receivers (needle deflects toward the storm discharge), compass errors can occur, and communication interference increases. Aircraft are equipped with static discharge wicks (trailing metal wires) to bleed off static charge continuously and reduce the buildup that causes interference.
exam_tip: St Elmo's Fire = visible corona discharge in high static field — harmless itself but means you are very close to a Cb. ADF needle deflects toward thunderstorm discharge — classic exam question.
key_facts: Charge separation in Cb: positive upper cloud, negative lower cloud, positive surface
St Elmo's Fire: blue/white corona on aircraft extremities in high electrical field
St Elmo's Fire is visually alarming but not structurally harmful
ADF/NDB needle deflects toward lightning — unreliable near storms
Static wicks (trailing wires) bleed off charge to reduce radio interference
Strong static field indicates proximity to Cb — immediate avoidance action required

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of thunderstorms on aerodrome operations
concept: A thunderstorm at or near an aerodrome has wide-ranging effects on operations. All ground handling and fuelling must stop — personnel and vehicles are extremely vulnerable to lightning. Aircraft must be securely tied down or hangared; unsecured aircraft can be damaged by wind gusts and hail. Runways become hazardous from standing water (aquaplaning risk), hail accumulation (slippery surface), and possible flooding of drainage systems. The wind direction and strength changes rapidly, so runway in use can change with little warning. Windshear is the primary hazard for aircraft attempting to land or depart — low-level windshear during approach is potentially fatal. Aircraft holding for the storm to pass must maintain sufficient fuel reserves for extended holds or diversion. In NZ, controlled aerodromes issue SPECIs for significant weather changes. ATC will advise crews of windshear reports and SIGMET conditions. The decision to depart or continue an approach during nearby Cb activity is ultimately the pilot in command's responsibility.
exam_tip: During thunderstorm at an aerodrome: stop all ground operations, secure aircraft, do not attempt approach if Cb is within 5 nm. Low-level windshear on approach is the primary aircraft hazard.
key_facts: All ground handling and fuelling must stop — lightning risk to personnel
Aircraft must be tied down or hangared — hail and gusts damage unsecured aircraft
Standing water and hail on runways create aquaplaning and braking hazards
Runway in use can change rapidly with wind shift — re-planning required
Low-level windshear on approach is the primary fatal hazard for aircraft
SPECI issued for significant weather changes at NZ controlled aerodromes

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of anticyclones
concept: An anticyclone is a region of high atmospheric pressure with air flowing outward from the centre in a clockwise direction in the Southern Hemisphere (and counter-clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere). At the surface, winds spiral outward and the isobars are generally widely spaced, indicating light winds. Anticyclones are characterised by descending air (subsidence), which warms adiabatically and suppresses cloud formation — anticyclonic weather is typically settled, with clear skies and good visibility at the surface. The descending air creates a subsidence temperature inversion aloft, which traps moisture and pollutants in the boundary layer below. While generally the best flying weather, anticyclones are not without hazards: radiation fog and overnight temperature inversions can produce very poor surface visibility, particularly in sheltered valleys. NZ anticyclones are generally subtropical high-pressure systems that drift across the Tasman from the northwest and bring typical 'high pressure' settled weather — 3–5 days of fine conditions followed by the next frontal system.
exam_tip: Anticyclone: high pressure, outward flow (clockwise SH), subsiding air, clear skies. The subsidence inversion traps fog and haze below — surface visibility can be very poor despite clear sky above.
key_facts: High pressure system — air descends and flows outward (clockwise SH)
Widely spaced isobars = light surface winds
Subsiding air warms adiabatically — suppresses cloud and precipitation
Subsidence inversion traps moisture, fog, and pollution in boundary layer
Generally settled, clear weather — but radiation fog risk overnight
NZ: subtropical highs cross Tasman bringing 3–5 days of fine weather

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of anticyclones
concept: Anticyclones are classified by their origin and nature. Thermal (warm-core) anticyclones form at the surface due to surface heating over continental areas — the warm air expands, builds surface pressure, and creates a high. These are shallow and transient, typical of continental summer. Dynamic (cold-core or cut-off) anticyclones form due to convergence in the upper troposphere, forcing air downward — these are the large, persistent subtropical and sub-polar high-pressure systems. Subtropical highs (like the South Pacific High — the NZ region's dominant anticyclone) are dynamic in origin, semi-permanent, and the source of the easterly trade winds on their equatorward side and westerlies on their poleward side. Blocking anticyclones are large, persistent dynamic highs that block the normal progression of fronts and troughs, diverting them north or south — they can persist for weeks and produce drought conditions on the dry side and persistent rainfall on the wet side. In NZ, the South Pacific High is the dominant feature of NZ's climate.
exam_tip: Two main types: thermal (warm core, shallow, continental surface heating) and dynamic (cold core, deep, convergence aloft). The South Pacific High is a dynamic subtropical anticyclone — it dominates NZ weather.
key_facts: Thermal anticyclone: warm core, shallow, forms over hot land surface
Dynamic anticyclone: cold core, deep, forms from upper troposphere convergence
Subtropical highs (e.g. South Pacific High) are dynamic — dominant in NZ
Blocking anticyclones persist for weeks — divert fronts and cause drought
South Pacific High drives NZ's prevailing westerlies and trade wind belt
Dynamic highs are deeper, stronger, and longer-lived than thermal highs

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather associated with anticyclones
concept: In an anticyclone, the dominant process is subsidence — air sinks slowly and warms adiabatically. This suppresses cloud formation and inhibits precipitation, producing generally settled weather. Near the centre, winds are very light and variable. At the outer edges, winds are moderate and follow the anticyclonic circulation. The main weather hazards within an anticyclone arise from the boundary layer below the subsidence inversion: radiation fog forms readily on clear nights in valleys; shallow stratus or stratocumulus may persist below the inversion; haze builds up as pollutants accumulate in the trapped surface layer. In summer, anticyclonic conditions allow strong surface heating during the day, which can produce afternoon convective cloud — even thunderstorms — if the instability is sufficient despite the overall subsidence. In winter, the stable, clear conditions of anticyclones produce hard frosts and potentially very cold temperatures. In NZ, the classic anticyclonic weather pattern delivers several days of light winds, clear to high cloud, excellent visibility (in the absence of fog), and settled conditions — ideal for VFR operations.
exam_tip: Anticyclone weather: settled, clear, light winds — ideal for VFR. But watch for radiation fog in valleys at night and haze from trapped pollution. Afternoon convection possible if surface heating overcomes subsidence.
key_facts: Settled, clear to partly cloudy weather with light to moderate winds
Radiation fog common in valleys on clear nights — poor surface visibility
Haze and pollution accumulate below subsidence inversion
Shallow stratus/stratocumulus may persist below the inversion
Summer: surface heating may produce afternoon convection despite subsidence
Winter: hard frosts and very cold temperatures in settled anticyclones

syllabus_desc: Describe the subsidence inversion and its effects on aviation
concept: A subsidence inversion is a layer where temperature increases with altitude (a temperature inversion) caused by the warming of descending air in an anticyclone. Air sinking from the mid-troposphere warms at the dry adiabatic lapse rate — typically faster than the air below, which has been cooled by surface contact overnight. The result is a layer where the lower air is cool and moist and the sinking air above is warm and dry, creating an abrupt inversion. Below the inversion, conditions are often hazy, misty, or foggy with poor horizontal visibility and low cloud. Above the inversion, visibility is excellent, air is dry, and conditions are clear. The inversion is an effective lid that prevents cloud from growing through it — it caps cumulus development. For aircraft departing through low stratus/haze below the inversion, the climb-out is brief — break through into excellent VMC above. But for pilots trying to descend through the inversion, visibility can suddenly deteriorate from excellent to IFR conditions. In NZ, subsidence inversions are particularly persistent in anticyclonic conditions over the northeast of both islands.
exam_tip: Subsidence inversion = warm dry air aloft over cool moist surface air. Excellent VMC above; haze, fog, or low cloud below. Descending through it means going from clear to suddenly IFR conditions.
key_facts: Caused by descending air warming adiabatically in an anticyclone
Temperature increases with height through the inversion layer
Traps cool moist air below — haze, fog, low stratus common
Excellent VMC and dry air above the inversion
Caps cumulus development — prevents convective cloud from growing through
Descent through inversion can transition from VMC to IFR very rapidly

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of the ridge of high pressure
concept: A ridge is an elongated extension of high pressure extending outward from an anticyclone centre — it is to pressure what a mountain range is to topography. Isobars bow outward around a ridge, and the ridge axis is the line of highest pressure along its length. Weather along a ridge is similar to that in an anticyclone: settled, with subsiding air, reduced cloud, and light winds near the ridge axis. Ridges are significant for NZ aviation because they often mark the brief period of fine weather between a departing frontal system and the next approaching system — the classic 'between-fronts ridge'. This ridge may last only 12–24 hours before the next front arrives, so careful monitoring is required. The passage of a ridge is characterised by a pressure maximum, wind shift from northwesterly to southwesterly, and a brief clearing of cloud before cloud thickens again as the next trough approaches. Pilots planning cross-country flights in NZ often target the passage of a ridge for the best weather window.
exam_tip: A ridge is an extension of high pressure between two troughs. It brings brief settled weather — the 'fine break' between fronts. Pressure peaks at the ridge, then falls as the next trough arrives.
key_facts: Elongated extension of high pressure between two troughs
Settled, clearing weather — subsidence, reduced cloud, light winds
Ridge axis = highest pressure along the ridge line
Often marks brief fine break between frontal systems in NZ (12–24 hours)
Wind shift from NW to SW at ridge passage; pressure peaks then falls
Plan cross-country flights to use the ridge window for best conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation and characteristics of the col
concept: A col (also called a saddle) is the area of relatively neutral pressure between two highs and two lows arranged diagonally opposite each other. On a pressure chart, it appears as the point where the pressure gradient is nearly zero — the isobars form a saddle shape. In a col, winds are very light and variable because the competing pressure gradients from the surrounding systems nearly cancel each other out. The weather in a col is unpredictable and can change rapidly as surrounding systems shift position. Fog and mist are common in col regions, particularly at night, because the light winds allow radiative cooling. In summer, the weak pressure gradient in a col can allow surface heating to produce convective cloud and even thunderstorms that develop without being steered by any particular airflow. A col is essentially a zone of meteorological indecision — the forecaster's nightmare — and weather in a col can deteriorate quickly as one of the surrounding systems expands into the space.
exam_tip: A col is the neutral pressure area between two highs and two lows. Very light variable winds, fog possible, and convective storms can develop in summer due to instability without directional steering.
key_facts: Neutral pressure zone between two highs and two lows arranged diagonally
Isobars form a saddle shape on the pressure chart
Very light, variable winds — pressure gradients nearly cancel
Fog common in col due to light winds allowing radiative cooling
Convective storms can develop in summer without directional steering
Unpredictable — weather can change rapidly as surrounding systems shift

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation, structure and characteristics of a warm front
concept: A warm front marks the leading edge of an advancing warm air mass replacing cooler air at the surface. Because warm air is less dense, it rides up and over the retreating cold air along a gentle slope (typically 1:150 to 1:200) that extends hundreds of kilometres ahead of the surface front position. This gradual overrunning produces a sequence of cloud types that give advance warning of the approaching front: beginning with cirrus (600–900 km ahead), then cirrostratus (with halo effects), altostratus, nimbostratus, and finally stratus as the front passes. Warm fronts are associated with widespread, continuous precipitation (rain or snow) that can begin 200–300 km ahead of the surface front. Visibility ahead of a warm front deteriorates progressively — from good visibility in the cirrus zone, to poor in the nimbostratus, to very poor in the frontal fog zone. The warm sector (air behind the warm front and ahead of the cold front) is warm, moist, and often hazy.
